###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-01T00:55:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<415CAD74.30108@shaw.ca>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<xy56d.1426$wG3.11561@news1.mts.net>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <zy26d.32011$vl.19974@fe40.usenetserver.com> <xy56d.1426$wG3.11561@news1.mts.net>`

```
Denis Bacon wrote:

>No It's NOT homework.... just don't feel like spending half a day looking
>for the best method, nor did I feel like coding it myself.
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>
Dennis,

Your question raised zillions of responses many of which I guess you 
couildn't really care about .

Well you did say specifically N/E V 3.1.  No the only reference to TRIM 
is associated with ODBC and SQL. The only other reference you  will find 
is in the OO CharacterArray class - 'timblanks' - given "^^^^abcde^^^^^' 
- that's going to give you 'abcde' - normally associated with GUI 
entryfields.

You can 'sort of write a function' if you use OO - so here goes. This is 
a 'play' on what Bill Klein coded and overcomes the problem where you 
have two words with more than one space between them in the same input 
element :-

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Program-id. InputText.

       Class-Control.      MakeAstring is class "makstrng".

       Working-storage section.

       01 TableA.
          10 pic x(80) value "   Humpty Dumpty      ".
          10 pic x(80) value "   sat on the wall,   ".
          10 pic x(80) value "Humpty          Dumpty".
          10 pic x(80) value "had a     great fall. ".
          10 pic x(80) value spaces.

       01 InputLength                       pic x(4) comp-5.

       01 StringInfo.
          05 StringLength                   pic x(4) comp-5.
          05 StringText.
             10 occurs 1 to 500
             depending on StringLength      pic x.

       01 StringChoice                         pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
          88 NoSpaces                          value 0.
          88 WithSingleSpace                   value 1.

       Procedure Division.

       compute Inputlength = function length (TableA)

       *> Returned string includes NO spaces between words

       set NoSpaces to true
       invoke MakeAString "makeText"
               using StringChoice, InputLength, TableA
               returning StringInfo

       if StringLength <> zeroes
          display StringText
       End-if

       *> Returned string includes one space between each word

       set WithSingleSpace to true
       invoke MakeAString "makeText"
               using StringChoice InputLength, TableA
               returning StringInfo

       if StringLength <> zeroes
          display StringText
       End-if

       STOP RUN.
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       *>----------------- makstrng.cbl --------------------------------

       Class-id.       MakeAstring inherits from Base.

       Class-Control.  MakeAstring             is class "makstrng"

                       CharacterArray          is class "chararry"
                       .

       *> Note : A period (.) follows the 'final' Class-Control entry

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       FACTORY.
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "makeText".
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

       *> Using input data, the class :-

       *> (1) creates a CharacterArray then
       *> (2) uses "asArrayOfSubstrings" to ignore intervening white
       *>     spaces. White space in this method, is defined as being
       *>     any character with an ascii value that is less than
       *>     or equal to 32 (space).
       *> (3) Builds the string setting the counters for
       *>     lengths of strings.


       Local-storage section.

       *> Max Values that we can check against - or change

       78 ls-StringLength                  value 80.
       78 ls-MaxLength                     value 500.

       *> other :
       01 n                                pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-bytes                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-NumberOfElements              pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-TempText                      pic x(ls-StringLength).
       01 ls-TempCount                     pic x(4) comp-5.

       01 ls-SeparatedStrings              object reference.
       01 ls-String                        object reference.

       Linkage Section.
       01 In-Choice                     pic x(4) comp-5.
          88 NoSpaces                   value 0.
          88 WithSingleSpace            value 1.
       01 In-Length                     pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 In-Text.
          10 occurs 1 to ls-MaxLength
             depending on In-Length     pic x(4) comp-5.

       01 Lnk-Out.
          05 Out-Length                    pic x(4) comp-5.
          05 Out-Text.
             10 occurs 1 to ls-MaxLength
                depending on Out-Length    pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division using In-Choice, In-Length, In-Text
                          returning Lnk-Out.

       initialize lnk-Out

       if In-Length > ls-MaxLength
          display "In-Length exceeds MaxLength"
          EXIT METHOD
       end-if

       invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
                using     In-length, In-Text
                returning ls-String
       invoke ls-String "asArrayOfSubstrings"
              returning ls-SeparatedStrings
       invoke ls-SeparatedStrings "size" returning ls-NumberOfElements

       initialize Lnk-Out, ls-TempCount
       Perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ls-NumberOfElements

           invoke ls-SeparatedStrings "at"
                  using n returning ls-String
           invoke ls-String "sizeinBytes" returning ls-bytes

           if ls-bytes > ls-StringLength
              move zeroes to Out-Length
              display "String exceeds Maximum Bytes for ls-TempText"
              EXIT METHOD
           End-if

           invoke ls-string "getValuewithSize"
                  using ls-bytes returning ls-TempText(1:ls-bytes)

           Evaluate true
             when NoSpaces
                compute Out-Length = Out-Length + ls-bytes
                String  Out-Text (1:ls-TempCount)
                        ls-TempText  (1:ls-bytes)
                        into Out-Text
                End-string

             when WithSingleSpace
                compute Out-Length = Out-Length + ls-bytes + 1
                String  Out-Text (1:ls-TempCount)
                        ls-TempText  (1:ls-bytes)
                        " "
                        into Out-Text
                End-string

             when other
                move zeroes to Out-Length
                display "Invalid String Choice"
                EXIT METHOD

           End-Evaluate

           move Out-Length to ls-TempCount

       End-Perform

       End Method "makeText".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       END FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       *> OBJECT.
       *> END OBJECT.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       END CLASS MakeAstring.
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------

Make a project with the 'Procedural' as your trigger/first program and 
then add the class program.
Compile both and Run.

You don't need to understand OO to make this run and there should be 
sufficient here so that you can adapt as necessary

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Comments embedded, Robert...

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:b26ml05bunh4mb9q8n5v8tvuflhcntajf7@4ax.com...
> On 29 Sep 2004 12:45:07 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
> >Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
> >
<snip>>
> >> True. I've worked with many inept Cobol programmers
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>
I read this with some poignancy.

I remember a passionate young COBOL programmer arguing fiercely against
installation standards that constrained the use of the language. (The issue
was over PERFORM...VARYING... which was BANNED on that installation as being
"unnecessarily complex". (hey, you can do it yourself with a loop and test,
right? <G>). The same site REQUIRED all IFs to have an ELSE (NEXT SENTENCE
was considered appropriate if there was no specific action...) and NOT was
severely frowned upon (if you use ELSE in every construct, you don't need
NOT, right ? <G>). EXAMINE (later replaced by INSPECT), SEARCH, STRING and
UNSTRING were all considered "new fangled nonsene" and consequently banned.)

You might wonder how such standards could have been written, let alone
implemented.

They were done by a Chief Programmer who had spent all his life writing
Assembler, been dragged kicking and screaming into COBOL, and was determined
that if he would have to endure COBOL he was damned well going to see that
nobody had any fun with it...

The young COBOL programmer used EXACTLY the same words that you expressed
above, Robert:

"They should learn to program or be replaced with people who know how."

It made not a jot of difference, apart from getting my card marked as a
"troublemaker" (which, in those days, I was... <G>).

(I left as soon as I could get another job. The company no longer exists and
that doesn't surprise me in the least, although I derive no personal
satisfaction from seeing any company go under.)

Many years later I encountered a similar situation. This time I was able to
get the standards changed. I had become older and wiser and knew more about
resolving conflict and persuading people. (I guess you could call it
"manipulative", if you were being unkind <G>, but I was then and still am,
up front about my position.)


>
> >> Should programming languages be designed for the Lowest Common
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>
Well, I disagree with both of you here...(can't make this too long as I am
on 60% battery... <G>)

No, Programming Languages should not be designed for the Lowest Common
Denominator. We have to assume that anyone who is going into Programming as
a career has some degree of enthusiasm for it and some degree of innate
intelligence. (They are like skills pre-requisite for the job. Most aptitude
tests should detect them...).

But, that doesn't mean that languages should be designed to be cryptic
either. Even bright people need some slack occasionally. How many people do
you know who still "write" APL? Incredibly powerful language, but totally
incomprehesible to the uninitiated. Result: They have their annual
conferences in phone booths around the planet...

"It doesn't need a 'Computer Scientist' to write it, staff with an
understanding of the business should be able to write the programs."

Robert is right and that certainly WAS a sales pitch 30 - 40 years ago. But
I disagree with it as a sales pitch today.

Rhetoric aside, Richard, I don't believe that you would really try and
support this argument in today's climate. Can you honestly say you have seen
non-programmers writing company applications in COBOL? Or even "reading"
COBOL code in order to understand how a business process is implemented? It
wouldn't make sense. Whether or not they SHOULD be able to, the fact is they
aren't and they don't...

>
> >Your attempts to make Cobol programs look like other languages does
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agree with this almost 100%. Almost? Because there are times and occasions
when it is really quite OK to use pointers (data and procedure) in COBOL.
And sometimes it is necessary to manipulate a tree or node in COBOL. But not
as a general rule,  or preferred approach. Use another language, build a
.DLL or component to encapsulate the functionality in a language that best
implemnets it, then use THAT functionality from COBOL (with CALL or INVOKE).
COBOL is an ideal vehicle for components, and this is a usage that is being
missed by most of the marketplace.

> Because Cobol is easier to read, which makes maintenance faster and
> less error-prone.
>

Only if you are going to maintain it. If you use encapsulated functionality
(based on OO components) you won't be maintaining it.

> Because of superior string handling and editing.
>

Arguable.

> Because Cobol is a do-anything tool in the same way that C is. It is a
> reasonable choice for systems software as well as applications.
>

EVERY programming language is a "do-anything" tool. If your vision is
limited by being restricted ONLY to that language. To a man with a hammer,
everything is a nail.

> >The strengths of Cobol are that it is standardised and portable: ie
> >'Common', and through this has lasted 40 years, it will only last
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I've covered this in another post.

> Languages go out of fashion because a) they're no good or b) a
> similar-looking language offers better features. An example of b. is
> the replacement of C with C++. Even if we set aside OO, C++ is a
> better C.
>

Yawn...who cares? And there are many more reasons than the two above as to
why languages go out of fashion. It is much more complex than suggested in
this argument.

> I haven't seen any Cobol-like languages threatening to replace Cobol.
> What I have seen is evolution through the standards process. Cobol 85
> is 'a better Cobol' than Cobol 60, and the 02 version is significantly
> better than 85.
>

Totally a matter of opinion.

> >Your so called 'best practices' promote non-standard 'uncommon'
> >mechanisms that are done better in other languages.
>
Tragically, true.

> Everything in Best Practices follows the 85 standard except pointers,
> which follow a de facto standard that's exactly the same in IBM, Micro
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Robert, the road to Hell is paved with good intentions. I don't question for
one moment your motivation in this. I have to ask... does the prhase
"Flogging a dead horse" mean anything to you? Anything that involves
submissions to the alphabet soup that represents the "keepers of COBOL" at
this time, comes in that category. They will let it die while they argue
where the next meeting should be... The credibility of COBOL (or lack of it)
is reflected in the credibility of the people who took 17 years to prepare a
standard for it. It's too late now...

Fortunately, the vendors have a more pragmatic approach (it's funny how the
need to make a living focuses the mind...). The only reason we have COBOL
today is because vendors support it.  For the most part, they are in touch
with the marketplace, observe the industry and see trends and requirements
long before any standards committee could. (Actually, that's not true... a
standards committee COULD do the same but it would mean abandoning the ivory
tower and cutting through all the procedural crap, to actually get in touch
with the user base. Just imagine if there was an open forum on it where
anyone could contribute their ideas for public discussion over a defined
period, then the consensus was embodied in a standard...naw, we don't have
the technology to do that...and besides, it would mean relinquishing some
control...Leave that to the Java people with their new-fangled web enabled
technology; next thing Dashwood will be advocating putting the COBOL
standard out on Nintendo or Game Boy consoles...hohoho)

Sadly, even if this was available tomorrow, I wouldn't contribute to it. (5
years ago, I would've.) I'm resolved now to simply let COBOL die with
dignity.

Can we reasonably expect vendors to continue investing in COBOL? Only as
long as the market justifies that investment. Emotion doesn't come into it.
It is a pure business decision.

And the outlook is not comforting.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-10-02T11:59:26+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjkr12$qfh$1@domitilla.aioe.org>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <3r8il0lavru86r2dlk53p2jors0bnddc7f@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409281125.7fe38572@posting.google.com> <hqqjl0dmq478ts4aemq26sj4dodpum2u51@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282230.2680e9b8@posting.google.com> <lf4ll05f6b735jmi0ndckfaf7nnv2fcrn6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291145.154b65ab@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0409291145.154b65ab@posting.google.com...
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
<snip>
>
> The strengths of Cobol are that it is standardised and portable: ie
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>
You hit the nail on the head, Richard. It isn't about whether COBOL is right
or wrong, good or bad. It is about the PERCEPTION of COBOL by the decision
makers and bean counters.

I predicted in 1995 that by 2015 COBOL would be extinct as a viable
commercial programming language. I see you are admitting the possibility of
this point of view. (Most people here just laughed it to scorn...)

The "standardisation" (don't start me on that <G>) and "portability" you
mention above as being major attractions of COBOL are no longer as relevant
as they once were, and they are further diminished by the "downside" of
verbosity. In the days when source code was important, verbosity was a "Good
Thing" because it could be translated as "self documenting".  As the
importance of source code (at least for commercial Corporations who are not
in the IT business) diminishes, and systems are put together from components
and packages, this argument carries less and less weight.  If you are not
going to maintain code, why would you care about the state of the source?

Although COBOL was originally touted as an "English like" language that
would empower business users (like Accountants) to write commercial
applications, in practice, I have never seen a senior manager or accountant
who programmed his own applications for the company. In COBOL or anything
else...

I love COBOL and will continue using it for the rest of my days. I
understand its strengths and weaknesses and I find it ideal for developing
commercial components. But I am definitely a minority. And I no longer use
it exclusively. I find myself writing more Java and JavaScript than I
would've thought possible a few years back. I know you use Perl and Python
and believe in using the right tools for the job. Others here have
experimented with PHP.

I agree with your position when you take Robert to task for trying to make
COBOL C or something else.

But Robert is passionate about his COBOL and for that alone, he deserves to
be here.

For many others, there is still the perception that "Everything I want to
do, I can do in COBOL...". This narrow-minded conservatism simply reflects
the resistance to change, lack of vision, and laziness of the average COBOL
programmer.

And THAT is what will kill the language as a commercial proposition.

Pete.
```

On 30 Sep 2004 16:44:31 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>the run-time.

Good point. Realia has no runtime package nor runtime fees. It
produces self-contained executables and dlls.  Problem is, it has no
OO either. :)

>> >A second issue is that the strength of Cobol comes from the writing of
>> >the source code, not from the use of libraries.  Selling compiled code
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>than one written in C#, Java, or assembler ?

I said don't tell them it's Cobol. Let that come as a surprise.

>What advantage to the component buyer is its Cobol origin ? What
>special things will it do that are not in some other Component written
>in another language ?

That's the same question all development shops face -- why use Cobol
rather than another language. So I'll ask: why do you use Cobol?

>What, to the compiled component, does its Cobolness add ?  (other than
>several megabytes of runtime where required).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>customers ?

Drat, I'm busted. For all those years I had them fooled into thinking
I was a real programmer. Now a Totoesque kiwi pulls the curtain aside
to reveal it was all a fraud .. or self-delusion.

That must be why Cobol jobs are drying up -- because they heard I was
looking. Billions of lines of inferior code are just background
scenery. The real reason for Cobol's decline is me. 

Excuse me while I disembowel myself in shame.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-01T14:21:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410011321.4903c941@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <cjc9lp$1eq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <h3jjl0lj0nklv7e9rj0vi6co8cf9shhb1a@4ax.com> <cjf185$8aq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cjf3nm$pn6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cjf9g5$dnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <uhdml09pa5j8ep3hfh2o278jt0bmvinu5s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291819.70bd9a13@posting.google.com> <g2hol0labnft1vnkvbb16h69cch26mf0gc@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409301544.b91ac81@posting.google.com> <f0jpl0lnfccrab5tkvgvm339vfd38ubkfi@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >Or putting it another way: why would someone buy a component, similar
> >to the ones available from that site, that are written in Cobol rather
> >than one written in C#, Java, or assembler ?
> 
> I said don't tell them it's Cobol. Let that come as a surprise.

Yes, but why would they buy it in the first place ?
 
> >What advantage to the component buyer is its Cobol origin ? What
> >special things will it do that are not in some other Component written
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> rather than another language. So I'll ask: why do you use Cobol?

I write in Cobol because, with 35 years experience it is easy to write
robust applications that build on my extensive code base.

I write in Python because it is easy to write robust scalable OO
programs in just a few lines that will do specialised tasks building
on the extensive base of libraries.

I write in Perl because I have to for some clients.

I write in C because it is a nice simple language that gets the job
done in a workmanlike manner.

I write in Java because that may be where at least some of my work is
in the future, and I need to have a wide experience to be able to make
_informed_ comments.  Actually I am more likely to use Jython for real
work inside Java.

I am sure that existing component developers also have similar reasons
for using the languages that they use, such as a hypothetical:

   "I write in C++ because I have 15 years of experience with it and
an
    extensive code base that I can reuse."

C++ and Java programmers are as good at those as I am in Cobol.  They
have solved the problems, are over 'the hump' and produce code as fast
and reliably as any other experienced programmers in other languages.

But that is not the point which is:

> >What, to the compiled component, does its Cobolness add ? 

You failed to answer this.

> >I know what the advantage to _you_, Robert, as the programmer is: you
> >don't know any other language, but what would be the advantage to the
> >customers ?
 
> Drat, I'm busted. For all those years I had them fooled into thinking
> I was a real programmer. Now a Totoesque kiwi pulls the curtain aside
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> scenery. The real reason for Cobol's decline is me. 

Dear oh me, the martydom defense.   

What I was saying is that you want to [have others] write components
in Cobol because you know Cobol and think it is the best language
ever, for everything, and want to show the world that you are a
superprogrammer and not an old gaffer that doesn't grok it.

You, presumably, have tried other languages and have expressed that
you don't like them. All new laguages are hard to learn, and don't
have the solutions that you can call on because, while learning, there
is no past experience or code base to reuse.

C++ developers find it just as hard to learn Cobol as you find it hard
to learn Java.  So why would developers write components in Cobol ?

"The customer will get a surprise" is not a reason.

A valid reason may be "Because it is the only language the developer
knows".
Or "I can do it in 3 lines of code (such as a SEARCH or SORT)".
Or "The components will have x extra facilities because it is in
Cobol".
Or .. or ..

Or what Robert ?

I am trying to get you to come up with an _actual_ idea.
```

```
On 1 Oct 2004 14:21:13 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yes, but why would they buy it in the first place ?

Because of its merits -- good design, features, etc.
 
>> >What advantage to the component buyer is its Cobol origin ? What
>> >special things will it do that are not in some other Component written
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>on the extensive base of libraries.

Those are good reasons. Mine are similar.

>I write in Perl because I have to for some clients.

Pragmatic.

>I write in C because it is a nice simple language that gets the job
>done in a workmanlike manner.

Anything you can do in C can be done better in Cobol.

>I write in Java because that may be where at least some of my work is
>in the future, and I need to have a wide experience to be able to make
>_informed_ comments.  Actually I am more likely to use Jython for real
>work inside Java.

I concur.  The demand for Java skills is high. 

>I am sure that existing component developers also have similar reasons
>for using the languages that they use, such as a hypothetical:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>and reliably as any other experienced programmers in other languages.

True. I've worked with fast-talking, fast-clicking RAD types. Flash
aside, they tend to be very good programmers.

>But that is not the point which is:
>
>> >What, to the compiled component, does its Cobolness add ? 
>
>You failed to answer this.

The virtuosity you alluded to above. I'm very good at Cobol and enjoy
using it to create systems software, not just applications.

You could ask the same of C++ tool developers and receive an equally
subjective answer. There's no special C++ness in tools developed with
that language.

>What I was saying is that you want to [have others] write components
>in Cobol because you know Cobol and think it is the best language
>ever, for everything, and want to show the world that you are a
>superprogrammer and not an old gaffer that doesn't grok it.

This _is_ a Cobol newsgroup. Its charter is not limited to 1970s
mainframe style. I want others to think of Cobol as a real programming
language that doesn't feel inferior to C++ and Java. We won't
accomplish that by writing PERFORM 2300-FOO THRU 2300-FOO-EXIT. 
Crap like that only confirms Cobol critics' accusations.

>You, presumably, have tried other languages and have expressed that
>you don't like them. All new laguages are hard to learn, and don't
>have the solutions that you can call on because, while learning, there
>is no past experience or code base to reuse.

True ..

>C++ developers find it just as hard to learn Cobol as you find it hard
>to learn Java.  So why would developers write components in Cobol ?

In the end, it's about art. Real programmers will choose the medium
that lets them be most expressive .. that fulfills their sense of
beauty. I've worked on C code that was so beautiful I wanted to shake
the hand of the author (in India).  I couldn't possibly write that
well in C, but I could in Cobol. 

I applaud the idea that someone in India found his voice, rather than
picking rice while white guys controlled the computer industry. I see
Indian programmers as culture-neutral, not carrying the baggage we do.
How do we convince them to take Cobol seriously?

>"The customer will get a surprise" is not a reason.

Sure, it would promote the respectability of Cobol.

>A valid reason may be "Because it is the only language the developer
>knows".
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am trying to get you to come up with an _actual_ idea.

Application specific ideas are outside the realm of Cobol and this
forum. Even duplicating the functionality of common tools such as MFC
would be a good proof-of-concept.
```

Well, Gee, Richard, I've been writing and selling COBOL "functions" for the
last 4 years. While I haven't made enough from doing so to retire yet, I've
certainly netted enough to encourage me to keep at this "silly idea".

I'm writng this on the beach at Omanu with a cold beer. I spend quite a bit
of time here, even on "workdays". How's things in your neck of the woods
(Auckland CBD?)<G>?

You are right about the Runtime, of course, but with Fujitsu, it's free. It
is more of a pyschological objection and I usually explain that they already
have a VB runtime on their system (thoughtfully installed by that nice
MicroSoft Corporation), so the COBOL runtime is just another one of those
invisible "servants". Version 6.0 is around 14 MBs which means CDs for
distribution, but most people are used to that.

Of course, if I can generate enough cash to afford the Dot NET compiler, the
whole thing is academic. (then they only need to download a 40MB Dot NET
environment, but it has a MicroSoft label on it so I can deflect any
criticism to that nice Mr. Gates... <G>)

Pete.

Pete.

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote

> Well, Gee, Richard, I've been writing and selling COBOL "functions" for the
> last 4 years. While I haven't made enough from doing so to retire yet, I've
> certainly netted enough to encourage me to keep at this "silly idea".

It wasn't the writing of components that was 'silly', it was that the
'astute marketer' was supposed to keep the language secret and then
have a 'revelation' to surprise the enthralled customers.

 
> I'm writng this on the beach at Omanu with a cold beer. I spend quite a bit
> of time here, even on "workdays". How's things in your neck of the woods
> (Auckland CBD?)<G>?

Not the CBD, no, ECB (East Coast Bays). It's a 5 minute walk to the
beach, or 2 minutes by bike (but 4 minutes back up the hill).
 
> You are right about the Runtime, of course, but with Fujitsu, it's free. 

It is free for developers to distribute, they get a licence when they
buy the product (but not for free compilers).  However, if you sell a
compiled component to a non-Cobol developer then he has no licence to
re-distribute the run-time with his product that includes your
component.
```

news:217e491a.0410051025.72407c69@posting.google.com...

<snip>

bit
> > of time here, even on "workdays". How's things in your neck of the woods
> > (Auckland CBD?)<G>?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>
Excellent! <G> I had forgotten you mentioned the biking a long time ago. And
I agree it is a beautiful place to live and work.
>
> > You are right about the Runtime, of course, but with Fujitsu, it's free.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Now, that is a point I have never considered. Are you absolutely sure about
that? I guess I could go and read all the fine print in my Fujitsu License
but if you have prior experience and know this to be so, I'll take your word
for it. I have to say, if it is true I am in violation of it, and I can't
really do much about it. It seems reasonable to me that if I develop a
component I should be able to distribute it to ANYBODY (whether they use
COBOL or not).  If they then distribute their application, and it uses my
component, that is what I sold it to them for. I wouldn't (and don't) expect
them to pay me a royalty on every copy of their application that includes my
component. They bought it, and that includes the right to use it.

What if the component DIDN'T need the runtime? (Say it was compiled to
native code...) The fact that it DOES need the Runtime is something I have
no choice over. I don't see how Fujitsu could morally enforce this.

If this is indeed the case, then all I can really do is stop using COBOL for
component development. (As this is mainly what I DO use it for, I might as
well stop using COBOL altogether. I can certainly build my components in
other languages, and not have any questions over whether they are
distributable or not.) Perhaps the answer is to go to dotNET with its CLR...

Anybody have any thoughts on this particular dilemma?

Pete.
```

           CONTINUE

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-01T09:16:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjjvsi$ii9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com> <cjema0$sl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com...

> Do you remember these timings, from the "function reverse" versus the
> indexed look?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (That's a link to Google Groups - just shortened it so it wouldn't
wrap...)

I went back and ran some tests myself, not quite parallel to what Judson did
in this reference, but close.

Givan an 80-character F1 and TEMPF1, the following two paragraphs intended
to obtain the number of trailing spaces in F1provided almost identical
performance on a Unisys ClearPath NX6830:

    TEST-1.
        MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE (F1) TO F2.
        INSPECT F2 TALLYING T2 FOR LEADING SPACES.

    TEST-2.
         PERFORM
                    VARYING T2
                    FROM FUNCTION LENGTH (F1) BY -1
                    UNTIL F1 (T2:1) IS EQUAL TO SPACE
                    OR T2 IS LESS THAN 1
            CONTINUE
            END-PERFORM.

In TEST-1, although the "function reverse" costs, the INSPECT is able to
take advantage of machine operators that aren't accessible in the
character-at-a-time comparison in TEST-2 which is I think the reason the
difference is a "wash".
.
My personal favorite,

    TEST-3.
        INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE (T1) TALLYING T2 FOR LEADING SPACES.

came in a distant third place, my guess because the "function" source for
INSPECT threw a monkeywrench into the compiler's optimizations for the
simple TALLYING case.  I haven't spent a lot of time analyzing the generated
code, though.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:cjjvsi$ii9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...

Sorry, didn't cut-and-paste the actual program, edited it in the message.  .

For
>        MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE (F1) TO F2.
>        INSPECT F2 TALLYING T2 FOR LEADING SPACES.

please read
>         MOVE FUNCTION REVERSE (F1) TO TEMPF1.
>         INSPECT TEMPF1 TALLYING T2 FOR LEADING SPACES.

This matches the introductory paragraph better.

Note that my TEST-1 was *slightly* superior overall in performance to the
PERFORM version in TEST-2.  The difference was on the order of 0.01%.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

>     TEST-2.
>          PERFORM
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>             END-PERFORM.

Just a technical note, you should reverse the tests around the OR, ie

>                     VARYING T2
>                     FROM FUNCTION LENGTH (F1) BY -1
                      UNTIL T2 IS LESS THAN 1
                         OR F1 (T2:1) IS EQUAL TO SPACE

Otherwise there is little point in having the boundary test.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-04T10:44:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10m2ogj9p5g6b29@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com> <cjema0$sl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cjjvsi$ii9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0410011726.63fe2ae1@posting.google.com> <cjrjq3$f8p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cjrjq3$f8p$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  1-Oct-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are CoBOL compilers required to make these tests in the order given?

Short answer, Yes.

Long answer, follows.

FDIS, page 137, 8.8.4.3 Order of evaluation of conditions
"Parentheses, both explicit and implicit, denote a level of
inclusiveness within a complex condition. Two or more
conditions connected by only the logical operator 'AND'
or only the logical operator 'OR' at the same level of
inclusiveness establish a hierarchical level within a complex
condition. An entire complex condition can be considered
to be a nested structure of hierarchical levels with the entire
complex condition itself being the most inclusive hierarchical
level. Within this context, the evaluation of the conditions
within an entire complex condition begins at the left of the
entire complex condition and proceeds according to the
following rules recursively applied where necessary:

1) The constituent connected conditions within a hierarchical
    level are evaluated in order from left to right, and evaluation
    of that hierarchical level terminates as soon as a truth value
    for it is determined regardless of whether all the constituent
    connected conditions within that hierarchical level have been
    evaluated.

2) Values are established for arithmetic expressions and
    functions if and when the conditions containing them are
    evaluated. Similarly, negated conditions are evaluated if and
    when it is necessary to evaluate the complex condition that
    they represent. (See 8.8.1, Arithmetic expressions.)"
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<cjrom1$9uv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

On  4-Oct-2004, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> > Are CoBOL compilers required to make these tests in the order given?
>
> Short answer, Yes.

It's nice to know even though I haven't been writing with portability in mind. 
(I knew it worked with *my* compilers).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-04T11:16:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410041016.5c42d071@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com> <cjema0$sl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cjjvsi$ii9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0410011726.63fe2ae1@posting.google.com> <cjrjq3$f8p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> > Just a technical note, you should reverse the tests around the OR, ie
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are CoBOL compilers required to make these tests in the order given?

Yes, and it 'short cicuits'.  Once T2 is < 1 it won't be used as a
subscript (or in this case reference notation) and thus won't
(potentially) cause a boundary violation - it doesn't need to make
that test.

Other languages also 'short circuit', for example C, and this can lead
to a potential problem with it where user defined functions are used
in the tests.  For example, in C:

      if ( ( value > 0 ) && ( makePayment(value) != 0 ) ) { .. };

makePayment() is a function that will only be executed if value is >
zero because the evaluate of the test stops as soon as the truth can
be determined. Any side effects from the function will not happen. 
This case is simple so it is easy to see what is happening, but it may
be wrongly assumed by the programmer that the function should have
been executed.

When user defined functions are available they will need to consider
this, or ensure they do not have side effects.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-04T08:33:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjrqgr$2r9e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com> <cjema0$sl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cjjvsi$ii9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0410011726.63fe2ae1@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

<< ... Just a technical note, you should reverse the tests around the OR, ie
... Otherwise there is little point in having the boundary test. >>

Actually, that's not the problem; there's a bug in the program elsewhere!

The first test in the PERFORM should have been NOT EQUAL TO SPACE.

The idea was that we check the boundary condition only to ensure we don't
run off the left end of the PERFORM, and I believe it is correctly located.
.

I wondered at the near-identity of my results.

Now that the PERFORM statement has been corrected, I find that both the
PERFORM case and the MOVE-and-INSPECT case require approximately 2.3 times
the processor resources as the single-statement INSPECT.

I stand by my preferemce for INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE ... .

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-04T10:45:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjs278$30h3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com> <cjema0$sl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cjjvsi$ii9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0410011726.63fe2ae1@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0410011726.63fe2ae1@posting.google.com...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Otherwise there is little point in having the boundary test.

As I indicated before, there was a bug in the logic:  this should have been
        ... F1 (T2:1) IS NOT EQUAL TO SPACE
in each case.

I maintain that the test is in the right place -- we want to check for
non-blank and exit if true -- but another way of expressing this is
        PERFORM VARYING T2 FROM FUNCTION LENGTH (F1) BY -1
                UNTIL T2 IS LESS THAN 1
            IF T1 (T2:1) IS NOT EQUAL TO SPACE
                    EXIT PERFORM
                END-IF
            END-PERFORM

but this "separate test" case doesn't perform (pardon the pun) quite as well
(about 5% worse, as I see it) as the case in which the test is part of the
perform termination logic and the action performed is a simple continue.  I
haven't explored the reasons.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-04T19:26:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410041826.27966feb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com> <cjema0$sl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cjjvsi$ii9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0410011726.63fe2ae1@posting.google.com> <cjs278$30h3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

> > Just a technical note, you should reverse the tests around the OR, ie
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> in each case.

I din't notice that, but I may have if it had been F1(T2:1) = SPACE  
;-)
 
> I maintain that the test is in the right place -- we want to check for
> non-blank and exit if true -- but another way of expressing this is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>             END-PERFORM

This doen't have the boundary problem.  It will never try to extract
character T1(0:1) because it will test T2 and exit before getting to
the IF.

However, with your original, if T1 had been all spaces (or actually
all not spaces) it would have tested T1(0:1) before testing T2 < 1. 
If bounds checking was on it would have failed.

Because Cobol does conditional checks in sequence and does 'short
circuit' it is possible to avoid the bounds check exception and not
abend by testing the limit first, as my revision did.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-05T08:35:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjuf01$1inr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com> <cjema0$sl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cjjvsi$ii9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0410011726.63fe2ae1@posting.google.com> <cjs278$30h3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0410041826.27966feb@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0410041826.27966feb@posting.google.com...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > As I indicated before, there was a bug in the logic:  this should have
been
> >         ... F1 (T2:1) IS NOT EQUAL TO SPACE
> > in each case.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> all not spaces) it would have tested T1(0:1) before testing T2 < 1.

My *amended* original would only (and would indeed) fail if T1 had all
spaces, but my defense on that is that as a matter of style I wouldn't be
trying to find out where the last *nonblank* character in T1 was located
without first having verified that there was a nonblank character to check
for!   There are implementations in which the comparison with spaces is
quick indeed, and the longer the data item, the greater the disparity
between that check and the backward scan.

> If bounds checking was on it would have failed.

Even if bounds checking was *off* it would fail if T1 happened to be at the
beginning of an allocated memory space.   Working-storage may be
"monolithic" on some systems, but that is completely orthogonal to whether
it can be presumed to be monolithic everywhere.  That's come up in this
forum in the recent past.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

> > > > Just a technical note, you should reverse the tests around the OR, ie
> > > >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > Otherwise there is little point in having the boundary test.

> My *amended* original would only (and would indeed) fail if T1 had all
> spaces, but my defense on that is that as a matter of style I wouldn't be
> trying to find out where the last *nonblank* character in T1 was located
> without first having verified that there was a nonblank character to check
> for!   

Then why do you have the 'OR T2 IS LESS THAN 1' if you have
pre-determined that it never will be ?

(hence my 'little point in having the boundary test').

Short circuiting the conditions was done for a purpose, but it
requires correct ordering of the tests.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-10-01T15:15:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjjsam012f4@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <10lkahs4mc4qp82@corp.supernews.com> <0tkll0pvubdb1oplcgr4j1lkn8fg89269c@4ax.com>`

```

In article <0tkll0pvubdb1oplcgr4j1lkn8fg89269c@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> On Tue, 28 Sep 2004 23:20:07 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> libraries.

There are no class libraries in C.  There are no classes in C.

> // I compiled and tested this string class on Linux (Redhat 7.1) and 
> // MS Windows Borland C++ version 5.2 (win32). This should also work
> // using MS Visual C++ compiler

The "//" comment marker was only added to C in the 1999 standard,
which is supported by almost no implementations.  (C implementations
which do not comply with the 1999 standard but support // comments
are nonconforming, and the language they implement, while it might
almost be C, is not quite C.)

The Borland C++ and MS Visual C++ compilers implement a language
named "C++", which is not C.  The ideology of C++ boosters like
Bjarne Stroustrop notwithstanding, C and C++ are not the same
language, and C++ is not a "superset of C" or a "better C".

> String trim();
> String rtrim();

There is no "String" type in C.  If there's one in C++ (I don't have
my copy of the C++ standard handy), it's a relatively recent addition.
C++ does have a "string" type, but it is far from the same thing as
C strings.  For one thing, it's a class; for another, it supports all
manner of operations that aren't supported by C strings.

In any case, the existence of a "String" type in C++, or in commercial
C++ libraries, and a trim() function that operates on it, says nothing
about what you can do with strings in C.
```

wrote:

>

>There are no class libraries in C.  There are no classes in C.

Right. We're talking C++.

>> // I compiled and tested this string class on Linux (Redhat 7.1) and 
>> // MS Windows Borland C++ version 5.2 (win32). This should also work
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>almost be C, is not quite C.)

Right again. // is C++.

>The Borland C++ and MS Visual C++ compilers implement a language
>named "C++", which is not C.  The ideology of C++ boosters like
>Bjarne Stroustrop notwithstanding, C and C++ are not the same
>language, and C++ is not a "superset of C" or a "better C".

All I know about C++ is what I read in Stroustrop. :)

>There is no "String" type in C. 

Actually, there is -- string.h -- but it doesn't have trim().

> If there's one in C++ (I don't have
>my copy of the C++ standard handy), it's a relatively recent addition.

It's been in the ANSI standard for at least five years.

>C++ does have a "string" type, but it is far from the same thing as
>C strings.  For one thing, it's a class; for another, it supports all
>manner of operations that aren't supported by C strings.

Right .. like maybe trim. C++ has class cstring for the old type.
It's usually unnecessary because conversion from char[] to string is
automatic.

>In any case, the existence of a "String" type in C++, or in commercial
>C++ libraries, and a trim() function that operates on it, says nothing
>about what you can do with strings in C.

They run together in my thinking because I use the same compiler for
both. Sorry about the confusion.

- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <10lkahs4mc4qp82@corp.supernews.com> <0tkll0pvubdb1oplcgr4j1lkn8fg89269c@4ax.com> <cjjsam012f4@news1.newsguy.com> <oe4rl019tq4n5ultc3c4bjtgga64dki724@4ax.com>`

```

In article <oe4rl019tq4n5ultc3c4bjtgga64dki724@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> On 1 Oct 2004 15:15:34 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Right. We're talking C++.

C++ is not C.  You wrote "C" originally, and when Rick used "C"
above you didn't indicate that you were talking about C++, not C.

> >There is no "String" type in C. 
> 
> Actually, there is -- string.h -- but it doesn't have trim().

There is a header named string.h in C.  It declares exactly one
type, named "size_t".  C90 declares no type names, or other reserved
identifiers, that begin with a capital letter followed by only
lowercase letters.  Nor are such names reserved for future use by
the language, which is why C99 had to give its new boolean and
complex types the names _Bool, _Complex, and _Imaginary.  Nor are
they reserved for the implementation.

C has a string type.  Its name is "char *".  It is not necessary to
include string.h in order to use it (though it is necessary to
include string.h in a hosted implementation if you wish to use the
standard string-handling functions).

You're correct that there is no standard "trim" function.

> > If there's one in C++ (I don't have
> >my copy of the C++ standard handy), it's a relatively recent addition.
> 
> It's been in the ANSI standard for at least five years.

The ISO C++ standard includes a class named "basic_string".  I hope
you can see that this is not the same as "String".

If you believe there's a type named "String" in C++, feel free to
post a reference to the section of the ISO C++ standard (the "ANSI
standard" for C++ is just the ISO one, rebadged by ANSI) describing
it.
```

In article <iqram0dvhthnlkgite18q484g8bhp5v74c@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> On 6 Oct 2004 15:15:30 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> The term "class library" indicated C++.

It indicated that you silently changed your argument, from what was
available in C to what was available in C++, and hoped no one would
notice.

If I were to write:

   "COBOL has an operator that distributes an arithmetic operation
    across a vector"

in one post, then in another claimed:

   "oh, I was talking about APL, not COBOL, as indicated by the
    term 'vector'"

I suspect my interlocutors would find that a dubious line of
argument.  Wouldn't you?

> >> >There is no "String" type in C. 
> >> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Richard explained the capitalized String library is third-party.

It's still not C, and it still has nothing to do with string.h.

Look, this is really quite simple.  You made a claim about C, which
was wrong.  You tried to wriggle out of that by pretending you were
talking about C++ all along.  In the meantime you made other errors
about C++ and C.

The existence of third-party implementations of string types, free
or commercial, of whatever name, and with whatever operations, does
not change that fact.  Neither language possesses what you initially
claimed C has, and later claimed C++ has, as a feature of the
standard language.
```

In article <8e6nm097b52kmgmo4ph2uc6ujo5s13gbkq@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> On 11 Oct 2004 22:54:07 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> I did not silently change my argument, I clarified it.

It's an odd clarification that substantially alters the terms of
your argument.

> Microsoft Visual C++ works for both C and C++.

Microsoft does not define C or C++.

> If the languages were so
> completely different, one compiler wouldn't work for both.

This argument is entirely specious.  If dogs are so different from
fish, how is it that both can swim in the ocean?

GCC supports C and Fortran (among many other things).  I suppose
that means that C and Fortran aren't "completely different".

Regardless, C is not C++.  Switching from one to the other to suit
your argument is an inconsistency and a weakness, and gives you no
ground from which to defend your argument against my accurate charges
that you claimed something of C which was not true.

> >The existence of third-party implementations of string types, free
> >or commercial, of whatever name, and with whatever operations, does
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> string class. It includes a trim function called find_last_not_in.

Moot.  That wasn't what you claimed C had (a "trim" function operating
on a "String" data type), nor is it what you later claimed C++ had.

You were wrong.  You can't demonstrate otherwise by prentending you
meant something different all along.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-01T18:33:28-07:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-01T04:10:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j49pl0l9skb77h9s53harjk3kac7hlunin@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com> <fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com>`

```
On 30 Sep 2004 16:10:24 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>routine is in an external library and does not have a mangled name.

Had I had put curly braces around the function prototype, it would
function as you say. Without braces, it is the header of a wrapper --
code bridging the gap between Cobol and C++ like this:
 Cobol -> wrapper -> C++. 

The header tells the C++ compiler not to mangle the wrapper's entry
point name. Inside the wrapper, it will do a normal C++ call to the
target, with a mangled name.

To avoid just this confusion, I should have used a different name for
the wrapper, such as cpptrim. 

Cobol:
    call 'cpptrim' using string

wrapper:
     #include <string>
     extern "C" void cpptrim(char*);
     void cpptrim(char* s)  // this name not mangled
     {
     string str = s;
    str.trim();                   // this name is mangled
    s = str.c_str();
    return;
    }
   
>What you were requiring was the complete opposite: accessing a mangled
>method name in a C++ class from a C or C compatible call (based on
>your example).

Right. The wrapper converts Cobol's C compatible call into a C++ call.

>You cannot put the 'extern "C"' in the compile of the class and get it
>to not mangle the method name - all method names are mangled, and in
>any case that is not what the extern "C" is saying to do.

Right again. It's saying the wrapper's name should not be mangled.

>What would be required is, in the _Cobol_ program a directive of the
>form:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>which would mangle the name trim() for you.  But there is isn't one.

Alternatively, the Cobol program should construct a C++ string, then
INVOKE trim with it. The names still wouldn't match because Cobol
doesn't mangle names (doesn't support overloading). You'd still need a
wrapper containing one line: this.trim();

Most compilers default to or let you specify C calling conventions. Is
it asking too much that INVOKE use C++ conventions?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-10-01T15:49:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjju9f014e2@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com> <fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com> <j49pl0l9skb77h9s53harjk3kac7hlunin@4ax.com>`

```

In article <j49pl0l9skb77h9s53harjk3kac7hlunin@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> 
> Had I had put curly braces around the function prototype, it would
> function as you say.

Wrong.  Applying 'extern "C"' to a block is the same as applying it
to a single identifier, except that it applies to all external
identifiers declared in the block.

> Without braces, it is the header of a wrapper --

No, it's a prototype.  It's not a wrapper or a header.  (A header is
a kind of file that's included in a translation unit via the
#include preprocessing directive.)

> The header tells the C++ compiler not to mangle the wrapper's entry
> point name. Inside the wrapper, it will do a normal C++ call to the
> target, with a mangled name.

Not unless you write that wrapper.  It doesn't get generated for you.
All you've done with

   extern "C" char *trim(char *);

is declared a function with external linkage, and provided a prototype
for it.  You haven't defined it.
```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> To avoid just this confusion, I should have used a different name for
> the wrapper, such as cpptrim. 

Well, well, you have been doing some learning, haven't you. Now you
have to backpedal on your previous claims (name mangling is only done
when overloaded, all that is needed is a one line wrapper) and find
that you have to write a C function to do the job.

Of course you pretend that this program is the 'wrapper' you referred
to, when you obviously thought that you could unmangle the method with
just one line.
 
> Cobol:
>     call 'cpptrim' using string
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>     }

(BTW C/C++ is case sensitive, the class is String not string.)

So, you have to write a C function now, it seems you admit that you
were quite wrong in your claims:

RW>> Calling C functions from Cobol is trivial. Just do it. You don't
need
RW>> anything special.
RW>> ...
RW>>I misspoke when I said CALL. I should have said INVOKE, because
RW>>rtrim() is in the C++ String class library. Linkage conventions in
the
RW>>OO world are compatible across languages that compile to machine
code.

Now the 'nothing special' has escalated to writing code in a
completely different language, and using a class library that emulates
a Java class that is from:

// Author : Al Dev  Email: alavoor@yahoo.com
// Use string class or String class

Also, of course, you need to back-pedal on the INVOKE because you
cannot use this to CALL your C routine that you have finally
discovered that you had to write.


> Alternatively, the Cobol program should construct a C++ string, then
> INVOKE trim with it. The names still wouldn't match because Cobol
> doesn't mangle names (doesn't support overloading). You'd still need a
> wrapper containing one line: this.trim();

"Should" ?  Why "should" it ?  Cobol is not a C++ add-on (though it
might be with gcc front end).
 
Given that the class that you chose is actually a C++ implementation
of the Java class String (which you probably also didn't notice when
you googled) why not suggest that Cobol should use Java calsses - no
wait ...


> Most compilers default to or let you specify C calling conventions. Is
> it asking too much that INVOKE use C++ conventions?

Yes.  Name mangling is done differently by different compilers,
especially Microsoft's.

Your glib and rather naieve assurance:

RW>> Linkage conventions in the OO world are compatible across
languages

Is not even true of _one_ language.


You have already claimed that you would lie to your users to hide the
real cause if they asked about an issue, I can quite believe that you
also use this bullshit and blind technique with them that you use
here.  Putting up semi-valid answers in the hope they know less about
it than you do while you scrape for the next factoid and hope they
forgot the previous wrong answer.

Yes, the String class is from Al Dev.  The ANSI C++ string class (note

that you found was written by Al Dev as an implementation in C++ of
the Java class String.

"" 5. How Can I trust Al Dev's String Class ?? ""


> >"Should" ?  Why "should" it ?  Cobol is not a C++ add-on (though it

things, but that is no reason why it _should_ do those.

> >of the Java class String (which you probably also didn't notice when
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

No. 'string' is but that is something quite different.

> Isn't the mangler in a class that could be accessed by a Cobol
> compiler? I'll bet it is.

Why would the mangler be a 'class' ? It is something built into the
compiler and is not done at run-time.

> FYI: factlet is a small fact; factoid is something resembling a fact.

'factoids'. They resemble facts, but aren't.

- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com> <fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com>`

```

In article <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com>, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) writes:
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> routine is in an external library and does not have a mangled name.

Sorry, Richard, but while Robert's solution isn't quite right, he's
correct that 'extern "C"' can be applied to a definition in C++ to
create a C++ function with an unmangled name.  I use that all the time
to create C bindings for C++ libraries, such as my C API for calling
the Xerces C++ XML parser.

In C++:

   extern "C" char *trim(char *src)
   {
      // do whatever
   }

In C:

   #include <stdio.h>
   extern char *trim(char *);

   int main(void)
   {
      puts(trim("abc "));
      return 0;
   }

C++'s 'extern "C"' indicates that the implementation should use the
C naming rules (exactly what that means is implementation-defined)
for either the following single external identifier, or if it's
followed by a block, for all the external identifiers in the block.
It works in either direction - to declare an external routine with
C linkage, or to define a C++ function which will be callable with C
linkage.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-01T18:01:08-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410011701.35bfca7a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com> <fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com> <cjju280f54@news2.newsguy.com>`

```
mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote 

> > > You get around mangling by writing a wrapper:
> > > 
> > > extern "C" char* trim(char*);

> Sorry, Richard, but while Robert's solution isn't quite right, he's
> correct that 'extern "C"' can be applied to a definition in C++ to
> create a C++ function with an unmangled name.

Yes, of course, but what he seemed to think would happen is that the
String method trim() would be left unmangled.  This would not happen. 
He later caught on an wrote, as you said, a C function that was
unmangled, moving further from his original claim of 'you only need
to' and revoking his use of INVOKE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-02T12:32:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ltm3cph5ooba9@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com> <fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com> <cjju280f54@news2.newsguy.com>`

```

"Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message
news:cjju280f54@news2.newsguy.com...
>
> In article <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com>,
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) writes:
> > Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
> >
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> linkage.

Mr Wojcik, I must disagree. Not having a copy of the C++ standard,
I will refer to the Working Draft dated 2 December 1996.

------
7.5 Linkage specifications
1 All function types, function names, and variable names have a
    language linkage. ... The default language linkage of all function
    types, function names, and variable names is C++ language
    linkage.
2 Linkage (3.5) between C++ and non-C++ code fragments can
    be achieved using a linkage-specification:
        linkage-specification:
            extern string-literal { declaration-seq opt }
            extern string-literal declaration
    The string-literal indicates the required language linkage. The
    meaning of the string-literal is implementation defined. A
    linkage-specification with a string that is unknown to the
    implementation is ill-formed. When the string literal in a
    linkage-specification names a programming language, the
    spelling of the programming language's name is
    implementation-defined. [Note: it is recommended that the
    spelling be taken from the document defining that language,
    for example Ada (not ADA) and Fortran or FORTRAN
    (depending on the vintage). ]
3 Every implementation shall provide for linkage to functions
    written in the C programming language, "C", and linkage to
    C++ functions, "C++". [Example:
        complex sqrt(complex); // C++ linkage by default
        extern "C" {
            double sqrt(double); // C linkage
        }
    -end example]
------

'extern "C"' indicates that a 'function type, function name, [or]
variable name' was compiled by another language and has the
language linkage of the C programming language.

A C++ compiler is not a C compiler, therefore it does not supply
"C" language linkage. [Note that in 1 and 2, the default language
linkage is C++, therefore 'extern "C++" is never required for a
'code fragment' compiled with C++.] This means that using
'extern "C"' 'to define a C++ function which will be callable with
C linkage' is an implementor extension and is inconsistent with
the intent expressed in 7.5.

That you 'use that all the time to create C bindings for C++
libraries' does not mean that 'extern "C"', in that context, is
C++. It might be that since standard C has no means for
identifying language linkage [there is 'fortran' as a
function-specifier] it is simply more convenient for C++
implementors to 'abuse' C++.

It seems to me that the means for COBOL to call C++
within the standards for both is the CALL-CONVENTION
directive. This is where a COBOL implementor may define
the equivalent of 'extern "C++"' to permit COBOL to call
C++ directly.

...
>>CALL-CONVENTION C++
    CALL "cpptrim" USING MY-STRING
>>CALL-CONVENTION C
    CALL "strlen" USING MY-STRING
        RETURNING MY-STRING-LENGTH
>>CALL-CONVENTION COBOL
...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-02T22:48:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t3bul0desqh2repus9c1aem2sibai5i6d7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com> <fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com> <cjju280f54@news2.newsguy.com> <10ltm3cph5ooba9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sat, 2 Oct 2004 12:32:22 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message
…[90 more quoted lines elided]…
>language linkage of the C programming language.

You didn't quote far enough. 7.5.6 gives a examples of C++ functions
being called with C linkage:

   namespace A {
              extern "C" int f();
              extern "C" int g() { return 1; }
              extern "C" int h();
          }
          namespace B {
              extern "C" int f();      // A::f and B::f refer
                                               // to the same function
              extern "C" int g() { return 1; } 
                 // ill-formed, the function g
                 // with C language linkage
                 // has two definitions
          }
          int A::f() { return 98; } // definition for the function f
                                         // with C language linkage
          extern "C" int h() { return 97; }
                 // definition for the function h
                 // with C language linkage
                 // A::h and ::h refer to the same function

http://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/open/n2356/

>A C++ compiler is not a C compiler, therefore it does not supply
>"C" language linkage. [Note that in 1 and 2, the default language
>linkage is C++, therefore 'extern "C++" is never required for a
>'code fragment' compiled with C++.]

Here is an example of code needing extern "C++":

extern "C" {void cpptrim (string c) {extern "C++" c.trim(); return;}}

> This means that using
>'extern "C"' 'to define a C++ function which will be callable with
>C linkage' is an implementor extension and is inconsistent with
>the intent expressed in 7.5.

The text you posted talks about calling OUT from C++. It doesn't talk
about RECEIVING a call from another language. Further down in 7.5 it
does talk about receiving.

While you're reading the standard, look at:

21.1.1.9.6                              [lib.string::find.last.not.of]

It is the trim function, by another name.

str[find.last.not.of(str, 30, 1) + 1) = char(0);
```

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-02T21:56:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10lun59h8bmbu4f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com> <fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com> <cjju280f54@news2.newsguy.com> <10ltm3cph5ooba9@corp.supernews.com> <t3bul0desqh2repus9c1aem2sibai5i6d7@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:t3bul0desqh2repus9c1aem2sibai5i6d7@4ax.com...
[snip]
> While you're reading the standard, look at:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> str[find.last.not.of(str, 30, 1) + 1) = char(0);

Mr Wagner, you exaggerate. 'basic_string::find_last_not_of'
is not trim, by another name. It is one means by which to
write a trim() function.

As to 'extern "C"', at this point, I am more inclined to
believe the C-FAQ, which refers to H&S.

Harbison & Steele, "C, A Reference Manual," page 285,
"C++ uses a different convention for calling its functions,
which means that, in general, it is not possible to call a
C++ function from a C program. However, C++ does
provide a way to call C functions from C++."

7.5.6 says nothing about calling C++ from C or any other
language. It discusses "C" language linkage for functions
compiled by C++, which is not the same a providing a
mechanism for calls *from* C. 7.5.2 discusses 'non-C++
code fragments' and only as declarations, not definitions.
7.5.3 discusses linkage *to* functions written in C.
7.5.9 discusses 'objects' to and from C++; but 7.5.6
distinguishes between functions and objects.

I find no mention in 7.5 of linkage *from* functions written
in C or any other language.

Anyway, until I actually see something that signals an intent
to provide a means for calling C++ functions from C, I will
remain skeptical that 'calling C++ from C' is any more than
an implementor extension.
```
