Duh, I must be missing something here.

Seems to me looking for anything using PERFORM UNTIL  is perforce itself a
linear search.

Unless, of course, the OP wanted to compare the use of the SEARCH verb with
some kind of   "Set idx up by +1, if equal...'PERFORM' loop".. in which case
the use of the SEARCH verb (generally implemented as a linear search anyway)
is likely preferable only because the compiler publisher probably optimizes
better than does the typical COBOL programmer.

MCM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Linear search Vs Perfrom Until

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-11T08:33:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-ED13E2.08333911102004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<9c9fdae5.0407160827.7b64a9b7@posting.google.com> <Jt2dnWf4hf-GbPzcRVn-tg@comcast.com> <cju86a$4t2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <R_y8d.14236$Qv5.1048@newssvr33.news.prodigy.com> <cjuh3b$1k4p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <%tC8d.7109$Rf1.719@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <%tC8d.7109$Rf1.719@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>,
 "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
> news:cjuh3b$1k4p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

Move '???' to Lookup-Key
Compute I = Hi-Idx / 2
Set Found to true

Perform Until Lookup-Key = My-Table-Key (I)
           or Not-Found

   Evaluate true

      when Hi-Idx = Lo-Idx
      when Hi-Idx < Lo-Idx
      when Hi-Idx = (Lo-Idx + 1) 
         Set Not-Found to true

      when Lookup-Key < My-Table-Key (I)
         Compute Hi-Idx = I
         Compute I = I - ((I - Lo-Idx)/2)

      when Lookup-Key > My-Table-Key (I)
         Compute Lo-Idx = I
         Compute I = I + ((Hi-Idx - I)/2)

      when Lookup-Key = My-Table-Key (I)
         continue

   End-Evaluate

End-Perform

Or something like that...
```

## Thread: COBOL Screen Question — 4 msgs

### COBOL Screen Question

- **From:** "news.gvtc.com" <dbrannic@gvtc.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-04T22:28:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0_WdneHQ6cFzif_cRVn-rA@gvtc.com>`

```
I have a screen section within an Acucobol program that has fields defined 
as input/output (i.e. Using). I would like to dynamically within the program 
code 'Protect' a field from the user entering in data and then unprotect it 
later on. I can't find or figure out how to do this.

Has anyone done this? Can any share their experiences/solutions?

thanks.
Doug
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Screen Question

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-05T18:05:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jnB8d.638245$gE.610533@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<0_WdneHQ6cFzif_cRVn-rA@gvtc.com>`
- **References:** `<0_WdneHQ6cFzif_cRVn-rA@gvtc.com>`

```
news.gvtc.com wrote:

>I have a screen section within an Acucobol program that has fields defined 
>as input/output (i.e. Using). I would like to dynamically within the program 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Seems to me you are pushing your luck on this one - you are trying to 
emulate the Windows "disable", "enable" or "readOnly".

Don't know your compiler but does it allow you to (1)ACCEPT WHOLE SCREEN 
v (2)ACCEPT THIS FIELD.  Given (1) - segment your ACCEPT to ACCEPT 
SUB-SCREEN (the majority), and programatically (2)ACCEPT THIS FIELD. 
Presumably when it comes to changing, you are prompting user for the 
Field-Number to be changed. If it is one you currently want to 'protect' 
- an error message, "Can't change this right now....".

I would have thought your choices are pretty limited - check out  the 
AcuCOBOL full screen syntax, addressing each 'word' to see if there's 
something hidden behind the general name. (I've just checked the Micro 
Focus Screen Section which is COBOL 2002 PLUS extensions - and I don't 
see anything there allowing a 'protect').

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Screen Question

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-05T15:46:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410051446.74ecc883@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<0_WdneHQ6cFzif_cRVn-rA@gvtc.com>`

```
"news.gvtc.com" <dbrannic@gvtc.com> wrote 

> I have a screen section within an Acucobol program that has fields defined 
> as input/output (i.e. Using). I would like to dynamically within the program 
> code 'Protect' a field from the user entering in data and then unprotect it 
> later on. I can't find or figure out how to do this.

If it is part of a DISPLAY it will be displayed.  If it is part of an
ACCEPT then ...

One of the reasons why I don't ACCEPT more than one field at a time
and have avoided SCREEN SECTION is the difficulty in interacting
precisely with the user. It can be done by making the TAB key or
falling off a field into returning back to the accept, then using the
CURSOR is to set the next field for the user.  In this way you would
have to control every field change but could avoid particular fields.

You could chenge the 'using' to some work field which you initally
fill and then reset if the user changes it.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Screen Question

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-05T22:34:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zN2dnTRdfIVYxP7cRVn-hg@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<0_WdneHQ6cFzif_cRVn-rA@gvtc.com>`

```
You could do it if you were using the emulated graphical controls on a
character mode screen.  For example:

01 main-screen.
    03 field-1 entry-field line 1 col 1 value ws-data-1.
    03 field-2 entry-field line 2 col 1 value ws-data-2
    03 field-3 entry-field line 3 col 1 value ws-data-3.
...
...
...
        display main-screen.
        modify field-2 enabled = false.
        accept main-screen.  | This will allow entry in only fields 1 and 3
...
...
        modify field-2 enabled = true.
        accept main-screen.  | This will allow entry in all 3 fields

Otherwise, don't accept a higher level screen section entry - accept the
lower level entries and handle moving back and forth between fields in the
code and determine which you will accept or not.


"news.gvtc.com" <dbrannic@gvtc.com> wrote in message
news:0_WdneHQ6cFzif_cRVn-rA@gvtc.com...
> I have a screen section within an Acucobol program that has fields defined
> as input/output (i.e. Using). I would like to dynamically within the
program
> code 'Protect' a field from the user entering in data and then unprotect
it
> later on. I can't find or figure out how to do this.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: IshellLink in Cobol — 2 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [2004-09](2004-09.md#t-0e272091e0)._

#### ↳ Re: IshellLink in Cobol

- **From:** christoph.dunker@land-data.de (Christoph Dunker)
- **Date:** 2004-10-05T07:05:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5872a124.0410050605.520995c4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5872a124.0409220754.7dd27022@posting.google.com>`

```
Thanks for your Example!

I don't want to use Windows Scripting Host because some of our
customers have deactivated it.

I want to use the Ishelllink function in shell32.dll.

does anyone know how to use it or does anyone have other ideas of
getting infos from a .lnk file via cobol?

Greetings 

Christoph Dunker
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IshellLink in Cobol

- **From:** "David Sands" <david.sands@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-11T10:12:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ckdhuk$or1$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<5872a124.0409220754.7dd27022@posting.google.com> <5872a124.0410050605.520995c4@posting.google.com>`

```
To use IShellLink directly using Net Express you need to have a
understanding of how COM works at the interface level.

Basically COM presents an Interface as a VTABLE which is an array of
procedure pointers in COBOL which correspond to the Methods in the
Interface.

You have to be very careful that you get the ordering of the functions
correct or your likely to get a crash.

The other key point is that when you get back a Interface it is actually a
pointer to the pointer to the VTABLE (2 levels of indirection). This should
explain what:-

           set address of vtabptr to IPersistFile
           set address of lnkvtab to vtabptr

is doing.

The code itself just adds a shortcut to the startmenu.

As you can see the code for WSH Objects is far simpler as that is exposed as
an automation object (hence you can use Invoke syntax on it).

Code is provided as-is.

If anyone wants a working Net Express project for this then just send me an
email.

Regards
David.

Code>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
      *> This is required for OLE applications
      $set ooctrl(+P)
       copy "windows".
       program-id. hshortcut.
       special-names.
           call-convention 74 is winapi.

       class-control.
       *> OLE automation classes
           dummy  is class "$OLE$dummy"
           olesup is class "olesup"
           .

       working-storage section.
       01  theShell            object reference.
       01  thedesktop          object reference.
      *01  thestartmenu        object reference.
       01  specialfolders      object reference.
       01  theshortcut         object reference.
       01  thelinkpath         pic x(300).
       01  unipath             pic X(500).
       01  anint               pic 9(9) comp-5.

       01  thestartmenu        pic x(300).
       01  procptr             usage is procedure-pointer.

      *copy "wintypes.cpy".
       copy "mfole".
       78 CSIDL-DESKTOP                  value h"0000".
       78 CSIDL-INTERNET                 value h"0001".
       78 CSIDL-PROGRAMS                 value h"0002".
       78 CSIDL-CONTROLS                 value h"0003".
       78 CSIDL-PRINTERS                 value h"0004".
       78 CSIDL-PERSONAL                 value h"0005".
       78 CSIDL-FAVORITES                value h"0006".
       78 CSIDL-STARTUP                  value h"0007".
       78 CSIDL-RECENT                   value h"0008".
       78 CSIDL-SENDTO                   value h"0009".
       78 CSIDL-BITBUCKET                value h"000a".
       78 CSIDL-STARTMENU                value h"000b".
       78 CSIDL-DESKTOPDIRECTORY         value h"0010".
       78 CSIDL-DRIVES                   value h"0011".
       78 CSIDL-NETWORK                  value h"0012".
       78 CSIDL-NETHOOD                  value h"0013".
       78 CSIDL-FONTS                    value h"0014".
       78 CSIDL-TEMPLATES                value h"0015".
       78 CSIDL-COMMON-STARTMENU         value h"0016".
       78 CSIDL-COMMON-PROGRAMS          value h"0017".
       78 CSIDL-COMMON-STARTUP           value h"0018".
       78 CSIDL-COMMON-DESKTOPDIRECTORY  value h"0019".
       78 CSIDL-APPDATA                  value h"001a".
       78 CSIDL-PRINTHOOD                value h"001b".
       78 CSIDL-COMMON-FAVORITES         value h"001f".
       78 CSIDL-INTERNET-CACHE           value h"0020".
       78 CSIDL-COOKIES                  value h"0021".
       78 CSIDL-HISTORY                  value h"0022".

       01 retval                          BOOL.

       78  IID-IConnectionPointContainer
                      VALUE x"84B296B1B4BA1A10B69C00AA00341D07".
       78  CLSID-ShellLink
                           VALUE x"0114020000000000C000000000000046".
       78  IID-IShellLink
                           VALUE x"EE14020000000000C000000000000046".
       78  IID-IPersistFile
                           VALUE x"0b01000000000000C000000000000046".
       01  IShellLink         pointer.
       01  IPersistFile       pointer.

       78  CLSCTX-INPROC-SERVER    value h"00000001". *> 0x1

       01  hres        DWORD.
       01  ws-success  PIC 9(9) COMP-5.

       linkage section.
       01  vtabptr                     pointer.
       01  lnkvtab.
           03  fptr        procedure-pointer occurs 100.
       01 lnk-IShellLink redefines lnkvtab.
           03 IUnknown-QueryInterface        usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IUnknown-Addref                usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IUnknown-Release               usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-GetPath             usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-GetIDList           usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetIDList           usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-GetDescription      usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetDescription      usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-GetWorkingDirectory usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetWorkingDirectory usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-GetArguments        usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetArguments        usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-GetHotkey           usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetHotkey           usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-GetShowCmd          usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetShowCmd          usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-GetIconLocation     usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetIconLocation     usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetRelativePath     usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-Resolve             usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IShellLink-SetPath             usage procedure-pointer.
       01 lnk-IPersistFile redefines lnkvtab.
           03 IUnknown-QueryInterfaceX       usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IUnknown-AddrefX               usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IUnknown-ReleaseX              usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IPersist-GetClassID            usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IPersistFile-IsDirty           usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IPersistFile-Load              usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IPersistFile-Save              usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IPersistFile-SaveCompleted     usage procedure-pointer.
           03 IPersistFile-GetCurFile        usage procedure-pointer.

       procedure division.
       start-it section.

      *    invoke wshshell "new" returning theShell
           invoke olesup "singleThread" returning ws-success

      *    Use CSIDL-COMMON-STARTMENU to get the ALL Users startmenu
      *
           call winapi "SHGetSpecialFolderPathA"
               using by value     0
                     by reference thestartmenu
                     by value     CSIDL-COMMON-STARTMENU
                     by value     0
               returning retval
           string thestartmenu                 delimited by x"00"
                  z"\Micro Focus Web Site.lnk" delimited by size
               into thelinkpath
           end-string

      ***** Initialize COM Libraries

           call winapi "CoInitialize" using by value 0 size 4

      ***** Create an Instance of IShellLink
           call winapi "CoCreateInstance"
                       using by reference  CLSID-ShellLink
                             by value      0 size 4
                             by value      CLSCTX-INPROC-SERVER
                             by reference  IID-IShellLink
                             by reference  IShellLink
               returning hres
           end-call
           perform check-hres

      ***** Setup the IShellLink VTABLE

           set address of vtabptr to IShellLink
           set address of lnkvtab to vtabptr

           call winapi IShellLink-SetPath
                   using by value IShellLink
                         by reference z"http://www.microfocus.com"
               returning hres
           end-call
           perform check-hres
           call winapi IShellLink-SetDescription
                   using by value IShellLink
                         by reference z"Micro Focus Web Site"
               returning hres
           end-call
           perform check-hres

      ***** Get IPersist file

           call winapi IUnknown-QueryInterface
               using by value IShellLink
                     by reference IID-IPersistFile
                     by reference IPersistFile
               returning hres
           perform check-hres

      ***** Convert Path to Unicode
           call winapi "MultiByteToWideChar"
               using by value CP-ACP
                     by value 0  size 4
                     by reference thelinkpath
                     by value -1 size 4
                     by reference unipath
                     by value length of unipath size 4
               returning hres
           end-call

           set address of vtabptr to IPersistFile
           set address of lnkvtab to vtabptr
           call winapi IPersistFile-Save
               using by value IPersistFile
                     by reference unipath
                     by value 1 size 4
               returning hres
           end-call
           perform check-hres


      ***** Cleanup used Interfaces

      ***** Release IPersistFile
           call winapi IUnknown-Release
               using by value IPersistFile
               returning hres
           end-call

      ***** Release IShellLink
           set address of vtabptr to IShellLink
           set address of lnkvtab to vtabptr
           call winapi IUnknown-Release
               using by value IShellLink
               returning hres
           end-call

           call winapi "CoUninitialize"

           stop run.



       check-hres section.

           if hres not = 0
               display "Error : Die Gracefully"
               stop run
           end-if
           .

endCode<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

news:5872a124.0410050605.520995c4@posting.google.com...

>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christoph Dunker

- **From:** Esmond Pitt <esmond.pitt@not.bigpond.com>

```
Richard wrote:

> It derives from the X/Open standard of 20 years ago or more.

It originally derives from Data General ICOBOL of around 1978-9. 
Definitely green-screen stuff & definitely should have become a 
USE-procedure-based event-driven thing in COBOL-2002. I did some work on 
a prototype for this about 1992 so we are really a terribly long way 
behind the state of the art now.

Esmond Pitt
formerly of Austec International Ltd
```

programming with the DECLARATIVES.

.    Am  08.10.04
schrieb  esmond.pitt@not.bigpond.com (Esmond Pitt)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  WZm9d.17818$5O5.7293@news-server.bigpond.net.au
  ueber  Re: Screen Section, Screen Events, Declaratives

LW>>    Although, really, COBOL provides all conditions for
LW>> event-driven programming with the DECLARATIVES.

EP> That's what I meant, USE PROCEDURES are in the DECLARITIVES.

   As I wrote in the message which opened this thread, dated 22.09.04,  
17:38, Msg-Id  yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4gpgi6.pminewsnews.t-online.de

   I was just considering the way I work with VDP (a database-oriented  
development tool) and Visual Basic, where one works with a graphical  
interface on the screen form and specifies the events and how to  
handle them more or less as properties of the fields and the form  
itself.

   This is something which I would like to have for a COBOL program.  
This tool should produce a COPY element for the SCREEN SECTION and  
another COPY element for the DECLARATIVES part of the PROCEDURE  
DIVISION. The program would then be compiled and linked with a screen  
handling run time module.

   Back in my Univac days, I worked with DPS (Display Processing  
Sytsem), which worked in a similar way, i.e. there is a program which  
allows to design a form, which then produces a COPY element to be used  
in the COBOL program (or for another language).

   The difference is of course that there is no screen event  
processing for the DECLARATIVES since that part is handled by the  
terminal which sends the data in one chunk to the 'big iron' host.


EP>>> I did some work on a prototype for this about 1992 so we are
EP>>> really a terribly long way behind the state of the art now.

LW>>    A specification or actual software?

EP> Both.

   And that went under with this Australian company whose fate you  
described in the other message?

   Nothing transferred to RM/COBOL? A pity.

   To use ACCEPT and DISPLAY unter control of a program with its  
strict sequence is, in my opinion, not the proper way to handle screen  
events.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Ohne Pressefreiheit, Vereins- und Versammlungsrecht ist keine
Arbeiterbewegung mï¿½glich"        - Friedrich Engels      (Februar 1865)
```

[snip]
> EP>>> I did some work on a prototype for this about 1992 so we are
> EP>>> really a terribly long way behind the state of the art now.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Nothing transferred to RM/COBOL? A pity.

If Esmond did this in 1992, it was several years after the dissolution of 
Austec.

>
>   To use ACCEPT and DISPLAY unter control of a program with its
> strict sequence is, in my opinion, not the proper way to handle screen
> events.

I certainly agree with this.  The 'strict sequence' tends to cause the 
combination of business rules with the presentation of data, confusing the 
distinction between the two.  This confusion leads in many cases to confused 
code.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section, Screen Events, Declaratives

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T15:36:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ynd9d.1693$Lk3.210@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4gpgi6.pminews@news.t-online.de> <cisa96$2pk7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0409221426.24a1569@posting.google.com> <qVN8d.16053$5O5.6476@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <EIOdndXYDLgkBPncRVn-vw@comcast.com>`

"JJ" <jj@nospam.com> wrote in message 
news:EIOdndXYDLgkBPncRVn-vw@comcast.com...
>> formerly of Austec International Ltd
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to Acucobol (after Austec was bought by RM or something like that).

Actually, it was the other way around.

Austec purchased Ryan-McFarland Corporation (RMC) from Don Ryan and Dave 
McFarland.  Austec, a publicly traded company in Australia, had a very rough 
go when the major stock market downturn caught it in the undertow.  Language 
Processors Inc (LPI) purchased the assets of Austec, including RMC, in the 
late 1980s.  The combined companies were renamed Liant Software Corporation. 
The corporate geneology actually goes back to the 1960s in the RMC branch. 
See http://www.liant.com/about/.

I got to travel to Melbourne (Austec's corporate HQ) as the folks in RMC and 
Austec began to learn about each others' technology.  Esmond, a VP in Austec 
at the time, was a gracious host and is a top-rate mind.

Tom Morrison

>
> "Esmond Pitt" <esmond.pitt@not.bigpond.com> wrote in message
> news:qVN8d.16053$5O5.6476@news-server.bigpond.net.au...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section, Screen Events, Declaratives

- **Date:** 2004-10-08T02:29:59+00:00

- **In-Reply-To:** `<Ynd9d.1693$Lk3.210@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4gpgi6.pminews@news.t-online.de> <cisa96$2pk7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0409221426.24a1569@posting.google.com> <qVN8d.16053$5O5.6476@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <EIOdndXYDLgkBPncRVn-vw@comcast.com> <Ynd9d.1693$Lk3.210@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Tom Morrison wrote:

> Austec, a publicly traded company in Australia, had a very rough 
> go when the major stock market downturn caught it in the undertow.

Hi Tom, you're much too kind. Austec expired from a bad case of 
founder's disease, started to acquire things it knew nothing about, took 
its eye completely off the RMC ball (at one point I believe actually 
*ran out of stock* of RM/COBOL installation diskettes), and blew $30m in 
listing capital which should have kept us going from then until now. 
Eventually it missed an interest payment, the bank moved in, and we all 
moved on. Onwards and upwards of course ... I got my money out in time.

> I got to travel to Melbourne (Austec's corporate HQ) as the folks in RMC and 
> Austec began to learn about each others' technology.  Esmond, a VP in Austec 
> at the time, was a gracious host and is a top-rate mind.

Again Tom you're too kind but I also greatly enjoyed your hospitality in 
Texas and found several top-rate minds at RM including yourself.

Best regards

Esmond Pitt

- **Date:** 2004-10-18T14:30:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0410181330.39091ff9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3cebc769.0410060635.5bc293e3@posting.google.com> <ckdidr$25kt$1@newsreader1.mclink.it> <57e9c7c9.0410160738.4579b285@posting.google.com> <0775n0tithm3psb4iaddqk3fjm95cl7n07@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote in message news:<0775n0tithm3psb4iaddqk3fjm95cl7n07@4ax.com>...

> On 16 Oct 2004 08:38:50 -0700, aortega@cobtools.com (Alberto Ortega)
> enlightened us:
 
> >i have developed a COBOL editor, is cobedit
> >is only for COBOL programmers

> >the site for download is www.cobtools.com
> 
> Anbody in CLC using the above program?  Is it useful if you only do
> mainframe Cobol?  Any CICS command editing?  Just curious.

Cobedit runs on MS-Windows 95 and up. It is designed to edit COBOL
programs and also to launch a number of tools using either menu
choices or tool bar buttons. It has a number of helpful features:

- highlighting of reserved words (but not real syntax coloring)
- a tree structured view of some parts of the COBOL program
- several ways of indenting code
- You can add your own choice of compilers, other tools or runtimes.
(Read the online help *first*.) You need to specify the proper command
line plus arguments to invoke those programs.
- option of saving the source file before editing (*nice*)
- option of capturing (standard) output from the compile step and
displaying it or displaying the contents of a selected error or log
file in a secondary window

It is set up to work with compilers that produce code which is
interpreted by a runtime module. It may work with compile, link and
execute style programs as well, but this probably requires adding the
link step and the execute step to the tool menu.

It may handle groups of files as a main file plus COPY files, but I
have not tried this feature. While I usually edit, compile, link and
run from the command line, this program is tempting. If you can figure
out the correct command lines to use, this might be an attractive
alternative to the "Programming Staff" supplied with the free version
of Fujitsu Cobol (Ver 3.0) which may be of interest to students.
```

<a id="t-435257d042"></a>
## Thread: Computer Statement Usage — 100 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@frontiernet.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-06T20:50:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny>`

```
I teach a class in File Structures in which COBOL is used as the 
language of interest.  We are using Thane Hubble's book as a text. 
After spending considerable time on the ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, and 
DIVIDE verbs, he introduces the COMPUTE verb.

My question is this - to what extent is the COMPUTE verb used.  The one 
advantage I can see is the ability to calculate exponents which would be 
extremely valuable for calculating interest payments.  But my sense, is 
that it is not used often.  The four previous verbs are much more common 
and continue to be widely used.  Am I correct?

The question arises out of a student question to which I did not know 
the correct answer.  Thank you for any direction you can give.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-06T21:03:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35Z8d.2192085$ic1.223442@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny>`

```
My impression is that the COMPUTE statement (at least in some environments and 
compilers) is OVER-used (i.e. used even when it isn't needed).  If you 
"understand" what each compiler does with intermediate results, then it can be 
pretty powerful.

It is also important to know that with the '89 Standard definition of intrinsic 
functions, you may NOT "MOVE" their results to a field, you MUST use COMPUTE. 
With the 2002 Standard (and as an extension with some - but not all earlier 
compilers) you can use MOVE, but this is another place where COMPUTE is 
valuable.

Personally, I have rarely seen a series of "simple" arithmetic statements, where 
a single (complex) COMPUTE statement could be used.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2004-10-06T22:07:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0410062107.51f6c06c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <35Z8d.2192085$ic1.223442@news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<35Z8d.2192085$ic1.223442@news.easynews.com>...

Bill Klein wrote:
>
> If you "understand" what each compiler does with intermediate results, then   
> it can be pretty powerful.                                                  

I came across the following in another group for IBM COBOL.  I don't
recall the author's name.

It seems like a good "one size fits all" solution to the "rounding" 
problem Bill alludes to.

Does anybody see a downside?  I'd appreciate your feedback.

Regards, Jack.

Simply define a variable (e.g. SEE-NOTE-ABOVE) in Working Storage
as follows:

*  NOTE: The " SEE-NOTE-ABOVE" variable below is used to prevent
*  erroneous truncation of COMPUTEd values that use division or multi-
*  plication.

 01   SEE-NOTE-ABOVE       PIC9(1)V9(17) VALUE ZEROS.

Now add this variable to any COMPUTE expression as follows.

 COMPUTE   F  =  (A / B) * C   +
                 SEE-NOTE-ABOVE

The addition of zero is not going to change the actual value,
but it does nullify the Truncation Menace!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T16:48:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ere9d.637111$M95.561582@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<8a2d426e.0410062107.51f6c06c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <35Z8d.2192085$ic1.223442@news.easynews.com> <8a2d426e.0410062107.51f6c06c@posting.google.com>`

```
Jack Sleight wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<35Z8d.2192085$ic1.223442@news.easynews.com>...
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That's a real interesting tip Jack.

Way back since RM/COBOL days I have had to take formulae and break them 
down into separate COMPUTEs to get the accuracy I wanted. I would much 
prefer to have stayed with the ASME formulae and turn them into single 
COMPUTE statements, with ROUNDING.

I'll give it a shot 'old style' and 'new style' and get back with info 
on what I achieved.

BTW - although the 'arithmetic' verbs allow for ROUNDING, I think the 
COMPUTE with ROUNDING is much less cumbersome.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2004-10-10T00:36:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0410092336.580bb0b@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Jimmy,

Just a heads up:

I arbitrarily moved the zero field from the start to the end of the
equation.

It just dawned on me that this MIGHT delay the expansion of
intermediate work fields during the COMPUTE process and nullify the
zero field's effect on precision.

Regards, Jack.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T14:38:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck4cvo$2hn5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <35Z8d.2192085$ic1.223442@news.easynews.com> <8a2d426e.0410062107.51f6c06c@posting.google.com>`

```
"Jack Sleight" <jacksleight@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:8a2d426e.0410062107.51f6c06c@posting.google.com...

> I came across the following in another group for IBM COBOL.  I don't
> recall the author's name.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ...

It may be a "one size fits all" approach for IBM COBOL, but I'm not
convinced it's an appropriate approach for implementations that provide
underlying precision of arithmetic results greater than 18 digits, or for
that matter the ability to declare items with greater precision.   It may
fit *you*, and it may even fit *some others*, but I don't think it fits
*all*.

As Bill has pointed out, the COMPUTE in question could, if the compiler
writer so chose, produce results identical to those produced by FUNCTION
RANDOM, with or without the "+ SEE-NOTE-ABOVE".

The COBOL compilers I deal with most often seem to be operating under the
presumption that multiplication is commutative and produce identical object
code for the following:
    COMPUTE F = (A / B) * C.
    COMPUTE F = (C*A) / B.
    COMPUTE F = A / B * C.
    COMPUTE F = C * A / B.
(namely something like value (C), value (A), multiply, value (B), divide,
description (F), store)
and equivalent object code for
    COMPUTE F = (A * C) / B.
    COMPUTE F = A * C / B.
(which differs only in that the first two operands in the expression are
reversed).

Is there a reason they shouldn't?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-06T17:40:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:
> I teach a class in File Structures in which COBOL is used as the 
> language of interest.  We are using Thane Hubble's book as a text. After 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Floyd Johnson

The way I choose between constructs is by the number of operands 
included in the final calculation.  More than a couple, and I will use 
compute.  In fact, compute is used a lot in code that I see daily. For 
example:

add 1 to line-counter.
compute percentage = amount-one / (this + that) * 100.

Both types of calculation are important, though in any given shop you 
may see a clear preponderance of one over the other. In fact, it is 
largely a matter of style, and IMO style should always lean toward 
readability.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T02:05:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Wed, 06 Oct 2004 17:40:16 -0400, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>.  In fact, compute is used a lot in code that I see daily. For 
>example:
>
>add 1 to line-counter.
>compute percentage = amount-one / (this + that) * 100.

That's incorrect. It should be compute percentage = (100*
amount-one)/(this + that). Your way loses precision.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T00:01:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >compute percentage = amount-one / (this + that) * 100.
> 
> That's incorrect. It should be compute percentage = (100*
> amount-one)/(this + that). Your way loses precision.

It is the assignment to percentage will most likely 'lose precision'
rather than the calculation. Donald's would only be 'incorrect' if
there was a loss in the precision that was specified, as indicated by
the number of decimal digits in 'percentage'.

It requires proper numeric analysis to show which is incorrect.  For
example if 'amount-one' is large enough then your way could exceed the
value allowable in the intermediate result leading to the completely
wrong answer, while Donald's may give a correct result always if
'percentage' is, say, PIC S9(m)V99.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T08:07:47-05:00

> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

Intermediate results matter for accuracy, they don't count for significant 
digits of precision. Division always loses precision.

N x M / P is always more precise than N/P x M

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T18:58:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<blg9d.2258266$ic1.230577@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <R7Gdner4pp05ovjcRVn-jA@giganews.com> <ck3td7$2778$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:ck3td7$2778$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >
<snip>
>> Intermediate results matter for accuracy, they don't count for significant
>> digits of precision. Division always loses precision.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just to go one step further.   As far as the '85 Standard goes (and the '68 and 
the '74 - and the '02 when NATIVE arithmetic is in effect), the Standard(s) are 
quite clear,

If one (a compiler) "wanted" to,  they could return the day of the month when a 
program coded:
   N x M / P

and the current pound-sterling to USD ration when the program coded
  N/P x M

When the Standard did (does, probably always will for NATIVE arithmetic) say 
"implementor defined" for evaluation of arithmetic expression, that is REALLY 
what it means.

Another example, is the (semi-famous) fact that one of the most "commonly used" 
vendor's COBOL compilers (often) evaluate

    7 / 4 * 4
        *AND*
    4 * 4 / 7
as 4 and not as 7

  ***

As I have reported in CLC before, I know of one implementation that both CLAIMED 
to be '85 Standard conforming *and* that passed all the NIST conformance tests 
when they "truncated" any decimal value used in exponentiation, i.e. they gave 
the same result for

   Num-Field ** .5
         and
    Num-Field ** 0

This all goes make to my original statement about the fact that one MUST 
understand the specific implementation's handling of "intermediate results" when 
using the COMPUTE statement.
```

> >

Standard(s) are
> quite clear,

when a
> program coded:
>    N x M / P
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> When the Standard did (does, probably always will for NATIVE arithmetic)
say
> "implementor defined" for evaluation of arithmetic expression, that is
REALLY
> what it means.
>

Good examples, Bill. I know you've always been worried about the term
"implementor defined". And with good reason.

> Another example, is the (semi-famous) fact that one of the most "commonly
used"
> vendor's COBOL compilers (often) evaluate
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> As I have reported in CLC before, I know of one implementation that both
CLAIMED
> to be '85 Standard conforming *and* that passed all the NIST conformance
tests
> when they "truncated" any decimal value used in exponentiation, i.e. they
gave
> the same result for
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> understand the specific implementation's handling of "intermediate
results" when
> using the COMPUTE statement.
>

That is a fair and reasonable statement. I'm not taking issue with it, but
in passing, I have to note that I have NEVER had problems with intermediate
results, causing an unacceptable result. I have to admit I neither know nor
care what COBOL COMPUTEs do with intermediate results, on all the platforms
for which I have written COBOL. The fact that I have managed to get away
without needing this information may be due to a combination of the
following factors:

1. I make sure that arithmetic fields are defined larger than needed.
2. In the real world there is probably less requirement for  extended
accuracy than there is in the academic exercise of looking at what COULD
happen. Most commercial applications don't need to be accurate beyond 4
decimals (for currency).

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rjones0@hotmail.com (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T13:08:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6dd8322.0410071208.12471c7d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <R7Gdner4pp05ovjcRVn-jA@giganews.com>`

```
Bottom posting

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<R7Gdner4pp05ovjcRVn-jA@giganews.com>...
> Richard wrote:
> > Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> N x M / P is always more precise than N/P x M


My view on this, without going into great detail, is that it is often
advisable to keep division and exponentiation out of compound compute
statements.  Even if the original author worked it out correctly, a
subsequent less experienced programmer may not appreciate the full
impact of minor code additions to a COMPUTE statement.

As I recall, even if the statement is "correctly" constructed for the
best stability, the accuracy of the results can vary considerably
according to the magnitude of the various values used in the compute
statement when divisions are involved.  Multiplying by reciprocals is
sometimes rather better behaved, if the reciprocal is of a suitable
type.

The 2002 standard has "standard" arithmetic with floating-point
conversions for intermediate values to keep accuracy at a high level,
but there is an efficiency premium to be paid.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T13:41:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck49l4$2fh1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <R7Gdner4pp05ovjcRVn-jA@giganews.com> <6dd8322.0410071208.12471c7d@posting.google.com>`

```

"Robert Jones" <rjones0@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:6dd8322.0410071208.12471c7d@posting.google.com...

> The 2002 standard has "standard" arithmetic with floating-point
> conversions for intermediate values to keep accuracy at a high level,
> but there is an efficiency premium to be paid.

Well, maybe, but I'm not convinced that things are going to stay that way
for very long.

The proposed 128-bit IEEE 754 floating-point formats provide more precision
than the 2002 COBOL standard requires, and could at least theoretically be
used as an "infrastructure" for the Standard Intermediate Data Item.

Once that proposed draft is promulgated as a standard -- I hear that's
expected in 2005 -- I'd expect it wouldn't take long for hardware support
for arithmetic in those formats to appear.

And once that happens, I think the [in]efficiency premium associated with
Standard Arithmetic would be *significantly* reduced.

My personal opinion is that COBOL ought to get out of the floating-point
arithmetic standards definition business altogether and rely on what ISO
does on the subject -- which will almost certainly be to adopt the IEEE
proposal.  The current IEEE 754 is inadequate for COBOL's needs, but the
draft for the next version looks very promising to me.

Changes in this area won't likely make it into the proposed 2008 COBOL
standard, but I suspect the US delegation will be raising this point at the
upcoming WG4 meeting as a possibility for an amendment or revision to that
proposed standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T16:54:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ggoam09lapt8kktqv6c3rss13iqpbfum2t@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com>`

```
On 7 Oct 2004 00:01:35 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>'percentage' is, say, PIC S9(m)V99.

Assuming intermediates are fixed-point ..
The number of digits left of decimal is easy for the compiler to
determine, but the number of digits to the right is indeterminate. It
is often infinite. For this reason, it is better to multiply first and
divide second.
```

On Thu, 7 Oct 2004 11:02:58 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message

…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>arithmetic expressions?

The circumstances present on most computers:

1. The hardware supplies high-speed fixed-point arithmetic
instructions. 

2. Floating point is IEEE-754 or other binary, which cannot represent
decimal numbers exactly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T12:01:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck43qv$2bj4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <ggoam09lapt8kktqv6c3rss13iqpbfum2t@4ax.com> <ck3tt2$27h4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <970bm0t4v4qp9sfvvtj87eh1gppgomjkvp@4ax.com> <ck40ci$2976$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <s82bm0pnck2v1g43l0orcudgacgcao1tg9@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:s82bm0pnck2v1g43l0orcudgacgcao1tg9@4ax.com...

> The circumstances present on most computers:
>
> 1. The hardware supplies high-speed fixed-point arithmetic
> instructions.

I can't speak to "most computers", but an ALU is an ALU, and the ones I know
about do an ADD just as fast as they do an ADD.  They might differ in speed
if one of the arguments is a double instead of a single, but the speed
doesn't differ depending on whether or not an argument has a nonzero
exponents.

More importantly:

> 2. Floating point is IEEE-754 or other binary, which cannot represent
> decimal numbers exactly.

Are you *truly* asserting that floating-point representations (IEEE or
otherwise) *cannot* represent decimal numbers exactly?

I was under the distinct impression that (using arbitrary binary
floating-point representation for illustrative purposes) if the number of
trailing zero bits in the binary mantissa exceeds the value of the binary
exponent the value can safely be considered an exact representation of an
integer.

In the case of a particular octal-based floating-point format of my
acquaintance I count thirteen different *exact* single-precision
representations and twenty-six *exact* double-precision representations for
the integer value

1.0E0 ** 1.0E0 may not prove exactly equal to 1 in your universe, but it
does in mine.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T16:26:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck4jas$2ln9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <ggoam09lapt8kktqv6c3rss13iqpbfum2t@4ax.com> <ck3tt2$27h4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <970bm0t4v4qp9sfvvtj87eh1gppgomjkvp@4ax.com> <ck40ci$2976$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <s82bm0pnck2v1g43l0orcudgacgcao1tg9@4ax.com> <ck43qv$2bj4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tscbm0p5bjlv0nseiuo2mhj3j2pollr9in@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:tscbm0p5bjlv0nseiuo2mhj3j2pollr9in@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 7 Oct 2004 12:01:50 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://babbage.cs.qc.edu/courses/cs341/IEEE-754.html

"Magnitude of the binary exponent" I think should cover it.

Your assertion is "Floating point is IEEE-754 or other binary, which cannot
represent
decimal numbers exactly."

Is that *really* what you meant?

Or did you mean something else, like "floating point representations in
which the mantissa is expresseed in a radix other than 10 and/or the
exponent is expressed in a radix other than 10 (for example the IEEE 754
floating-point formats) cannot always represent rational but noninteger
decimal numbers exactly"?

Or do you believe the terms "decimal number", "decimal fraction" and
"decimal noninteger" are all synonymous?   If so, I think you're mistaken.

Last time I looked, 19.00 is a *decimal* number, and offhand I can't think
of a functioning *floating-point* format that couldn't represent it
*exactly* ...

     -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T19:10:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<owg9d.2258651$ic1.230601@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <ggoam09lapt8kktqv6c3rss13iqpbfum2t@4ax.com> <ck3tt2$27h4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <7c4bm0lh2p2unikdjihuql7s29ar6apdd7@4ax.com>`

```
Jeff,
  Did you MISS the part that said *decimal* floating-point?   This has little 
(if any) to do with the "traditional" binary or "hexadecimal" floating-point 
that many/most computer people are familiar with from the past.

It is interesting to note that *decimal* floating-point is being "added" to 
international Standards for *non* COBOL and, as such, it is possible (likely?) 
that such *may* be added to hardware in the relatively near future - and hence, 
COBOL "ARITHMETIC IS STANDARD" support *may* actually perform well (when/if it 
is implemented where there is hardware support for such).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T12:10:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck44b3$2bsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <ggoam09lapt8kktqv6c3rss13iqpbfum2t@4ax.com> <ck3tt2$27h4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <7c4bm0lh2p2unikdjihuql7s29ar6apdd7@4ax.com>`

```
> What bloody lunatic saw fit to introduce floating-point intermediates
> into a fixed-point *Business* language?

What our compiler does is retrieve all arithmetic data as integers and apply
the appropriate scale factors after computing an intermediate result to the
maximum precision the hardware allows.  Dividing an integer by an integer
may or may not produce an *integer* result.

The "floating-point" intermediates in COBOL are *decimal* and have
carefully-defined rounding rules.   I am unconvinced as yet that specifying
that all arithmetic expressions be evaluated as if all the operands were
specified so as to maintain a mantissa precision of 32 decimal digits
(instead of the unspecified behavior and the 18 digits provided for in '85
COBOL) were the ravings of a "bloody lunatic".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T14:46:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck4dg9$2i3a$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <ggoam09lapt8kktqv6c3rss13iqpbfum2t@4ax.com> <ck3tt2$27h4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <7c4bm0lh2p2unikdjihuql7s29ar6apdd7@4ax.com> <ck44b3$2bsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tfcbm050608pn546bbdv9u7nfone43qroc@4ax.com>`

```
"Jeff York" <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote in message
news:tfcbm050608pn546bbdv9u7nfone43qroc@4ax.com...

> To you and Bill K..  I was so busy being shocked by the "floating
> point" that I didn't take in the "decimal"..
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> And *very* relieved..  :-)

I'd go one step further (see another part of this thread):  The proposed
IEEE 754 revision provides for two basic 128-bit floating-point formats, one
binary, one decimal.   Both have sufficient mantissa precision and exponent
range to exceed the requirements of SIDI's.

And I believe, presuming hardware support, the rounding rule differences
between even the BINARY128 format and the SIDI could be provided for in the
object code without all that much cost.

Using DECIMAL128 as a structure underlying the SIDI would require even less
"adjusting" code, but it looks to me like the format would require rather
more complex hardware for arithmetic and conversion to "more conventional"
representations (it uses 10 bits for each 3 decimal digits).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'percentage' is, say, PIC S9(m)V99.

    Do any of the offending compilers (from a  microfocus point of view)
offer the
ability to use maximum size intermediate results, for people that want to
have
some control over precision.

    When a compute always uses a maximum length intermediate result,
truncation (most of it) takes place only during the final assign.

    This decision - the decision to truncate intermediate results to the
pictures of input variables sounds very unwise to me.  It can very
unexpected behavior.

    This would seem to be (in the absence of a compiler option to allow
maximum length intermediates) be the greatest single obstacle to porting to
and from a mainframe.

    I took a quick look at the docs (NetX 3.1) and I do not see any
provision for emulating the above behavior.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T13:10:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 06 Oct 2004 17:40:16 -0400, Donald Tees
> <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> amount-one)/(this + that). Your way loses precision.

Mr Wagner, this seems to be a case where you have
substituted opinion for fact. It is not possible to know
whether there will be any loss of precision without
knowing the pictures of the variables involved, how
the compiler determines the size of intermediate
results from those pictures, and how many guard
digits the compiler will supply to protect against loss
of precision.

I understand 'incorrect' to be 'not correct as to fact';
yet, the facts necessary to claim 'That's incorrect' are
not present.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T18:18:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 7 Oct 2004 13:10:31 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>not present.

Using D=A*B/C as an example, if A has size a and B has size b, the
compiler will supply an intermediate with size a+b so no left digits
are lost. However, in D=A*(B/C), the intermediate for the division
would have to hold a+d digits right of decimal point. Will the
compiler be smart enough to figure that out? I wouldn't count on it.
Since the number of right digits is potentially infinite, there is a
good chance the compiler will use an arbitrary 'standard' precision ..
such as the size of a register. 

In the best case, both will produce the same answer. In the worst
case, the first will be more accurate. In no case will the second be
more accurate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

  "If A has size a and B has size b, the  compiler will supply an intermediate 
with size a+b so no left digits  are lost."

That is neither a requirement nor is it actually done by all (Most? Many?) 
compilers.  Just check out the ARITHMETIC compiler directive in Micro Focus for 
all the options available *and* the default behavior (old and current releases).
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T22:46:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u3fbm01qv1p5pmh4fcd7lhuot9plv0up3o@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <ltg9d.2258556$ic1.230379@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Thu, 07 Oct 2004 19:06:57 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
>news:0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>all the options available *and* the default behavior (old and current releases).

The description of ARITHMETIC says five IBM mainframe compilers do
exactly what I said, and provides an option to duplicate that
behavior. 

The default is 40 digit decimal floating-point, which is essentially
Standard Arithmetic. I did not know that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T15:17:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 7 Oct 2004 13:10:31 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiler be smart enough to figure that out? I wouldn't count on it.

Mr Wagner, do you mean, "Will the compiler designer be
smart enough to use the picture clauses to determine how
large a decimal fraction will be required?" It seems to me
that you may count on such things, regularly.

Understand that what you present here is not the same form
as that you called 'incorrect'. My complaint was not that the
arrangement of an expression has no effect on accuracy; but
only that there was not enough information, in the original
example, for you to have concluded 'That's incorrect'.

> Since the number of right digits is potentially infinite, there is a
> good chance the compiler will use an arbitrary 'standard' precision ..
> such as the size of a register.

Even if the number of digits to the right of a result is inifinite,
there is a very good chance the compiler will use the picture
clause to provide the requested precision. Thus precision
depends upon the programmer.

> In the best case, both will produce the same answer. In the worst
> case, the first will be more accurate. In no case will the second be
> more accurate.

In the best case, the progammer will exercise good
judgment, in selecting picture clauses and in sequencing
operations, to provide accurate results.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T22:46:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 7 Oct 2004 15:17:42 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>> In the best case, both will produce the same answer. In the worst
>> case, the first will be more accurate. In no case will the second be
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>operations, to provide accurate results.

I've seen the result of statements like A/B*100 -- percentages in the
form zzz.99 where one or two rightmost digits are zero. After changing
it to A*100/B, the rightmost digits appeared. 

So my suggestion IS the exercise of good judgment. If it wasn't
necessary, it didn't cost anything.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T21:28:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mbrdclfieu933@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com> <lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 7 Oct 2004 15:17:42 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> So my suggestion IS the exercise of good judgment.
[snip]

Is it really, Mr Wagner? Consider that the form

    compute percentage = <ratio> * 100

may be readily understood. However, because this did not
work as expected, you changed it into something, perhaps,
less readable. Is that correct?

One could have provided the dividend of the ratio with,
say, two additional places for the decimal fraction and
amounts for that dividend could be moved to or summed
in that field before calculating the percentage. Would that
not have maintained the readability of ratio times 100?

Alternatively, one could have calculated the ratio before
calculating the percentage. Would that also not have
maintained the readability of ratio times 100?

Mr Wagner, is not the writing of readable, working code
the realization of good judgment? Or, do you disagree
with the opinion 'style should always lean toward
readability' also expressed by Mr Tees?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T04:30:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com> <lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com> <10mbrdclfieu933@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 7 Oct 2004 21:28:47 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>less readable. Is that correct?

Only the innumerate would find it less readable. Tenth-grade algebra
taught us that (A/B)*100 is equivalent to (A*100)/B. Do your employers
hire high-school dropouts? (If they do, I'm available.)

>One could have provided the dividend of the ratio with,
>say, two additional places for the decimal fraction and
>amounts for that dividend could be moved to or summed
>in that field before calculating the percentage. Would that
>not have maintained the readability of ratio times 100?

Playing with pictures in the unfounded hope they will affect the
precision of intermediates strikes me as unprofessional. To what end?
Because you had trouble with high-school algebra?

>Alternatively, one could have calculated the ratio before
>calculating the percentage. Would that also not have
>maintained the readability of ratio times 100?

Seperate calculations of intermediates DO hurt readability.

>Mr Wagner, is not the writing of readable, working code
>the realization of good judgment? Or, do you disagree
>with the opinion 'style should always lean toward
>readability' also expressed by Mr Tees?

I write code that's very readable .. to a competent reader. I don't
target readership to the LCD .. because that impairs readibility to
competent people. 

Imagine what the Glashow-Salam-Wienberg GUT (theory of physics) would
look like if it had to be understandable to Sesame Street viewers or
mainframe programmers.
```

On  8-Oct-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

>    I wouldn't compute the percentage (multiplying the factoir by 100)
> at all. Percent is only an aid to the human reader, since "33%" is
> easier to express than 0,33.

When I was a kid, I was confused by the "formula" to created percentage that
included "multiply by 100".    That was because I already implicitly removed the
decimal point.

33% is also more clear when we are posting across cultures.   It still causes me
to double-take when I see a comma used as the decimal, and as a child I had
never heard of it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T15:44:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vcbdm0tn7t5n7c5cc11d085hqgategi9q3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com> <10mbrdclfieu933@corp.supernews.com> <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com> <ck5m03$gjt$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 8 Oct 2004 05:17:55 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

>>I write code that's very readable .. to a competent reader.
>
>What an unusual statement... outside of its circular nature, Mr Wagner, do 
>you know of *anyone* who would state, say ... 'I write code that's very readable to 
>an incompetent reader'?

Such statements frequently appear in Cobol discussions. For
politeness, the incompetent reader is called entry-level programmer or
maintenance programmer. 

For instance, we are told to avoid complex conditionals .. not because
the speaker has trouble with logic, heaven forbid, rather for the
benefit of maintenance programmers. Instead of putting all logic in
one statement, we are advised to sprinkle it throughout the program.
The act of sprinkling somehow simplifies the complex conditional.

>>I don't
>>target readership to the LCD .. because that impairs readibility to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>criteria which determine competence?

In measures of productivity and reliability.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T12:02:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck6dmt$kpr$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com> <ck5m03$gjt$1@panix5.panix.com> <vcbdm0tn7t5n7c5cc11d085hqgategi9q3@4ax.com>`

```
In article <vcbdm0tn7t5n7c5cc11d085hqgategi9q3@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
>On 8 Oct 2004 05:17:55 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>maintenance programmer. 

Mr Wagner, each of these examples you give indicates a level of competence 
(entry-level or maintenance-level), not a lack of competence.

>
>For instance, we are told to avoid complex conditionals .. not because
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>The act of sprinkling somehow simplifies the complex conditional.

Mr Wagner, separating the components of a complex condition is often 
called simplifying it; likewise, it is often said that KISS is a superior 
communication strategy than 'look, Ma, I'm a programmer!'.  Do you 
believe, as did Newton, that matters should be kept to a certain level of 
complexity in order to keep out 'the unworthy'?

>
>>>I don't
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>In measures of productivity and reliability.

There are a variety of those, Mr Wagner; might you be so kind as to detail 
the methods you find applicable?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-09T19:27:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ssbgm0tedfjdje65sbi3fgb0b7mf7fijdi@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com> <ck5m03$gjt$1@panix5.panix.com> <vcbdm0tn7t5n7c5cc11d085hqgategi9q3@4ax.com> <ck6dmt$kpr$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 8 Oct 2004 12:02:37 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <vcbdm0tn7t5n7c5cc11d085hqgategi9q3@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>(entry-level or maintenance-level), not a lack of competence.

The qualifier was used to indicate low competence.

>>For instance, we are told to avoid complex conditionals .. not because
>>the speaker has trouble with logic, heaven forbid, rather for the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>complexity in order to keep out 'the unworthy'?

I believe that a program evaluating 'a and (b or c)' ONE WAY OR
ANOTHER is clearer when the conditions are expressed in one statement
rather than distributed. For example, which of these is clearer?

e = m * c**2

move m to e
if c not equal to zero
   perform factor-in-c 2 times
end-if
...
factor-in-c.
  multiply c by e.

>>>>I don't
>>>>target readership to the LCD .. because that impairs readibility to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>the methods you find applicable?

productivity: actual / estimated (time to complete)

reliability:  actual / shop average (problem reports)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-12T08:57:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ckgusj$1spj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <8acim0li65l1q1arcm9dvcki6148onujj4@4ax.com> <ckbcqu$835$1@panix5.panix.com> <q98jm0ho6darq1tdf7ur63pa18k8tqlo66@4ax.com> <ckccgp$jbn$1@panix5.panix.com> <43rjm0d75jtgqmriq0tc4ffbrltsbunauv@4ax.com> <ckep5q$dt8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dl3mm011cq6ctjpl1micqvtfccmklqf8ah@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:dl3mm011cq6ctjpl1micqvtfccmklqf8ah@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 11 Oct 2004 13:07:22 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> hour or two celebrating victory. Putting it bluntly, I was drunk.

So am I to understand that being drunk is a valid reason to behave badly?

In most locations I've been, a person can be presumed responsible for his
behavior while "impaired" by alcohol in exactly the same fashion and to the
same degree as if that behavior had been premeditated.

As for me, I could find lots of excuses to get drunk -- either to celebrate
a victory or to drown my sorrows over a defeat -- as I go about my life.
But what I have found is that the anaesthetic effects of alcohol aren't
really a very good idea for me.

If I post in this or any other forum I must presume that the people who sign
my paychecks that pay for food and clothing for myself, my wife and our
foster daughter are reading my posts, and some modicum of civility is
appropriate in that context.

And in that context, I have to wonder about the fundamental wisdom not only
of the attack on civility in the earlier post but also of the subsequent
explanation that you were posting on a public forum *while drunk* when you
wrote it.   "Officer, I didn't mean to run over that baby carriage.  I
wouldn't have done it if I hadn't been drunk."   Oh, goody.  That makes
*everybody* feel better.

We've talked a lot about the Brooklyn Bridge defense lately.  How about the
"Geraldine" defense?  The "Twinkie" defense?  Or, with apologies to the late
William Wilson, the "John Barleycorn" defense?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
