- **In-Reply-To:** `<ckgusj$1spj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <8acim0li65l1q1arcm9dvcki6148onujj4@4ax.com> <ckbcqu$835$1@panix5.panix.com> <q98jm0ho6darq1tdf7ur63pa18k8tqlo66@4ax.com> <ckccgp$jbn$1@panix5.panix.com> <43rjm0d75jtgqmriq0tc4ffbrltsbunauv@4ax.com> <ckep5q$dt8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dl3mm011cq6ctjpl1micqvtfccmklqf8ah@4ax.com> <ckgusj$1spj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> And in that context, I have to wonder about the fundamental wisdom not only
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> *everybody* feel better.

I think the harm done is on a slightly smaller scale...  There's no DWP 
because you can't harm human life through Usenet (directly, the way you 
can with a car).  You can't really expect us to believe this is the same 
thing.  (Did I miss a tongue-in-cheek here?)

I remember the first time I mixed beer, gin & juice, and Zima on the 
same night - I made some pretty interesting posts in the FidoNet echoes 
that night!  A few other times, I posted stuff while inebriated (though 
it took quite a bit before I felt anything, so it didn't happen often - 
Lack of funding can severely limit consumption!)  Most just got me a few 
laughs, though - especially the one after I found the 32oz. beer bottles...

"Hey - did you guys know they make REALLY BIG beers?!?!"

(oh to be young and irresponsible again....)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-13T09:01:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ckjjgv$l1i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <8acim0li65l1q1arcm9dvcki6148onujj4@4ax.com> <ckbcqu$835$1@panix5.panix.com> <q98jm0ho6darq1tdf7ur63pa18k8tqlo66@4ax.com> <ckccgp$jbn$1@panix5.panix.com> <43rjm0d75jtgqmriq0tc4ffbrltsbunauv@4ax.com> <ckep5q$dt8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dl3mm011cq6ctjpl1micqvtfccmklqf8ah@4ax.com> <ckgusj$1spj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fc%ad.42562$VJ2.36916@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:fc%ad.42562$VJ2.36916@fe40.usenetserver.com...

> I think the harm done is on a slightly smaller scale...  There's no DWP
> because you can't harm human life through Usenet (directly, the way you
> can with a car).  You can't really expect us to believe this is the same
> thing.  (Did I miss a tongue-in-cheek here?)

Not really tongue-in-cheek, more reductio ad absurdum.

Let's try another, slightly less inflammatory tack:

If somebody throws up on my lap because he's having a heart attack, I'd be
inclined to overlook it.

If somebody throws up on my lap because he's too drunk to make the decision
to turn his head the other way so his vomitus landed on the floor, or in the
nearest houseplant, I wouldn't be so inclined to overlook it as wholly
accidental.

I grant that if a person vomits on my lap because he is too drunk to do
otherwise is not quite as directly or deliberately offensive or aggressive
an act as forcing himself to vomit on my lap by tickling the back of his
throat with a finger, but I contend that throwing up on my lap would be a
*direct* consequence of a *deliberate* act on his part -- namely the
decision to place himself in that position, by the very act of drinking that
much.

I also grant that, if drinking himself into that level of stupor is a
pattern in his life, whatever the reasons, the deliberate act in question
might be the decision to take that *first* drink -- as many a member of
Alcoholics Anonymous will tell you, for the alcoholic, it's not the *last*
drink that gets you drunk, it's the *first* one.

In either case, the outcome -- a puddle of puke in my lap -- is a direct,
and unwelcome, consequence of that person's decisions.

> (oh to be young and irresponsible again....)

Yeah, well, welcome to adulthood.  And civilization.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-13T17:33:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ckjoti$njj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <8acim0li65l1q1arcm9dvcki6148onujj4@4ax.com> <ckbcqu$835$1@panix5.panix.com> <q98jm0ho6darq1tdf7ur63pa18k8tqlo66@4ax.com> <ckccgp$jbn$1@panix5.panix.com> <43rjm0d75jtgqmriq0tc4ffbrltsbunauv@4ax.com> <ckep5q$dt8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dl3mm011cq6ctjpl1micqvtfccmklqf8ah@4ax.com> <ckgusj$1spj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fc%ad.42562$VJ2.36916@fe40.usenetserver.com> <ckjjgv$l1i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On 13-Oct-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> If somebody throws up on my lap because he's too drunk to make the decision
> to turn his head the other way so his vomitus landed on the floor, or in the
> nearest houseplant, I wouldn't be so inclined to overlook it as wholly
> accidental.

There's been a change in society over the last few centuries.   People have
become more willing to look at intent than before.   That's why we are more
willing to punish someone who murdered someone for the insurance policy than
someone who murdered someone during road rage.

But the latter is a much greater danger to the rest of us.

Victims are just as dead either way.

In 1980 I heard a Catholic Bishop on the radio lament the attitude that "good
Catholic girls" have where they don't use birth control because it is a planned
sin, but are willing to have abortions because it was a "backed in the corner"
sin.

I know people allow themselves to become angry or to become drunk, or to allow
whatever gives them an excuse for not being in control.   They get taught that
from a young age that it doesn't count so much.   It counts to the victims.
```

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

I'll buy that.  :)

> I grant that if a person vomits on my lap because he is too drunk to do
> otherwise is not quite as directly or deliberately offensive or aggressive
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> and unwelcome, consequence of that person's decisions.

True.  But this is (to my recollection) Mr. Wagner's first admission 
that he posted inaccurate information because he was inebriated.  I just 
didn't take it to the "alcoholic" extent that you did.  In fact, because 
it was isolated, one could make the argument that he consumed more than 
he could handle because he doesn't do it often, thereby reducing his 
tolerance.

>>(oh to be young and irresponsible again....)
> 
> 
> Yeah, well, welcome to adulthood.  And civilization.

heh heh - I guess that came across wrong.  (No, I wasn't drunk...  ;> )

Even if I were 21 again, knowing what I know now, I wouldn't do many of 
the things I did when I was 21.  I guess I was just wistfully 
remembering the days when I didn't have the responsibilities I do now - 
so I had the ability to do stupid things and not really affect anyone 
but myself.  :)
```

True.  Just today, the kids got out of school at noon, so I took my 
lunch and drove out with my wife to pick them up.  We pull up at 11:48, 
and she says "We're really early."  I said, "We can just sit here for a 
few minutes," and she replied "That's boring."

After the morning I had, I was looking forward to sitting still for a 
few minutes without a keyboard in front of me, and a phone to my left. 
:)  How opinions change...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10md529rkivjp5a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com> <lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com> <10mbrdclfieu933@corp.supernews.com> <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 7 Oct 2004 21:28:47 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> hire high-school dropouts? (If they do, I'm available.)

Mr Wagner, e equal c-squared m doesn't seem as
familiar as the original and neither does rat times 100
divided by io.

> >One could have provided the dividend of the ratio with,
> >say, two additional places for the decimal fraction and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Because you had trouble with high-school algebra?

Mr Wagner, it is common knowledge that some compilers
for IBM mainframes use the picture clause to determine
the precision to use for intermediates and that manipulating
the picture clauses may be done to achieve the desired
precision. [My trouble was with high-school English; just
couldn't write on demand.]

> >Alternatively, one could have calculated the ratio before
> >calculating the percentage. Would that also not have
> >maintained the readability of ratio times 100?
>
> Seperate calculations of intermediates DO hurt readability.

Yet, there are times when instructions, even algorithms,
are presented as separate steps to improve clarity and
understanding and, dare I say, improve readability.

> >Mr Wagner, is not the writing of readable, working code
> >the realization of good judgment? Or, do you disagree
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> competent people.

Ah, like the two-year progammer, who might have to
maintain what you wrote.

> Imagine what the Glashow-Salam-Wienberg GUT (theory of physics) would
> look like if it had to be understandable to Sesame Street viewers or
> mainframe programmers.

That would be difficult for me to imagine, Mr Wagner,
since I have no knowledge of it. It is my gut feeling that
I might find it esoteric; though not being a Sesame Street
viewer nor a mainframe programmer, it is not clear that
such a translation would be of benefit, either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T11:07:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ck6afa$dfm$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <10md529rkivjp5a@corp.supernews.com> <ck64jh$3ig$1@panix5.panix.com> <10mdael1k8o8761@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <10mdael1k8o8761@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:ck64jh$3ig$1@panix5.panix.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>the equation; but cannot place the source.

My error and apologies... gotta love this Web-thingie.  From:

http://www.sas.org/E-Bulletin/2002-02-15/features3/body.html

--begin quoted text:

If a body gives off the energy L in the form of radiation, its mass 
diminishes by L/c�. The fact that the energy withdrawn from the body 
becomes energy of radiation evidently makes no difference, so that we are 
led to the more general conclusion that

The mass of a body is a measure of its energy-content; if the energy 
changes by L, the mass changes in the same sense by L/9 � 1020 (ed. note: 
1020 = ten to the twentieth power, no superscripts here), the energy being 
measured in ergs, and the mass in grammes.

It is not impossible that with bodies whose energy-content is variable to 
a high degree (e.g. with radium salts) the theory may be successfully put 
to the test.

--end quoted text

... so it was m = L/c-squared.

I remember when I first read this that parenthetic note of '(e.g. with 
radium salts)' gave me goose-bumps.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T15:23:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mdqd96s1nkrb7@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com> <lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com> <10mbrdclfieu933@corp.supernews.com> <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com> <10md529rkivjp5a@corp.supernews.com> <b7cdm05skqn162locclqd0fq0kkl1cltd8@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:b7cdm05skqn162locclqd0fq0kkl1cltd8@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 8 Oct 2004 09:19:55 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 100 * rat / io

Percentage is also known as rate per hundred where rat / io
is the gross rate, also ppm is rate per million. Consider the
following:

    compute accepted-percentage = accepted-count / total-count
        * 100
    compute reject-rate-ppm = rejected-count / total-count
        * 1000 * 1000

Backward making things clear not always is; but these examples
place the terms into the same sequence as may be expected by
their definitions. Mr Wagner, I do see a difference.

[snip]
> >> I write code that's very readable .. to a competent reader. I don't
> >> target readership to the LCD .. because that impairs readibility to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> find it hard to argue against that.

Mr Wagner, a programer, expressing complex business
rules as a series of simple, clear steps, may communicate
information to another programmer, new to the business;
thus helping that programmer to become more productive,
faster. On the other hand, a progammer using complex
and obscure code to express those rules may communicate
nothing, at least, nothing helpful.

It seems to me, Mr Wagner, that I am not defending bad
programming; but rather, qualifying what I consider to be
good programming.

[qualify: to provide with proper and necessary skills,
knowledge, credentials, etc.; make competent. -- RHCD]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T17:10:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wmD9d.36030$HO1.1696410@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10mdqd96s1nkrb7@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com> <lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com> <10mbrdclfieu933@corp.supernews.com> <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com> <10md529rkivjp5a@corp.supernews.com> <b7cdm05skqn162locclqd0fq0kkl1cltd8@4ax.com> <10mdqd96s1nkrb7@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> Mr Wagner, a programer, expressing complex business
> rules as a series of simple, clear steps, may communicate
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Aye.  I was asked, politely, by a teacher of beginers, whether 
professionals used one style or another. I responded in kind, with two 
very simplistic examples. I did not go into great detail about the 
merits of the two because that was not part of the question. The 
question was what do you base the choice on?  The answer was 
readability. Secondarily to that, that I personally thought readablility 
was largely a matter of the number of elements involved.

*That* was the purpose of the example, and there have been few germaine 
comments since. We sound like a bounch of damned old women bickering on 
a friday afternoon.  Since it is 5:25, I am going to light a second 
dooby, and sign out.

Donald
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6rcgm0tgc3ajohj8ujdg9hht1jjsr5jffg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com> <lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com> <10mbrdclfieu933@corp.supernews.com> <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com> <10md529rkivjp5a@corp.supernews.com> <b7cdm05skqn162locclqd0fq0kkl1cltd8@4ax.com> <10mdqd96s1nkrb7@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Fri, 8 Oct 2004 15:23:25 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

>> >Mr Wagner, e equal c-squared m doesn't seem as
>> >familiar as the original and neither does rat times 100
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>their definitions. Mr Wagner, I do see a difference.

You think the rules of English are superior to the rules of
mathematics. This deficiency is understandable when it comes to logic,
because most of us weren't taught to symbolically manipulate logical
statements. It is inexcusable in the case of numeric computations,
because nearly ALL of us were taught high-school algebra. Nowadays,
they teach albegra in elementary school.

>> >> I write code that's very readable .. to a competent reader. I don't
>> >> target readership to the LCD .. because that impairs readibility to
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>nothing, at least, nothing helpful.

That's what comments are for. If you think business rules are too
complex for another to understand, explain them in comments.

>It seems to me, Mr Wagner, that I am not defending bad
>programming; but rather, qualifying what I consider to be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>knowledge, credentials, etc.; make competent. -- RHCD]

I don't care what the Red Hat Certified Developer program says;
Merriam-Webster defines qualify as "3b (1) : to declare competent or
adequate : CERTIFY". That's accomplished by testing, not by a certain
programming style.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-09T18:41:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mgqaurjc9h10c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com> <10mb5l93nic801@corp.supernews.com> <lofbm0pfj836r3omprm19o0pq97tbiq9ki@4ax.com> <10mbrdclfieu933@corp.supernews.com> <2r5cm0pd6ut8m2c34l5t0kung40qqm4kr7@4ax.com> <10md529rkivjp5a@corp.supernews.com> <b7cdm05skqn162locclqd0fq0kkl1cltd8@4ax.com> <10mdqd96s1nkrb7@corp.supernews.com> <6rcgm0tgc3ajohj8ujdg9hht1jjsr5jffg@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6rcgm0tgc3ajohj8ujdg9hht1jjsr5jffg@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 8 Oct 2004 15:23:25 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> they teach albegra in elementary school.

Mr Wagner, I think that, among English speaking people
writing in an English-like language, some attention to an
English style of presentation is appropriate. And, when
the code is a translation of a specification written in
English, I think it is inappropriate to deviate far from what
was written. Furthermore, if these same English speaking
people, think in English when writing such code, it is more
likely that others of the same background will understand
what was written.

Now, substitute Klingon, or any other language, for English
and I believe the same fundemental truth will hold.

> >> >> I write code that's very readable .. to a competent reader. I don't
> >> >> target readership to the LCD .. because that impairs readibility to
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> complex for another to understand, explain them in comments.

And, if, as a result of one's personality, education, etc.,
one does not find complexity on which to comment,
then comments will be absent. However, a requirement
for 'simple, clear steps' supplemented by code review
by one's peers may go a long way toward preventing
the 'complex and obscure code' that communicates
nothing helpful.

> >It seems to me, Mr Wagner, that I am not defending bad
> >programming; but rather, qualifying what I consider to be
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming style.

Interestingly, I was asked to review code samples
provided by applicants for a position as a C programmer.
The bulk of my critique was based on style, in particular,
the use of C idioms, which tend to indicate experience
with C. I also looked at partitioning of code among
functions, interfaces, the use of global variables, etc.,
each of which may be seen as style. I found it easy to
distinguish those with Pascal training and those with little
experience with C. I never talked to any of the applicants.
I was later told that one hapless soul, after being told of
my comments, blurted out, "So that's why it doesn't work!"

Mr Wagner, style is not everything, but it does reveal a
lot about knowledge and experience with the language
and with programming, in general, and is an indicator of
competence.
```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6d9hm01ueg2vftpbd42vhe8f8jhfdf2gev@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 9 Oct 2004 18:41:09 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

[snip]
> >>  It is inexcusable in the case of numeric computations,
> >> because nearly ALL of us were taught high-school algebra. Nowadays,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> English when dealing with mathematical computations.

Mr Wagner, would that not lead to such useless
duplication as:

* Let P be the item-price
* Let Q be the item-quantity
* Let E be the extended-price
* Let R be the discount-rate-percent
* Let D be the discount-price
move item-price to D
move item-quantity to Q
move discount-rate-percent to R
compute E rounded = P * Q
compute D rounded = E * R / 100
move E to extended-price
move D to discount-price

algebra 1 : a generalization of arithmetic in which letters
representing numbers are combined according to the
rules of arithmetic (www.m-w.com)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T17:12:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410071612.8555a78@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <0u0bm0pcv8m26oqsnuj0egua51buv4id6b@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >I understand 'incorrect' to be 'not correct as to fact';
> >yet, the facts necessary to claim 'That's incorrect' are
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> are lost.

Up to the maximum that the compiler will assign for that intermediate
result, which may be, for example, 18 digits.  If the result of the
multiplication exceeds that number of digits then the answer will be
completely wrong and ON SIZE ERROR will be taken:

"""The Micro Focus COBOL system can evaluate each intermediate result
using up to 18 integer and 18 decimal places. If you select the
ARITHMETIC"MF" Compiler directive, intermediate results are not
truncated, and thus this maximum accuracy is obtained. In this
instance, each intermediate result has a picture of 9(18)V9(18)."""

> However, in D=A*(B/C), the intermediate for the division
> would have to hold a+d digits right of decimal point. Will the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> such as the size of a register. 

Which probably will make no difference to the actual answer which
depends on the precision required in the final assignment.

> In the best case, both will produce the same answer. In the worst
> case, the first will be more accurate. In no case will the second be
> more accurate.

In the worst case for the first the answer will be completely wrong.

In the worst case for the second there will be a loss of 10 ^ -18 or
so.

The _correct_ mechanism to use depends entirely on the scale of the
numbers involved and not on some religious pontification.
```

>>
>> That's incorrect. It should be compute percentage = (100*
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> not present.

The assertion regarding precision has nothing to do with pictures, 
floating-point, or anything even remotely associated with COBOL.

The fact that (A/B) x C is inferior to (A x B) / C is pure number theory.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T18:09:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mbfn7g0j7if2f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <2KOdndEfkorMKfjcRVn-pA@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:2KOdndEfkorMKfjcRVn-pA@giganews.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> The fact that (A/B) x C is inferior to (A x B) / C is pure number theory.

Mr Mouse, I recall seeing a poster of Bertrand Russell
with the quote (as I recall it): "There ought to be a world,
a mathematician's paradise, where everthing works in
practice as it does in theory."

What Mr Tees presented was a case where a decimal
fraction was multiplied by 100. If the intermediate result
(the decimal fraction) has at least two fractional digits
more than the number of fractional digits in the picture
for 'percentage', there will be no loss of accuracy nor
of precision. It works in practice and pure number
theory be damned!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T06:26:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g-SdndJtpZbv5PvcRVn-qQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <2KOdndEfkorMKfjcRVn-pA@giganews.com> <10mbfn7g0j7if2f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
> news:2KOdndEfkorMKfjcRVn-pA@giganews.com...
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> theory be damned!

Whatever.

(1 / 3) x 3 = .3 x 3 = .90
(1 x 3) / 3 = 3 / 3 = 1.0

Notice the precision of a division operation can never be greater than the 
precision of the least significant operand. Multiplication adds 1 digit of 
precision to the most significant number of digits.

10% inaccuracy - due to precision loss - is good enough for government work.

And, no, expanding 1/3 to .3333333... then rounding is NOT good math (it's 
more like history, cello, or potato).

As to the above's applicability to COBOL, note that if the variables just 
happen to be defined as integers, you are screwed, blued, and tatooed in the 
first example but golden in the second.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T08:36:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10md2gp2it1gl75@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <2KOdndEfkorMKfjcRVn-pA@giganews.com> <10mbfn7g0j7if2f@corp.supernews.com> <g-SdndJtpZbv5PvcRVn-qQ@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:g-SdndJtpZbv5PvcRVn-qQ@giganews.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> precision to the most significant number of digits.

Mr Mouse, 'x 3' is not 'x 100'. With enough guard
digits, either through extending picture clauses (IBM,
for example, or the compiler always using extended
precision (Micro Focus, for example), the result of
rearranging the expression is the same.

Given a picture of zzz.99 for percentage and assuming
that '1' and '3' are the values of other variables.

1 / 3 * 100 =  33.33, and
1 * 100 / 3 =  33.33.

It is not number theory, it happens to be the way COBOL
compilers work, in my experience or to the best of my
knowledge.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <2KOdndEfkorMKfjcRVn-pA@giganews.com> <10mbfn7g0j7if2f@corp.supernews.com> <g-SdndJtpZbv5PvcRVn-qQ@giganews.com> <10md2gp2it1gl75@corp.supernews.com>`

Rick Smith wrote:
>>
>> Whatever.
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> knowledge.

You would require infinitely many "guard" digits (whatever they are).

By using as many artificial digits of precision, you can come up with almost 
anything. 1 / 3 * 100 is NOT 33.33. With at most two digits of precision, 
the only meaningful answer is "30" (but more likely, zero).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T16:21:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mdtolrjgmlp25@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <2KOdndEfkorMKfjcRVn-pA@giganews.com> <10mbfn7g0j7if2f@corp.supernews.com> <g-SdndJtpZbv5PvcRVn-qQ@giganews.com> <10md2gp2it1gl75@corp.supernews.com> <S7Sdnezh1MtJQfvcRVn-sg@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:S7Sdnezh1MtJQfvcRVn-sg@giganews.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> You would require infinitely many "guard" digits (whatever they are).

IEEE 754, as I recall, requires 3 guard bits to protect against
loss of precision during floaing-point operations. Similarly,
decimal rounding uses 1 guard digit to which 5 is added before
a final divide by 10. I have seen the term 'guard' used in these
descriptions.

> By using as many artificial digits of precision, you can come up with
almost
> anything. 1 / 3 * 100 is NOT 33.33. With at most two digits of precision,
> the only meaningful answer is "30" (but more likely, zero).

Without knowing the picture clauses of all the variables
involved, one cannot know what the 'only meaningful
answer' will be. Given percentage = A / B * 100, if A
has a picture of 9 and value 1, the result will be much
different (for some implementations) than if A has a
picture 9v9(17) value 1. My statements were qualified
by 'Given a picture of zzz.99 for percentage and assuming
that '1' and '3' are the values of other variables.' I never
stated what the pictures for the other variables were. To know
the answer, one must use the value relative to the picture.
1 / B has a different result than 100000000000000000 / B.
In the latter case, A has more guard digits than required.
In the former case, A has no guard digits.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-09T07:33:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mfjb83a77ur93@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <10mau6rp5onfpf1@corp.supernews.com> <2KOdndEfkorMKfjcRVn-pA@giganews.com> <10mbfn7g0j7if2f@corp.supernews.com> <g-SdndJtpZbv5PvcRVn-qQ@giganews.com> <10md2gp2it1gl75@corp.supernews.com> <S7Sdnezh1MtJQfvcRVn-sg@giganews.com> <10mdtolrjgmlp25@corp.supernews.com> <fO2dnZsFoYcGzvrcRVn-iw@giganews.com> <10mf6f1i5qi5kd0@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:10mf6f1i5qi5kd0@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> program supplied. Is Microsoft wrong? <g>

No pictures (except for result) and using precision Micro Focus
supplies.
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
----
Using Micro Focus COBOL Version 3.2.24

1 / 3 * 100
33.333333333333333333
1 * 100 / 3
33.333333333333333333

       identification division.
       program-id. test-1.
       data division.
       1 result-1.
        2 compute-text pic x(11) value '1 / 3 * 100'.
        2 high-part pic ------------------9.
        2 low-part pic .9(18).
        66 both-parts renames high-part thru low-part.
       1 result-2.
        2 compute-text pic x(11) value '1 * 100 / 3'.
        2 high-part pic ------------------9.
        2 low-part pic .9(18).
        66 both-parts renames high-part thru low-part.
       1 result-display pic x(38).
       1 result-start comp pic s9(4).
       procedure division.
       begin.
           compute high-part of result-1 low-part of result-1
             = 1 / 3 * 100
           compute high-part of result-2 low-part of result-2
             = 1 * 100 / 3
           move 1 to result-start
           inspect both-parts of result-1
             tallying result-start for leading spaces
           move both-parts of result-1 (result-start:)
             to result-display
           display compute-text of result-1
           display result-display
           move 1 to result-start
           inspect both-parts of result-2
             tallying result-start for leading spaces
           move both-parts of result-2 (result-start:)
             to result-display
           display compute-text of result-2
           display result-display
           stop run.
       end program test-1.
```

That is the first time in almost 40 years that I have seen level 66 actually
used in a program.

Most people let REDEFINES do the job, but for regrouping data it works
admirably.

(Still never used it myself, but became aware of it while doing compiler
maintenance many years ago.)

Pete.

news:1097451648.IBP+pkn076EHky1601sdjQ@teranews...
> Congratulations Rick.
>

actually

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Mr Dashwood, as I wrote the program, I was concerned
with only one thing; ensuring that neither compute statement
interfered with the other's results; hence the duplication of
code and the need for qualification.

The conventional choice might have been to place 'high-part'
and 'low-part' under the group 'both parts'. I think, maybe,
that since I was challenging the idea that a result could not
exceed 18 digits, I may have gone overboard with the rest
of the program, as well. This includes the use of 66 and
single digit level numbers, which are challenges to
conventional style.

I have used 66 twice before, in code posted to the group,
and was involved in a discussion regarding 66 about 7 years
ago.

[Your post took about 24 hours to arrive.]


> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-10T03:24:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mhovoailb7009@corp.supernews.com>`

#### ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-06T18:59:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3rCdnW-m0uAjG_ncRVn-og@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:
> I teach a class in File Structures in which COBOL is used as the
> language of interest.  We are using Thane Hubble's book as a text.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> much more common and continue to be widely used.  Am I correct?

Maybe. Why would anyone care?

Compute X = X + 1
Add 1 to X

are interchangable on a whim.

Compute X = (A + ((B + C) / 2) ) * 1.5 + (2 * D + E) / 3.3333
has no primitive counterpart.

>
> The question arises out of a student question to which I did not know
> the correct answer.  Thank you for any direction you can give.
>
> Floyd Johnson
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T13:28:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0410071228.1927e28d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <3rCdnW-m0uAjG_ncRVn-og@giganews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<35Z8d.2192085$ic1.223442@news.easynews.com>...

Bill Klein wrote:
>
> If you "understand" what each compiler does with intermediate results, then   
> it can be pretty powerful.                                                  

I came across the following in another group for IBM COBOL.  I don't
recall the author's name.

It seems like a good "one size fits all" solution to the "rounding" 
problem Bill alludes to.

Does anybody see a downside?  I'd appreciate your feedback.

Regards, Jack.

Simply define a variable (e.g. SEE-NOTE-ABOVE) in Working Storage
as follows:

*  NOTE: The " SEE-NOTE-ABOVE" variable below is used to prevent
*  erroneous truncation of COMPUTEd values that use division or multi-
*  plication.

 01   SEE-NOTE-ABOVE       PIC9(1)V9(17) VALUE ZEROS.

Now add this variable to any COMPUTE expression as follows.

 COMPUTE   F  =  (A / B) * C   +
                 SEE-NOTE-ABOVE

The addition of zero is not going to change the actual value,
but it does nullify the Truncation Menace!
```

#### ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-10-09T12:21:31+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1097277697.cBBnw42V2LQlGGEAhvNrGw@teranews>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny>`

```
"Floyd Johnson" <floydj@frontiernet.net> wrote in message
news:IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny...
> I teach a class in File Structures in which COBOL is used as the
> language of interest.  We are using Thane Hubble's book as a text.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
When COMPUTE first became available there was quite a bit of resistance to
it.

I remember one site I worked on (which used an NCR 315) only allowing it if
it included functions: COMPUTE gradient = tan(theta)

(Yes, NCR had a tape based COBOL compiler that included all trig and
mathematical functions, in 1965...The downside was that it took around 40
minutes to compile 1000 lines of COBOL. This led to extensive patching of
object decks, with the consequent disasters you would expect. But those NCR
programmers did enjoy job security... <G>)

Another site (Burroughs equipment B200) used it as a form of "arithmetic
move". It was "quicker" to do a compute than to MOVE numeric fields that
required conversion. (I know, it doesn't make sense but that was the
perceived wisdom at the time...)

Today, we would simply smile at the above. We all "know better".

As a matter of style (and that is ALL it is...) I would use COMPUTE where
there was a requirement for more than one arithmetic operation, in line, or
I needed a function (see NCR example above).

So...

ADD 1 to count
COMPUTE result = ((x * y) + (z ** .5)) - w      *> Exponentiation to .5 is
often more efficient than SQRT function.

(Obviously, if "count" above needed further manipulation or was used in the
subsequent calculations, I would simply include it in the COMPUTE.)

From the code  I have observed, I would say that COMPUTE is used just as
much as the other COBOL arithmetic verbs (possibly even more so) so I don't
think your conclusion stands up, Floyd. It is true that the arithmetic verbs
are still widely used, but so is COMPUTE.

Pete.
```

## Thread: define for pathway server — 2 msgs

### define for pathway server

- **From:** Tom <nomail@pleaseno.invalid>
- **Date:** 2004-10-06T23:01:24+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9l8m0h32k88khf11t1prs4a1g7mfjgp5v@4ax.com>`

```
Is there any way to make a define known to a serverprocess other than
through the serverclass-definition ? 

I want the define to be available to all serverclasses in all pathways
of an application. 

I know a definename is normally resolved in the structure of a
filename. Is it possible to have it resolved to a text longer than 8
chars (what i get using the class map define, stripping the last part
of it). i need this to know to what serverclass I need to go to.  

Exploitation doesn't want to deal with params for this, though it's
probably the best way to reolve this, only you need to specify this
with every serverclass in every pathway.


regards,

Tom
```

## Thread: Dummy — 6 msgs

### Dummy

- **From:** "Ron S" <NoSpam@SpamStopper.org>
- **Date:** 2004-10-06T22:01:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KfadnQJ6EpI-LPncRVn_vA@giganews.com>`

```
So....I guess we're all agreed? There should be no problem using DD DUMMY for
an output file except sometimes you need a DCB which is hardly unpredictable. But, no one
knows why IBM, in the Enterprise COBOL manual, says results are unpredictable for
DD DUMMY output nor has anyone any experience with it not doing exactly what they
expect or "predict". Can we therefore say the manual is incorrect? Or at least mysterious?

I'm going to post this question on mvshelp see if anyone has a clue what this is supposed to mean.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dummy

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T05:09:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1c49d.2218165$ic1.226989@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<KfadnQJ6EpI-LPncRVn_vA@giganews.com>`

```
I have sent my question to my "usually reliable source" - but still haven't 
heard anything back.

One issue that *might* cause a problem is if you have a EXCEPTION DECLARATIVE. 
I am not certain if a DD DUMMY might "interfer" with that.  I know that IBM says 
to code

    EROPT=ACC

See:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg20/1.9.3

but most people do NOT actually do so.

As far as coding a DCB for a DD DUMMY file, at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg20/1.9.4.1

it says,
 "DCB attributes coded for a DD DUMMY do not override those coded in the FD 
entry of your COBOL program. "
```

As I remember it (many years ago) the reason for needing the DCB in
the JCL for an output file was to complete the DCB information,
specifically the blocksize, which was defined as zero in the FD to
allow variable blocking according to the JCL.

Robert

#### ↳ Re: Dummy

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-10-11T15:16:26+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1097460991.GoVk7xejNPd9kpRI5Gm2vg@teranews>`
- **References:** `<KfadnQJ6EpI-LPncRVn_vA@giganews.com>`

```
Ron,

I can't say for sure the following applies in this case, but it might...

Put yourself in the place of the manual writer.

A situation arises where you are not 100% sure of the outcome. You approach
the software developers and they are not entirely sure either. There are
many complex factors that make it nearly impossible to check all possible
code and see what happens or what the impacts are for non-compliance. What
is for certain is that if you do the RIGHT things, the correct results will
follow.

So you want to document that it is on the user's own head if he does not
comply, even though it COULD be OK.

The "standard" phrase for this is "results are unpredictable".

I have used this phrase when writing manuals, for exactly this scenario.
(The results may very well be predictable, but I can't predict them at time
of writng.)

BOTTOM LINE: Don't read too much into this phrase. If something works, then,
the empirical approach beats the theoretical one in much the same way as a
straight flush beats four aces... unexpectedly.

Pete.

unpredictable for

what they

One other anomaly using DUMMY:

If you concat a DUMMY file any other place but the last file position,
the first read of the DUMMY sends you to EOF and all succeeding
concats are not read.

Jack

## Thread: OT: The Ultimate in Outsourcing — 32 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: The Ultimate in Outsourcing

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T15:26:21+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y8ON1LBNIVZBFwjZ@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
OUTSOURCING OF JOBS REACHES THE PRESIDENT

by Staff Reporter Melynda Jill

Washington DC - Congress today announced that the Office of President of 
the United States of America will be outsourced to overseas interests as 
of June 30th, the end of this fiscal year. The move is being made to 
save not only a significant portion of the President's $400K yearly 
salary, but also a record $521 Billion in deficit expenditures and 
related overhead. "We believe this is a wise move financially. The cost 
savings should be significant," stated Congressman Thomas Reynolds 
(R-Wash). Reynolds, with the aid of the GAO (the General Accounting 
Office), has studied outsourcing of American jobs extensively. "We 
cannot expect to remain competitive on the world stage with the current 
level of cash outlay," Reynolds noted. Mr. Bush was informed by email 
this morning of his termination. Preparations for the job move have been 
underway for some time. Sanji Gurvinder Singh of Indus Teleservices, 
Mumbai, India will be assuming the Office of President of the United 
States as of July 1. Mr. Singh was born in the United States while his 
parents were vacationing at Niagara Falls, thus making him eligible for 
the position. He will receive a salary of $320 (USD) a month but with no 
health coverage or other benefits. It is believed that Mr. Singh will be 
able to handle his job responsibilities without support staff. Due to 
the time difference between the US and India, he will be working 
primarily at night, when few offices of the US Government will be open. 
"Working nights will allow me to keep my day job at the American Express 
call center," stated Mr. Singh in an exclusive interview. "I am excited 
about this position. I always hoped I would be President someday." A 
Congressional Spokesperson noted that while Mr. Singh may not be fully 
aware of all the issues involved in the office of President, this should 
not be a problem. Mr. Singh will rely upon a script tree that will 
enable him to respond effectively to most topics of concern. Using this 
tree, he can address common concerns without having to understand the 
underlying issues at all. "We know these scripting tools work," stated 
the Spokesperson. "Mr. Bush has used them successfully for years." Mr. 
Bush will receive health coverage, expenses, and salary until his final 
day of employment. Following a two week waiting period, he will be 
eligible for $240 dollars a week unemployment for 13 weeks. 
Unfortunately he will not be eligible for Medicaid as his unemployment 
benefits will exceed the allowed limit. Mr. Bush has been provided the 
outplacement services of Manpower, Inc. to help him write a resume and 
prepare for his upcoming job transition. According to Manpower, Mr. Bush 
may have difficulties in securing a new position due to limited 
practical work experience. One possibility is re-enlistment in the Army 
National Guard. Should he choose this option, he would likely be 
stationed in Iraq, a country he has visited. "I've been there, I know 
all about Iraq," stated Mr. Bush, who gained invaluable knowledge of the 
country in a visit to the Baghdad Airport non-smoking terminal and gift 
shop. Sources in Baghdad and Fallujah say Mr. Bush would receive a warm 
reception from local Iraqis. They have asked to be provided with details 
of his arrival so that they might arrange an appropriate welcome.
```

Robert Wagner wrote:

>> Would you rather the oil fields go to France, German, or Russia?
>
> They're has-beens. More likely contenders are China and Japan.

China and Japan do not have the weasel mindset and the corruption skills of 
the other three.


>
>> PS
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.halliburton.com/about/history_new_phase.jsp


Had you clicked on the "History" link in the site you found, you could have 
read the following sentence:

"Major purchases include Brown & Root, an engineering and construction 
company, in 1962..."

I live less than two miles from B&R's corporate headquarters. I know who 
they are and what they do.

>
>> 2. It is ludicrous to say someone is "saving the oilfields" for
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> and abandonment. "

I say again: Halliburton is not in the oil exploration, drilling, pumping, 
transport, or refining business. They CONSTRUCT these things for companies 
who do dabble in oil. Halliburton also builds roads, hospitals, all manner 
of buildings, pipelines, and other large-scale construction projects.

>
>> 3. Halliburton DOES, however, put out oil field fires - it would be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Control is not owned by Halliburton.

I live in Houston. I knew Red Adair and his company. I haven't been watching 
too many movies (why would you think I did?) Halliburton, through its KBR 
subsidiary, DOES put out oil well fires. One of the first contracts 
Halliburton got in Iraq was for putting out oil well fires (should there be 
any).

"The contract to do the work in Iraq, similar to work the company performed 
in both Bosnia and Kosovo, was competitively bid in 2001. Part of the 
deliverables of that contract was a contingency plan to fight oil well fires 
in case of war with Iraq. When the war began, Halliburton was asked to 
implement the plan. "
http://www.halliburton.com/news/archive/2003/corpnws_091903.jsp

Stick to things you know about, such as (and I'm guessing here) tormenting 
chickens.
```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:10mieu2ai8v7h37@corp.supernews.com...
[snip]
> [You just can't keep Texas politicians from interfering with
> free enterprise!]

I misspoke, actually you can't keep politicians from interfering.

One does not often find, in film, antagonist politicians from
Iowa; but do find them from Texas. For example, in the film
classic "G.I. Jane" (1997), the evil Senator Lillian DeHaven
(Anne Bancroft) was from Texas and would have sacraficed
our heroine's career to further her political interests. But,
Lt. Jordan O'Neil (Demi Moore) fought back and achieved
her goal. It doesn't get better than that!

----
Sen. Lillian DeHaven : (On why she started Lt. O'Neil on the
    SEAL program in the first place) Truthfully, I never expected
    you to do so damn well! I figured you'd ring out in two weeks,
    bing bang it's over, and we're popular. In Washington, you
    don't even need the Ten Commandments when you're popular!
----
Lt. Jordan O'Neil : Master Chief...
Master Chief John Urgayle : Lieutenant, seek life elsewhere.
Lt. Jordan O'Neil : Suck my ****!
----
[after O'Neill saves his life in combat]
Master Chief John Urgayle : I'll never live this one down.
----

You go, Girl!

[There ought to be some humor in there, somewhere.]
```

- **References:** `<y8ON1LBNIVZBFwjZ@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <na7gm0dj9cqbdc3f007g49umi29rkrfslm@4ax.com> <Gb-dnROJippy1fXcRVn-rQ@giganews.com>`

```
.    Am  09.10.04
schrieb  nospam@bisusa.com (JerryMouse)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  Gb-dnROJippy1fXcRVn-rQ@giganews.com
  ueber  Re: OT: The Ultimate in Outsourcing

>> We learned our lesson in Korea, Vietnam and Iraq v1.0. As a result,
>> out ambition is reduced to saving the oilfields for Halliburton, Brown
>> & Root and George W's other corporate friends.

n> Would you rather the oil fields go to France, German, or Russia?

   Why not leaving the oil fields to the countries where those oil  
fields happen to be? Why not work in international cooperation for the  
eradication of hunger, providing clean drinking water, housing,  
sanitation, education, medical care, to each and every human being?


n> No, having Iraq oil under the influence of the U.S. is a goal worth
n> fighting, and dying, for.

   For whom? Do those who profit from the control over world oil  
production and distribution do the actual dying?

   By the way, it is not only dying, but also killing.

n> With out sufficiently cheap oil, many, many thousands will suffer.

   Actually, the price of oil is going up up up since that war, and at  
least in part _because_ of it.

   And many many suffered and still do suffer. Iraq lost more than  
10'000 lives, and the US more than 1000. And this is only in that  
country.

   How many more have to die before the bad dream of a world under a  
single control vanishes and makes room for a cooperation where _all_  
of humanity can grow and develop?

   Wouldn't it be better for us human beings if we could combine our  
forces to _save_ lives instead of destroying them?

   Bringing down infant mortality instead of killing human beings in  
wars?

   Consider the hundreds, and in Haiti more than thousand of human  
lives lost due to the recent Hurrican season. All these deaths could  
have been prevented, as the Cuban example shows, where Hurrican Ivan  
did not destroy a single human life.

   Wouldn't it be better to try and learn from Cuba how to cope with  
Hurricans instead of punishing its people with trade embargo and  
travel bans?



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

  "Nach meiner Ansicht besitzt die Presse _das_ _Recht_,
Schriftsteller, Politiker, Komï¿½dianten und andere ï¿½ffentliche
Charaktere zu _beleidigen_. Achtete ich [so einen Angriff gegen mich]
einer Notiz wert, so galt mir in solchen Fï¿½llen der Wahlspruch: ï¿½
corsaire, corsaire et demi [auf einen Schelmen anderthalben]."
                  - Karl Marx   17.11.1860  (Herr Vogt, Kapitel XI)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Ultimate in Outsourcing

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-10T07:26:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u46dnZIfGdHgt_TcRVn-jw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<y8ON1LBNIVZBFwjZ@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <na7gm0dj9cqbdc3f007g49umi29rkrfslm@4ax.com> <Gb-dnROJippy1fXcRVn-rQ@giganews.com> <9IcxlOzuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:

You raise some good points. Wrong, but good.

> n> Would you rather the oil fields go to France, German, or Russia?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> sanitation, education, medical care, to each and every human being?

International cooperation does little to ease hunger, potable water, etc. 
The political systems are the main determinate. There has never been a 
famine in a democracy.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> production and distribution do the actual dying?

Everyone profits from access to oil and many die without access. Freeze in 
the dark is the alternative - for everyone. Some try to level the playing 
field so that everyone benefits. Some countries, like France, Germany, and 
Russia try illegal and immoral methods of gaining an advantage. The French 
are particularily bad weasels.

>
>   By the way, it is not only dying, but also killing.

Not if the enemy bends to our will.

>
> n> With out sufficiently cheap oil, many, many thousands will suffer.
>
>   Actually, the price of oil is going up up up since that war, and at
> least in part _because_ of it.

The most recent spike is the result of hurricanes in the Gulf of Mexico. The 
price of oil IS higher, though. And it will get worse. US oil consumption is 
expected to rise by 30% by 2050. Consumption will DOUBLE in China. A 
democratic Iraq will tend to stabilize oil prices for a few years until the 
Russian fields come online.

>
>   And many many suffered and still do suffer. Iraq lost more than
> 10'000 lives, and the US more than 1000. And this is only in that
> country.

And many, many more benefited. I saw a tabulation of how many lives were NOT 
lost because Hussein was not killing his own people at his average rate. He 
was killing people at rates orders of magnitude larger than current losses.

>
>   How many more have to die before the bad dream of a world under a
> single control vanishes and makes room for a cooperation where _all_
> of humanity can grow and develop?

As many as necessary to rid the world of tyranny. This does not imply 
"single control."

>
>   Wouldn't it be better for us human beings if we could combine our
> forces to _save_ lives instead of destroying them?

No, not directly. The goal is to erradicate tyranny. A by-product of 
instituting democracy is saving lives, but saving lives is not the actual 
goal. You fight the disease, not the symptom.

>
>   Bringing down infant mortality instead of killing human beings in
> wars?

Again, it's the political system that's the chief determinate. Under Israeli 
occupation, for example, Palestinians enjoyed, by every objective standard, 
a better quality of life than their co-religionists elsewhere. Infant 
mortality, for example, is now about twice (20.4/1k births) as it was under 
Israeli occupation (~9/1k). [Current: US = 8.4/k, Egypt = 35/k, Israel = 
6.3/k, Germany = 5.4/k]

>
>   Consider the hundreds, and in Haiti more than thousand of human
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> travel bans?

There are many lessons to learn from Cuba; hurricane survival is not one of 
them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Ultimate in Outsourcing

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Ultimate in Outsourcing

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-10T13:41:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10mit5jmpp56i50@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<y8ON1LBNIVZBFwjZ@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <na7gm0dj9cqbdc3f007g49umi29rkrfslm@4ax.com> <Gb-dnROJippy1fXcRVn-rQ@giganews.com> <9IcxlOzuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <u46dnZIfGdHgt_TcRVn-jw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:u46dnZIfGdHgt_TcRVn-jw@giganews.com...
[snip]
> As many as necessary to rid the world of tyranny.
[snip]
>  The goal is to erradicate tyranny.

I hate to be the one to tell you this; but the government
of the United States is a tyranny and has been one for
more than 110 years.

One definition of tyanny is 'the arbitrary or unrestrained
exercise of power' [RHCD]. It is generally acknowledged
that the Constitution of the United States is a restraint on
the exercise of power; but that changed slowly.

I am uncertain when the phrase developed; but it is said
that 'The Supreme Court is the final arbiter of the
Constitution'. However, there is nothing in the Constitution
to suggest that should be the case. Article III states 'The
judicial power shall extend to all cases, in law and equity,
arising under this Constitution, ...' Notice that the words
'under this Constitution' cannot be presumed to mean the
Constitution itself, as such a decision would be arbitrary
('decided by a judge or arbiter rather than by law or
statute [RHCD] ). Under Article VI, the Constitution
is Supreme Law and judicial officers of the United States
are legally obligated to support it. By my understanding
of the words in Article VI, they have not.

Whatever restraint is exercised is by judges ('judicial
restraint') and is done so arbitrarily. This seems to fit
the definition of tyranny, given above.
```

else you may have to say.'

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: The Ultimate in Outsourcing

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-10-12T20:35:46+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1097566554.6IY5bimlc5pj2/nw15YtGg@teranews>`
- **References:** `<y8ON1LBNIVZBFwjZ@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <u46dnZIfGdHgt_TcRVn-jw@giganews.com> <10mit5jmpp56i50@corp.supernews.com> <9I$0qm3eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9I$0qm3eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    Am  10.10.04
> schrieb  ricksmith@mfi.net (Rick Smith)
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Unfortunately (from your point of view - not everybody's), Lueko, this is an
unmoderated, uncensored, forum and anbody can post anything they like. It is
not your place (or anybody else's) to try and moderate it.

I sympathise with your position, but the OT prefix in the message means it
is "Off Topic". Therefore, anyone who only wants to talk about COBOL, can
(should?) simply ignore such messages.

This forum always has been and still remains, one of the last bastions of
free speech on the planet. Sometimes the discussions here are boring,
sometimes they are stimulating, (it depends on personal taste and
persuasion) but we should all value the fact that things posted here are
very often from the heart.

There will be times when you won't like what you read here. It is just
opinion. It isn't life and death.

Be thankful there is still at least one unregulated place where vested
interests have failed to penetrate, and what you read is what people really
think.

We have discussed here on numerous occasions whether it SHOULD be a
moderated newsgroup. It raises a lot of questions:

Who would moderate it?
Would people continue to post to it?
Is there some value in having a free outlet for expression?
Does it NEED regulation?
Are COBOL people so serious and dull that the ONLY thing they can talk about
is COBOL?

These questions in turn raise other questions and the result is that it
remains a free unmoderated, uncensored newsgroup.

I hope it remains ever so.

Pete.

>
> "Die Arbeit in wei�er Haut kann sich nicht dort emanzipieren, wo sie
> in schwarzer Haut gebrandmarkt wird."     - Karl Marx     12.11.1866
>
"Gut ist der schlaf
Der tot ist besser.
Das beste wurde nie geboren zu sein."

On that cheerful thought from Heinrich Heine, lighten up and enjoy CLC. <G>
```

<a id="t-9ced87cbcb"></a>
## Thread: Help:: IMS - Count of Values in Field using FileAid — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help:: IMS - Count of Values in Field using FileAid

- **From:** "Karim Jessani" <kalusalu*!spam not*!@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T11:09:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41655cb4$1@post.usenet.com>`

```
**** Post for FREE via your newsreader at post.usenet.com ****

Hello all. I have access to FileAid for IMS. I have tried 2 ways to do the
following.
I need to have a count of the amount of a certain value in a field.
ie in a "TYPE CODE" that has Y and N, I need to know the number of Y's and
the number of N's.

I have tried to use the FLEX JCL Option and came up with this:

TYPE RUN;
PSB DBNAME=MYCPCSE;
SELECT SEGMENT=MYCSCSRT MAX=ALL
WHERE CS-CAPP-AGCY=4
COUNT SEGMENT=MYCSCSRT
;

Where I am looking for the count of 4's in this field. Running it, I get the
count of everything in the field. So this is not working:

So I thought to create a "Selection Criteria" through Option 6: Which
created the following for me:

IXPSC MYCPCSE YCNY 06.02.00
DIXPSC ATFD.DEVL.ATFDT8.CNTL FILEAID1
DIXPD1 ATGN.TIMS.DBDLIB MYCPCSE
DIXPD2 NOT USED
DIXPAR NOT USED
DIXPFD ATFD.TIMS.XREFC MYCPCSE
DIXPC1 ATFD.DEVL.COPYLIB
DIXPC2 ATFD.PROD.COPYLIB
1
1
1
3 CS-CAPP-AGCY=4
4MYCSCSRTY S
5 � YDR CS-CAPP-AGCY=4
6CS-CAPP-AGCY X � 14


Using the above when extracting from the database gives me all the values in
this field not just the ones I am interested in.


ANYONE Has any other ideas? Perhaps a FLEX JCL that would allow me to find
out the count of a certain field?

THANKS SO MUCH
Karim



-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
 *** Usenet.com - The #1 Usenet Newsgroup Service on The Planet! ***
                      http://www.usenet.com
Unlimited Download - 19 Seperate Servers - 90,000 groups - Uncensored
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
```

<a id="t-04000a5c60"></a>
## Thread: OFF Topic — 277 this month / 339 total

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OFF Topic

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-07T19:45:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net>`

```
Rock the boat!

As a long time reader of the comp.lang.cobol newsgroup the
discussions are often very redundant. I find it easy to get
ideas from their view of the subject.

What can I do that will bring out the inventiveness of these
COBOL brains? See subject above. "They" below  is aimed
at everyone who posts on a regular basis and some on
an irregular basis.


They do a great job of helping those who seek help. They
share different methods that they use which is a general
benefit to the readers,

But, there is always a but! Often they get into the mode
of "I'm smart, you're dumb."

In my view, they really are looking for something
constructive to do, and IF they were to turn their
energies to mutual development proposals for
the COBOL language specifications, it would result in
a major step forward in the direction of the goals of
COBOL.

I really feel that _had _the members of this group been
assigned the job of the short range committee,
and given COBOL '85' as a starting point, a much better
result might have easily been achieved by 2050.
If they would only debate, and demonstrate, what a difference
they could make.  (Or the only thing wrong is they won't let me
run it!)

Maybe by 2020!


Outside of washing dishes I have Nothing to do but look,
and read c.l.cobol and the world news. This takes me into
all kinds of countries.

Here is one I had not heard of, and I'm wondering if the C.L.COBOL
newsgroup has, or has discussed such tools. As it's billed as
a Business Processing Language, it seemed it might be of interest
to the "gang".

Wish I had known how to make this a url.

http://activebpel.org/info/intro.html


Warren

P.S. I also read Freshmeat daily. I have a few interesting
items from todays listing that I plan to post here next.


I hope you can tell I was being sarcastic! <VBG>
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lNi9d.657961$gE.286730@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net>`

```
Warren Simmons wrote:

#### ↳ Re: OFF Topic

```

"Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote in message
news:41659C51.30908@optonline.net...
> Rock the boat!
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, reading it gives me ideas too, Warren... That's why I've almost
stopped... It brings out the dark side in me...<G>

> What can I do that will bring out the inventiveness of these
> COBOL brains?

Lobotomy is a tried and tested process...


> See subject above. "They" below  is aimed
> at everyone who posts on a regular basis and some on
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What?! CLCers?! never....

I could see much of what goes on here being a repetition of the Church in
the Middle Ages.

They spent over 200 years arguing about the number of angels that could
stand on a pinhead.

(Mind you, I don't believe that would happen here... no-one could stay on
topic long enough.  Long before the first hundred years was up, the
discussion would have moved to the necessity for angels to have pins, then a
bitter argument over types of pins, whether they were being sourced by cheap
labour offshore, and whether they complied with the Heavenly Standard for
Pins laid down by St. Anne (the patron Saint of Seamstresses). There would
then be a 50 year argument between two or three contributors regarding the
details of the process of manufacturing pins, with one arguing that the
composition was all important, another that the temperature was, and the
third pointing out that both the other two were being pedantic when it was
really just a matter of style...)

Midway through the second century someone else would chime in that the whole
point was being missed, and the discussion was really about foot size, as
that was the determining factor in standing on pinheads. After a few decades
of discussion on angelic footwear and whether sandals would occupy more
space than sneakers or gumboots, someone would observe that it was a bit
unnecessary for angels to occupy pinheads anyway, and how could such a
stupid topic be occupying the brilliant minds of the group?

In the meantime, various contributors (having done their own painstaking
calculations) would have offered various numbers, and been rejected as not
understanding the nature of the problem or having a proper grasp of the
ramifications.

As the third century of discussion began, algorithms for calculating angelic
footprints would start to emerge and the whole newsgroup would split into
various camps supporting one of several approaches. A "religious War" would
then ensue with a lot of unangelic abuse and bad behaviour. Robert Wagner
would be blamed for everything and each of his posts would be carefully
shredded. Undeterred, Robert would continue in his assertion that he had
been counting angels on pinheads before any of the other contributors were
aware that there were such things as pins, and had once developed a software
engine that could instantly calculate the number of angels from the increase
in weight.At this point Richard would observe that angels have no weight,
and therefore Robert's machine was totally inaccurate and pointless and his
claim to superiority could be dismissed as trivial.

This exchange would continue for another 60 years or so, until Robert would
suddenly change his position by remembering that it wasn't the weight, it
was the mass...  (and on that very appropriate thought for a religious
discussion, I shall leave this little whimsy...)


> In my view, they really are looking for something
> constructive to do, and IF they were to turn their
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Always assuming there WERE identified goals for COBOL... It would probably
take a century or two to get agreement on those, before anything really
started.

> I really feel that _had _the members of this group been

> and given COBOL '85' as a starting point, a much better
> result might have easily been achieved by 2050.

ROFL! Good one, Warren... <G>

> If they would only debate, and demonstrate, what a difference

On the evidence that is exhibited here, it would appear extremely doubtful
whether any difference would be made. It was "COBOL people" on J4...

   Your links don't work with Mozilla. You need a little bit more

that "form1 is not defined".

   "forms" are in the object hierarchy under the document object, and  
you would have to specify "document.forms[1]" or  
'document.forms["form1"]' or "document.form1". Maybe it is still more  
complicated by using frames.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creative Javascript writing (was: OFF Topic)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-10-12T09:25:24+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1097540482.Ryt2G/HX3XTBWCi501aGEg@teranews>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <f4H9d.15711$VJ2.12336@fe40.usenetserver.com> <b3638c46.0410091754.731d9cb3@posting.google.com> <9IcxnTKPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Thanks for that Lueko.

(And thanks to the many people who accessed it from all over the world. You
created quite a ripple of excitement in our little group... I have to
apologise to the ones who used Netscape or Mozilla. Explanation follows.)

The site is only guaranteed with IE4 and above.

I started out testing it with Netscape and IE but it got too cumbersome to
keep detecting which Browser is being used and react accordingly.

My ISP shows that over 95% of access is with IE so that's the one I cater
for and test it with.

Currently the site is hosted on one of my ISPs but we have now obtained a
proper domain name and will be moving to a proper host, as soon as I can
find one that is cheap, reliable, supports CGI and will let me run the COBOL
runtime in it, and has a remote server for MySQL (that can support ACCESS
through ODBC).

I am aware of the document model for JavaScript, but I didn't realise it was
necessary to qualify back to the document
when it only contains one form and that form IS defined. (And it icertainly
ISN'T necessary with IE; the site works.)

I'll try adding the document qualifier to form references and see if it
makes any difference.

I'll also add a logo indicating that it is best viewed with IE.

As this is a "labour of Love" for which I do not get paid, I really can't
spend too many hours on it.

Thanks for the "heads up". In the meantime, try accessing it with IE.

If this is not available and you really want to see the newsletter, another
alternative would be to click the "archive" link on the same page and select
"current" from the table of past documents. As it doesn't use a form control
that SHOULD work.

Pete.

(Top post, nothing below the signature.)

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> My ISP shows that over 95% of access is with IE so that's the one I cater
> for and test it with.

Is that visitors or page hits ?  Obviously if a site doesn't work for
one visitor there will be much fewer hits from him.

Also you shouldn't assume that because a browser identifies as IE that
it is IE.  Many MS sites give different pages out depending on which
browser is identified. Opera especially will identify as IE:

""" ----------------------
Two weeks ago it was revealed that Microsoft's MSN portal targeted
Opera users, by purposely providing them with a broken page. As a
reply to MSN's treatment of its users, Opera Software today released a
very special Bork edition of its Opera 7 for Windows browser. The Bork
edition behaves differently on one Web site: MSN. Users accessing the
MSN site will see the page transformed into the language of the famous
Swedish Chef from the Muppet Show: Bork, Bork, Bork!

In October 2001, Opera users were blocked from the MSN site. The event
caused an uproar among Web users and MSN was forced to change their
policy. However, MSN continues a policy of singling out its Opera
competitor by specifically instructing Opera to hide content from
users.

"Hergee berger snooger bork," says Mary Lambert, product line manager
desktop, Opera Software. "This is a joke. However, we are trying to
make an important point. The MSN site is sending Opera users what
appear to be intentionally distorted pages. The Bork edition
illustrates how browsers could also distort content, as the Bork
edition does. The real point here is that the success of the Web
depends on software and Web site developers behaving well and rising
above corporate rivalry."
--------------- """

More recently:

""" ------------
Opera declaring itself as Opera got an apparently broken page, but was
OK if it claimed to be IE. Par for the course? For sure (there's
always something that is), but we couldn't help noticing that the
error message arrived after the browser was first fielded to this
page: http://www.playsforsure.com/MobileDefault.aspx. Now, we're just
guessing here, but that kind of looks like where a site might be
putting you if you were accessing it via a mobile phone.
---------------- """
```
