```
On 24 Oct 2004 23:25:18 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>completely informal mechanisms.

Users don't care about standards; they care about portability. The
standards process was created as a means to that end. If I have to
choose between two formats, one more standardized and the other more
portable, my preference is the latter. 
 
>> >It is primarily informal data exchange (ie application defined such as
>> >CSV) that just uses 'text files' divided into 'lines'.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>done here.

Nor have you. You have not shown that ASCII text is weaker nor defined
by applications. In fact, it is defined by common practice and by
operating systems. 

>> >Your assertion that 'it is ASCII text terminated by CR/LF' is wrong. 
>> >It is ASCII text that may, or need not be, contaminated by various
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Control codes such as CR, LF, TAB.

First you say I'm wrong about CR/LF, then you say I'm right. Which is
it?

>> >Given that 'text files' may include EDIFAC, XML, or unnamed CSV data
>> >then users may have no clue at all how to read and/or use the data.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>'language' ?  

Make that 'communication format'. 

>> Users are on my side.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>EMails is what 'file transfer' is about.

They also recognize transfers to and from Web sites. Email is a good
way to time shift transfers to a client that may be offline. For
example, report distribution.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T13:08:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410251208.5939181f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<t78jn0ljbdpon6kqao42td963riaf3v8rc@4ax.com> <c7kkn09vrs3uli27n81acsdgmmqk64jc6b@4ax.com> <AWCed.3812446$6p.636571@news.easynews.com> <up4mn0dd1h3d8o92vqkaimf59dblcpuvsa@4ax.com> <hIFed.3820586$6p.637682@news.easynews.com> <bntmn01ba71iuv5aiu6fltvo8pia277jc8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410241042.13d1ee6e@posting.google.com> <5doon0t50cmetignjeqm413hceelmghs5i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410242225.7cae4ddc@posting.google.com> <rsqpn09hjj9rphr193efqmfs33kcu2o627@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> Users don't care about standards; they care about portability. 

They care about neither.  They only want the data for the program they
want to use, they neither know nor care about 'standards' or what
issues are involved in portability.

This is why they send Excel spreadsheets and Word documents as EMail
attachments (with or without viruses).

If they actually cared about portability they would use a portable
format themselves.

> The
> standards process was created as a means to that end. If I have to
> choose between two formats, one more standardized and the other more
> portable, my preference is the latter. 

You seem to have gone off against an argument never made by anyone. 
What actual point do you imagine you are arguing against ?

> Nor have you. You have not shown that ASCII text is weaker nor defined
> by applications. In fact, it is defined by common practice and by
> operating systems. 

Nor have I even attempted to argue against ASCII or line terminated
files. I have been pointing out that:

   'LINE SEQUENTIAL' is not necessarily congruent to 'ASCII text
files'

   EDI/EDIFACT does not require nor care about cr/lf

   SEQUENTIAL is perfectly adequate for writing ASCII text (in many
cases),
   as is DISPLAY.  It does not _require_ LINE SEQUENTIAL.

   Many of your assertions are wrong, such as HTML and XML requiring
CR/LF.

You seem to have a fixation that 'ASCII text file' is inseparable from
and defined by 'LINE SEQUENTIAL' and vice versa.
 
> >> >Your assertion that 'it is ASCII text terminated by CR/LF' is wrong. 
> >> >It is ASCII text that may, or need not be, contaminated by various
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> it?

You are wrong that is _is_ terminated by CR/LF as a requirement.  It
may not be, or it may have them, but will completely ignore them (ie
they are contamination that are stripped out).

> >> >Given that 'text files' may include EDIFAC, XML, or unnamed CSV data
> >> >then users may have no clue at all how to read and/or use the data.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Make that 'communication format'. 

EDIFACT, XML, or other may, but need not, or in some cases must not,
have cr/lf control codes.  These are still 'text files'.

CSV and TSV formats certainly do require line terminators to indicate
to start on the next line (or row).

You proclaim to support standards but an extremely limited view of
what they are.

> >> Users are on my side.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> They also recognize transfers to and from Web sites. 

If you knew anything about web sites you should know that web page
content does not care about cr/lf at all (except in <pre> blocks).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-26T02:42:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95crn05k8muunpd7tqdeka3jh34tmr9sr2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c7kkn09vrs3uli27n81acsdgmmqk64jc6b@4ax.com> <AWCed.3812446$6p.636571@news.easynews.com> <up4mn0dd1h3d8o92vqkaimf59dblcpuvsa@4ax.com> <hIFed.3820586$6p.637682@news.easynews.com> <bntmn01ba71iuv5aiu6fltvo8pia277jc8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410241042.13d1ee6e@posting.google.com> <5doon0t50cmetignjeqm413hceelmghs5i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410242225.7cae4ddc@posting.google.com> <rsqpn09hjj9rphr193efqmfs33kcu2o627@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410251208.5939181f@posting.google.com>`

```
On 25 Oct 2004 13:08:35 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>format themselves.

True. The challenge is bridging the gap between programming languages
and unsophisticated users. XML tries to do that. Excel's import wizard
works surprizingly well on flat files. 

If we ever get there, the solution will take control out of the hands
of users AND programmers. Both suffer limited vision.

>> The
>> standards process was created as a means to that end. If I have to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>What actual point do you imagine you are arguing against ?

I'm arguing against fixed-format fixed-length records (more
standardized) and in favor of CSV ASCII text (more portable).

>> Nor have you. You have not shown that ASCII text is weaker nor defined
>> by applications. In fact, it is defined by common practice and by
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>files'

Reality check. It is on IBM mainframe (except codeset is EBCDIC),
Micro Focus, Fujitsu and Realia, at least. Can you name a compiler
where LINE SEQUENTIAL is *not* ASCII text?


>   SEQUENTIAL is perfectly adequate for writing ASCII text (in many
>cases),
>   as is DISPLAY.  It does not _require_ LINE SEQUENTIAL.

Sure, one can also produce ASCII text files with REALTIVE/RANDOM
written one byte at a time. But it's not the most convenient way.

SEQUENTIAL is adequate primarily when records are all the same size,
in which case alpha fields will be padded with spaces and numeric
fields padded with leading zeros. 

People here like to recommend 'best tool for the job'. If LINE
SEQUENTIAL is expressly designed for ASCII text, why do you resist
using it? Is this a quasi-religious issue?

>   Many of your assertions are wrong, such as HTML and XML requiring
>CR/LF.

They don't require CRLF, but they're usually packaged that way for
editing convenience. 

>You seem to have a fixation that 'ASCII text file' is inseparable from
>and defined by 'LINE SEQUENTIAL' and vice versa.

It is.

>> >> Users are on my side.
>> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>content does not care about cr/lf at all (except in <pre> blocks).

I meant they think 'file transfer' is about downloading from a Web
site. For once, they're right.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-26T00:57:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410252357.5df158fd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c7kkn09vrs3uli27n81acsdgmmqk64jc6b@4ax.com> <up4mn0dd1h3d8o92vqkaimf59dblcpuvsa@4ax.com> <hIFed.3820586$6p.637682@news.easynews.com> <bntmn01ba71iuv5aiu6fltvo8pia277jc8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410241042.13d1ee6e@posting.google.com> <5doon0t50cmetignjeqm413hceelmghs5i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410242225.7cae4ddc@posting.google.com> <rsqpn09hjj9rphr193efqmfs33kcu2o627@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410251208.5939181f@posting.google.com> <95crn05k8muunpd7tqdeka3jh34tmr9sr2@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> Excel's import wizard
> works surprizingly well on flat files. 

It may read in the data, but this doesn't mean that the data can be
understood, it is often just a series of numbers. This may mean
nothing to the user.

> I'm arguing against fixed-format fixed-length records (more
> standardized) and in favor of CSV ASCII text (more portable).

Yet Rick's was tab-separated, cr/lf terminated ASCII text.

The problem with CSV is that it is completely informal, the data is
just numbers and/or text with no structure and no inherent meaning.

EDIFACT is a formalised mechanism that identifies what each data item
means.  XML applications (not XML itself) formalise the data items as
well.
 
> Reality check. It is on IBM mainframe (except codeset is EBCDIC),
> Micro Focus, Fujitsu and Realia, at least. Can you name a compiler
> where LINE SEQUENTIAL is *not* ASCII text?

Yes, IBM Mainframe.  You just said it was EBCDIC, did you not notice ?
 There are also compilers where LINE SEQUENTIAL is not anything at
all.

> >   SEQUENTIAL is perfectly adequate for writing ASCII text (in many
> >cases),
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> written one byte at a time. 

NO.  WRONG. YOU CANNOT.  You certainly cannot on Fujitsu or
MicroFocus, I seriously doubt that you can on other systems.

> But it's not the most convenient way.

It is 'not convenient' because it simply doesn't work, unlike the
mechanisms that I suggested.

It seemed SEQUENTIAL was perfectly convenient in Rick's program, it is
perfectly convenient to use DISPLAY.

> People here like to recommend 'best tool for the job'. If LINE
> SEQUENTIAL is expressly designed for ASCII text, why do you resist
> using it? Is this a quasi-religious issue?

Where do you see me "resist using it" ?  I, however, have not argued,
as you have, that there is 'only one correct way' in a dogmatic way.

You may notice, if you actually read the messages, that my point has
always been that they can do it whatever way they wish as long as it
gets the results they require.

(Whereas RELATIVE/RANDOM does not get the right result at all).

> >You seem to have a fixation that 'ASCII text file' is inseparable from
> >and defined by 'LINE SEQUENTIAL' and vice versa.
> 
> It is.

I can have perfectly valid and useful ASCII text files that cannot be
read or written correctly as LINE SEQUENTIAL. There is a common subset
of 'ASCII text file' and 'LINE SEQUENTIAL' where there is an overlap,
that doesn't make the two congruent (except in your mind).

> I meant they think 'file transfer' is about downloading from a Web
> site. For once, they're right.

Most of the file transfer that I deal with is entirely automatic, _my_
users prefer it that way, they only notice it when they get an EMail
from my programs that tell them something failed, (their clients get
an EMail acknowlegement when it works).

If your users have to get involved with file transfer by downloading
from a web page then they may be poorly served.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-26T14:50:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9rosn0dg8ab6sugs14uoojundigi6topf0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<up4mn0dd1h3d8o92vqkaimf59dblcpuvsa@4ax.com> <hIFed.3820586$6p.637682@news.easynews.com> <bntmn01ba71iuv5aiu6fltvo8pia277jc8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410241042.13d1ee6e@posting.google.com> <5doon0t50cmetignjeqm413hceelmghs5i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410242225.7cae4ddc@posting.google.com> <rsqpn09hjj9rphr193efqmfs33kcu2o627@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410251208.5939181f@posting.google.com> <95crn05k8muunpd7tqdeka3jh34tmr9sr2@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410252357.5df158fd@posting.google.com>`

```
On 26 Oct 2004 00:57:48 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

>The problem with CSV is that it is completely informal, the data is
>just numbers and/or text with no structure and no inherent meaning.

Usually, but not necessarily. The first record can be a template
containing field names.

>EDIFACT is a formalised mechanism that identifies what each data item
>means.  XML applications (not XML itself) formalise the data items as
>well.

I don't dispute the superiority of EDIFACT and XML. They're not as
widely used or supported .. yet.

>It seemed SEQUENTIAL was perfectly convenient in Rick's program, it is
>perfectly convenient to use DISPLAY.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Where do you see me "resist using it" ? 

In the paragraph above, where you advocate RECORD SEQUENTIAL and
DISPLAY, even though they do not conform to the OS standard. If such a
program was moved to another platform, code would have to be manually
converted. 

> I, however, have not argued,
>as you have, that there is 'only one correct way' in a dogmatic way.

I said ASCII text is the most widely used, not the only way.

>> >You seem to have a fixation that 'ASCII text file' is inseparable from
>> >and defined by 'LINE SEQUENTIAL' and vice versa.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>that doesn't make the two congruent (except in your mind).

The only definition of LINE SEQUENTIAL is in compiler manuals. The
Micro Focus version, below, says format follows the operating system
standard. In the case of Unix and Windows, that's variable length with
a terminator. In other words, ASCII text.

" the file is treated as consisting of fixed-length records, each
containing one line of data. The records are stored with trailing
spaces removed. The definition of a line of data varies among
different operating systems. Some terminate line records with one or
both of the carriage-return and line-feed characters, and some pad out
as fixed length records. Your COBOL system, therefore, always produces
files which are compatible in this respect with the editor software in
any operating system. "

>> I meant they think 'file transfer' is about downloading from a Web
>> site. For once, they're right.
>
>If your users have to get involved with file transfer by downloading
>from a web page then they may be poorly served.

They are not my (captive) users, they are the general public running
Web browsers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T13:12:16-07:00

```
On Sun, 24 Oct 2004 19:48:48 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
>news:bntmn01ba71iuv5aiu6fltvo8pia277jc8@4ax.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>'comma delimited' files" questions

I did as you suggested and was surprised to find 'problems' coming,
not from dumb users as expected (they all have Excel), but rather from
programmers using older languages with poor string handling such as
Delphi, SAS, APL, LISP and Mathematica. I would have thought
'professional programmers' would have more facility with older
scripting languages like awk and perl, which easily handle the task. 

Cobol's UNSTRING seems designed for the task. Too bad it doesn't
handle quoted strings.
```

- **Date:** 2004-10-25T02:47:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vkon0tgomaj80u59int6kqpq298ok8sao@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<0epin0p8155dsc5eqao2d9s49fhr8cvlqt@4ax.com> <NFfed.3161893$ic1.325550@news.easynews.com> <t78jn0ljbdpon6kqao42td963riaf3v8rc@4ax.com> <3Zled.3175587$ic1.326382@news.easynews.com> <c7kkn09vrs3uli27n81acsdgmmqk64jc6b@4ax.com> <AWCed.3812446$6p.636571@news.easynews.com> <up4mn0dd1h3d8o92vqkaimf59dblcpuvsa@4ax.com> <hIFed.3820586$6p.637682@news.easynews.com> <bntmn01ba71iuv5aiu6fltvo8pia277jc8@4ax.com> <nJTed.3267645$ic1.334258@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Sun, 24 Oct 2004 19:51:47 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>growing (and - in some "standards" - required)

ISO-10646 is a superset of ISO-646, indicated by their designation
similarity. As you pointed out, they standardize code set rather than
file format. 

I don't know whether XML defines file format. I'd guess it does not,
instead deferring to prior standards, which in this case are de facto.
That means records are still delimited by Cr and/or Lf.

Your point is well taken .. Unicode is the future code set standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-24T23:33:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410242233.1e1de2cc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<0epin0p8155dsc5eqao2d9s49fhr8cvlqt@4ax.com> <t78jn0ljbdpon6kqao42td963riaf3v8rc@4ax.com> <3Zled.3175587$ic1.326382@news.easynews.com> <c7kkn09vrs3uli27n81acsdgmmqk64jc6b@4ax.com> <AWCed.3812446$6p.636571@news.easynews.com> <up4mn0dd1h3d8o92vqkaimf59dblcpuvsa@4ax.com> <hIFed.3820586$6p.637682@news.easynews.com> <bntmn01ba71iuv5aiu6fltvo8pia277jc8@4ax.com> <nJTed.3267645$ic1.334258@news.easynews.com> <7vkon0tgomaj80u59int6kqpq298ok8sao@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> I don't know whether XML defines file format. I'd guess it does not,
> instead deferring to prior standards, which in this case are de facto.
> That means records are still delimited by Cr and/or Lf.

XML is not in 'records' at all.  It is irrelevant if there are control
codes or not, these are merely for human convenience where lf move to
the noxt line on the terminal or printer and cr returns to column 1
and tab moves to the next stop point.

The concept of a 'record' in XML is defined by the tags and takes no
accounts of how this is in display 'lines' or not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T12:18:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cljji7$rm7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<0epin0p8155dsc5eqao2d9s49fhr8cvlqt@4ax.com> <NFfed.3161893$ic1.325550@news.easynews.com> <t78jn0ljbdpon6kqao42td963riaf3v8rc@4ax.com> <3Zled.3175587$ic1.326382@news.easynews.com> <c7kkn09vrs3uli27n81acsdgmmqk64jc6b@4ax.com> <AWCed.3812446$6p.636571@news.easynews.com> <up4mn0dd1h3d8o92vqkaimf59dblcpuvsa@4ax.com> <hIFed.3820586$6p.637682@news.easynews.com> <bntmn01ba71iuv5aiu6fltvo8pia277jc8@4ax.com> <nJTed.3267645$ic1.334258@news.easynews.com> <7vkon0tgomaj80u59int6kqpq298ok8sao@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:7vkon0tgomaj80u59int6kqpq298ok8sao@4ax.com...

> ISO-10646 is a superset of ISO-646, indicated by their designation
> similarity. As you pointed out, they standardize code set rather than
> file format.

Are you sure that's the appropriate conclusion to be drawn from the
designation similarity?

I observe that the rules about what sorts of signs are to be used to
identify shipboard ventilation systems (ISO 644) doesnt' strike me as being
an obvious predecessor to the specifications for the hardness classes of
washers in screw and washer assemblies (ISO 10644).  Nor do the
specification of information interchange formats for dates and times (ISO
8601) serve as a meaningful predecessor to the specifications and test
methods for micaceous iron oxides for paints (ISO 10601) or as a direct
successor to the specification of test parameters for friction and wear by
sliding for plastics (ISO 6601).

From these examples, and from my own experience, I believe the only
appropriate conclusion I could safely draw from the fact that both ISO 646
and ISO 10646 deal with character sets is that ISO issued numbers for some
ten thousand documents between them.

True enough, I don't have a copy of ISO/IEC 646 or ISO/IEC 10646 handy to
verify this, but I'd be curious to know if the latter ISO document (or any
other ISO document) supports the contention that the latter *number* was
assigned specifically to indicate its relationship to the former *number*.

I would be really, really surprised if either ISO 10646, or any other ISO
document for that matter, so stated, because I don't believe that's the way
the mechanisms for the assignment of ISO document numbers works.  The
similarity between the number assigned to ISO 646 and that assigned for ISO
10646 is, I am convinced barring evidence to the contrary, purely
happenstance.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
and ISO 10646.  I realize that this is probably not the normal case.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-23T11:39:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <cl5r85$ate$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ndln2mem78r97@corp.supernews.com> <9JHJrpiuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <10nf9km25av146a@corp.supernews.com> <1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> On 21 Oct 2004 22:41:53 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> sequential file is "perfectly standard ANS85".

That is irrelevant to the issue.  Rick had used entirely standard
ANS85 Cobol syntax and produced an entirely standard text file that
was perfectly acceptable to whatever he wanted to feed it into.

Other systems may not be able to do that and thus may need to use
non-standard (ANS85) vendor extensions, if they have those available.
 
> The Cobol standard does not specify file formats. They are
> implementer-defined, an euphemism for non-standard. The only standard
> we have is ASCII (despite Micro Focus' attempt to screw it up by
> inserting nulls). Thus, line sequential files are standardized whereas
> ANS85 sequential are non-standard.

You are confused.  'Line sequential' is also just as
'implementor-defined' as every other file format.  Fujitsu defaults to
using specified record lengths, MF defaults to null insertion, others
may not have it at all.  Terminators may be combinations of CR and/or
LF.

If Rick reliably gets the output that _he_ requires then the program
works.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-23T22:59:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <cl5r85$ate$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ndln2mem78r97@corp.supernews.com> <9JHJrpiuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <10nf9km25av146a@corp.supernews.com> <1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com>`

```
On 23 Oct 2004 11:39:15 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

>> The Cobol standard does not specify file formats. They are
>> implementer-defined, an euphemism for non-standard. The only standard
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>'implementor-defined' as every other file format. 

No, it is not implementor-defined; it is defined by ISO-646 and
industry practice. 

> Fujitsu defaults to
>using specified record lengths, MF defaults to null insertion, others
>may not have it at all.  Terminators may be combinations of CR and/or
>LF.

When the compiler produces a non-standard file by default, we find a
way to make it deliver a standard one. In the case of MF, we turn off
null-insertion; in the case of Fujitsu, we supply the record length.

For a standards-friendly forum, I'm surprised at the amount of
resistance here to a standardized file format .. one that has been
very widely used for 30 years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-23T21:59:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410232059.1a3c4fb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <cl5r85$ate$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ndln2mem78r97@corp.supernews.com> <9JHJrpiuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <10nf9km25av146a@corp.supernews.com> <1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com> <9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >You are confused.  'Line sequential' is also just as
> >'implementor-defined' as every other file format. 
> 
> No, it is not implementor-defined; it is defined by ISO-646 and
> industry practice. 

When one defines a file one does not say: ORGANIZATION ISO-646, one
says: ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL and then one gets what the compiler
writer has implemented (also depending on compiler and run-time
options).

> When the compiler produces a non-standard file by default, we find a
> way to make it deliver a standard one. In the case of MF, we turn off
> null-insertion; in the case of Fujitsu, we supply the record length.

Or, for Fujitsu use the supplied environment variable.  In Rick's case
he got exactly the format he required by other means using entirely
standard ANS85 contructs.
 
> For a standards-friendly forum, I'm surprised at the amount of
> resistance here to a standardized file format .. one that has been
> very widely used for 30 years.

I am quite surprised that you seem to completely miss the point, once
again.  What difference is there between the file that Rick wrote and
a 'text file' ? What is it about the file that he output that doesn't
fit what you call 'standard' ?

All you seem to be objecting to is that it wasn't done _your_ way, a
way that you seem to claim is the only 'correct' way.
```

```
On 23 Oct 2004 21:59:23 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>options).

Whatever the semantics, users WANT it to be 'ASCII text'. When the
compiler company supplies something else, it hears complaints until it
provides an option to give users what they want.

>> For a standards-friendly forum, I'm surprised at the amount of
>> resistance here to a standardized file format .. one that has been
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>fit what you call 'standard' ?

Nothing, but his records are all the same length. In his tab-delimited
example, description is padded with spaces. That's a waste of
bandwidth.

>All you seem to be objecting to is that it wasn't done _your_ way, a
>way that you seem to claim is the only 'correct' way.

It's not my way, it's they way millions of files are formatted every
day.
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> Nothing, but his records are all the same length. In his tab-delimited
> example, description is padded with spaces. That's a waste of
> bandwidth.

So, after all this ranting from you, the only actual criticism is that
it has a few spaces in the file.  In fact using Report Writer in that
way would give fixed length fields anyway.

This is actually an advantage, programs can read the data by using the
tabs as field separators, or, if they can't read it that way they can
use the fixed field lengths and skip the tabs.

> >All you seem to be objecting to is that it wasn't done _your_ way, a
> >way that you seem to claim is the only 'correct' way.
> 
> It's not my way, it's they way millions of files are formatted every
> day.

Your rant started when Rick used the completely ANS85 Cobol SEQUENTIAL
and created a perfectly good file exactly as required.  Your only
objection was that he didn't use the MF extension (to ANS85) of LINE
SEQUENTIAL, as you would have, to create an identical file.

Or was it just that you didn't notice how his resulting file looked.

All your whining about 'standards' is completely bogus.  The file was
completely within all you were winging about. It seems simply that you
are trying to get Rick to use LINE SEQUENTIAL.

I have great difficulty in believing that you were ever a manager of
anything, the way that you argue and rant makes you appear to be a
frustrated control freak.
```

```
>All your whining about 'standards' is completely bogus.  The file was

The message you are now reading was transported as LINE SEQUENTIAL. So
too were all your email messages. Why not write to the administrators
of those systems to say they're using a "completely bogus" format?
Tell them it's all my fault. They might thank you for the
enlightenment although it's far more likely they'll think you a kook. 

>I have great difficulty in believing that you were ever a manager of

>frustrated control freak.

If your goal is to convince others, feelings are inadequate, you'll
have to present facts and logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-24T23:45:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10notpkthqf4a9e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<9JHJrpiuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <10nf9km25av146a@corp.supernews.com> <1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com> <9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410232059.1a3c4fb@posting.google.com> <envmn0h0ga3ppmnami2927quk24p2lp460@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410241104.3b6fd98b@posting.google.com> <62mon0tg9bduajpohfjkvkeunkaqhcr1a1@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:62mon0tg9bduajpohfjkvkeunkaqhcr1a1@4ax.com...
> On 24 Oct 2004 12:04:56 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
[snip]
> >Your rant started when Rick used the completely ANS85 Cobol SEQUENTIAL
> >and created a perfectly good file exactly as required.  Your only
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> spaces would have been truncated.

My point was to demonstrate how Report Writer may be
used to accomplish a particular task and to show that that
particular use could be widely adapted. The program was
simple. Compare that with the following, which I wrote
earlier today. This is not widely adaptable.

And to be clear, I ran this program to create the byte-stream
file, imported that file into a table in Access, exported from
Access the same table to a text file, and used WinDiff to
compare the files. They were identical.

-----
       identification division.
       program-id. tab-test.
       environment division.
       configuration section.
       special-names.
           symbolic characters
               separator-tab is 10
               line-feed is 11
               carriage-return is 14.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select item-file assign to "item.dat"
               organization is indexed
               access is sequential
               record key is item-id.
       data division.
       file section.
       fd item-file.
       01 item-record.
         05 item-id pic x(6).
         05 item-description pic x(30).
         05 item-price packed-decimal pic s9(5)v99.
         05 item-quantity comp pic 9(5).
       working-storage section.
       01 item-text-name pic x(9) value "item.txt" & x"00".
       01 access-mode pic x comp-x value 2.
       01 deny-mode pic x comp-x value 0.
       01 device pic x comp-x value 0.
       01 file-handle pic x(4).
       01 file-offset pic x(8) comp-x value 0.
       01 flags pic x comp-x value 0.
       01 byte-count pic x(4) comp-x value 0.
       01 string-buffer pic x(80).
       01 string-length comp-5 pic s9(4) value 0.
       01 work-id pic x(6).
       01 work-description pic x(30).
       01 work-price pic +9(5).99.
       01 work-quantity pic 9(5).
       01 text-length comp-5 pic s9(4) value 0.
       01 text-source comp-5 pic s9(4) value 0.
       01 number-work pic x(24).
       procedure division.
       begin.
           open input item-file
           call "CBL_CREATE_FILE" using item-text-name
               access-mode deny-mode device file-handle
           perform until exit
               read item-file
                   at end exit perform
               end-read
               move spaces to string-buffer
               perform create-string
               move string-length to byte-count
               call "CBL_WRITE_FILE" using file-handle
                   file-offset byte-count flags string-buffer
               add byte-count to file-offset
           end-perform
           call "CBL_CLOSE_FILE" using file-handle
           close item-file
           stop run.

       create-string.
           *> accepts string-buffer
           move 1 to string-length  *> used as position until end

           move function length (item-id) to text-length
           move item-id (1:text-length)
               to string-buffer (1:text-length)
           add text-length to string-length

           move separator-tab to string-buffer (string-length:1)
           add 1 to string-length

           perform get-description-length
           move item-description (1:text-length)
               to string-buffer (string-length:text-length)
           add text-length to string-length

           move separator-tab to string-buffer (string-length:1)
           add 1 to string-length

           move item-price to work-price
           move work-price (1:) to number-work
           perform compress-number
           move number-work (1:text-length)
               to string-buffer (string-length:text-length)
           add text-length to string-length

           move separator-tab to string-buffer (string-length:1)
           add 1 to string-length

           move item-quantity to work-quantity
           move work-quantity (1:) to number-work
           perform compress-number
           move number-work (1:text-length)
               to string-buffer (string-length:text-length)
           add text-length to string-length

           move carriage-return & line-feed
               to string-buffer (string-length:2)
           add 2 to string-length

           subtract 1 from string-length
           .  *> returns string-buffer string-length

       get-description-length.
           move function length (item-description)
               to text-length
           perform until text-length < 1
               if item-description (text-length:1) not = space
                   exit perform
               end-if
               subtract 1 from text-length
           end-perform
           .  *> returns text-length

       compress-number.
           *> accepts number-work
           *> uses text-length as position until end
           move 1 to text-length
           move 1 to text-source
           perform until exit
               evaluate number-work (text-source:1)
                 when "-"
                   add 1 to text-length text-source
                 when "+"
                 when "0"
                   add 1 to text-source
                 when "."  *> one "0" before decimal point
                   move "0" to number-work (text-length:1)
                   add 1 to text-length
                   exit perform
                 when other
                   exit perform
               end-evaluate
           end-perform
           if number-work (text-source:1) = space
                                   *> occurs with integer 0
               move "0" to number-work (text-length:1)
               add 1 to text-length text-source
           end-if
           perform until number-work (text-source:1) = space
               move number-work (text-source:1)
                   to number-work (text-length:1)
               add 1 to text-length text-source
           end-perform
           move spaces to number-work (text-length:)

           subtract 1 from text-length
           .  *> returns number-work text-length
       end program tab-test.
-----
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-24T23:57:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410242257.2dae7ae6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9JHJrpiuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com> <9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410232059.1a3c4fb@posting.google.com> <envmn0h0ga3ppmnami2927quk24p2lp460@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410241104.3b6fd98b@posting.google.com> <62mon0tg9bduajpohfjkvkeunkaqhcr1a1@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> The message you are now reading was transported as LINE SEQUENTIAL. 

Completely bogus.  These are _not_ 'LINE SEQUENTIAL' at all.  They
were not written by COBOL programs.  They _are_ ASCII text, but in
fact are HTML and the content need not have CR or LF codes at all,
line breaks are indicated by <br> or other means.

It may be that writing a file using a LINE SEQUENTIAL organization
may, but need not, result in a completely standard ASCII text file,
but so can SEQUENTIAL organization or DISPLAY on many systems.

> So too were all your email messages.

Again the content may, or need not, have CR and/or lf and may be
entirely HTML.

> and say they're using a "completely bogus" format?

It is your argument that is completely bogus, not the formats.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T13:40:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<chupn05s1k1mvapc1tj4a6tm6vge4lqevk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com> <9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410232059.1a3c4fb@posting.google.com> <envmn0h0ga3ppmnami2927quk24p2lp460@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410241104.3b6fd98b@posting.google.com> <62mon0tg9bduajpohfjkvkeunkaqhcr1a1@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410242257.2dae7ae6@posting.google.com>`

```
On 24 Oct 2004 23:57:55 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>line breaks are indicated by <br> or other means.

The NNTP standard says:

"all USENET news messages must be formatted as valid Internet mail
messages, according to the Internet standard RFC-822. "
http://www.cse.ohio-state.edu/cgi-bin/rfc/rfc1036.html

And RFC-822 says:

"Each header field may be represented on exactly one line  con-
sisting  of the name of the field and its body, and terminated
by a CRLF; "
http://www.cse.ohio-state.edu/cgi-bin/rfc/rfc0822.html

>> So too were all your email messages.
>
>Again the content may, or need not, have CR and/or lf and may be
>entirely HTML.

A relatively recent change.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T02:48:15+00:00

```
What a technicality.

>Robert,
>  Just admit that "line sequential" (like record sequential) is what the 
>implementor WANTS it to be (in response to THEIR users - for THAT environment).

This message and all the others on Usenet are line sequential. So are
all the messages in your mailbox. 

Whether you like it or not, there IS a standard in the real world ..
and it's not record sequential.
```

- **References:** `<1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com> <9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410232059.1a3c4fb@posting.google.com> <envmn0h0ga3ppmnami2927quk24p2lp460@4ax.com> <rETed.1763538$yk.290585@news.easynews.com> <imton0tj1dc1iqjt1vs99a773q69oopvp2@4ax.com> <10np0gtjv1p8s35@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 25 Oct 2004 00:31:39 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>source code; not interoperability.

That's true for Cobol. I was referring to a broader scope such as ASC
X12. For instance, the EDI Standard said 11 years ago:

"This FIPS PUB recognizes the reality that some families of EDI
standards were developed to provide solutions to immediate needs, and
that inclusion of the goal of universality in their development would
have unacceptably delayed their availability. However, a future is
envisioned in which the benefits of universality outweigh the sunk
costs in specialized solutions, leading first to cooperation among
standards developers, then to harmonization of standards, and
eventually to a single universally accepted family of EDI standards. "
http://www.itl.nist.gov/fipspubs/fip161-2.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T12:54:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10nqc24l3tu30e2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com> <9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410232059.1a3c4fb@posting.google.com> <envmn0h0ga3ppmnami2927quk24p2lp460@4ax.com> <rETed.1763538$yk.290585@news.easynews.com> <imton0tj1dc1iqjt1vs99a773q69oopvp2@4ax.com> <10np0gtjv1p8s35@corp.supernews.com> <7jvpn01ahr4cmrum6p9q252hp1cjh3r5hv@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:7jvpn01ahr4cmrum6p9q252hp1cjh3r5hv@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 25 Oct 2004 00:31:39 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >  Just admit that "line sequential" (like record sequential) is what
the
> >> >implementor WANTS it to be (in response to THEIR users - for THAT
> >environment).
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's true for Cobol. I was referring to a broader scope [...]

Mr Wagner, I have no doubt that a broader scope
is involved; but, perhaps, it is time for Hume's law
'that conclusions about what ought to be cannot be
deduced from premises stating only what was, what
is, or what will be--and the other way about.' It may
be that premises outside of 'what was, what is, and
what will be' are beyond one's immediate
understanding.

Mr Wagner, it appears to me that you have reached
a conclusion about what 'line sequential files' ought
to be and, used as premises, claims about 'what was,
what is, or what will be'. [I did not use 'only' because
I have not examined every exchange.] Hume's law
suggests that the conclusion is invalid.

Wouldn't it be wonderful if one could use
ORGANIZATION IS format-name to take care
of those nasty data interchange problems! Or, maybe
this was addressed, in COBOL 2002, by the FORMAT
clause, which I never really understood.

FDIS, page 285-287, 13.16.22 FORMAT clause,
"The FORMAT clause specifies that records written to
the file are to be formatted to an external representation
during the output process and records read from the file
are to be converted from the external format into internal
representation during the input process. The external
representation is suitable for presentation or printing."
...
GR10, "The implementor shall specify the representation
produced as a result of processing the FORMAT clause,
including any control information that may be necessary
for presentation or printing. This representation may be
the same as internal representation."

Could this mean tab-delimited, CSV, etc., as specified
by the implementor?
```

```
1960, when Cobol was 'strategic', which survived to an age when the
original reasons to be incompatible no longer make competitive sense.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-10-26T18:52:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clmkeq$a5l$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<envmn0h0ga3ppmnami2927quk24p2lp460@4ax.com> <sqhrn09gs0elcqken87grt3hbkth4jm3ps@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410260956.2182075d@posting.google.com> <227tn0h1dpgi6t90mvlo57m3glj8bsj801@4ax.com>`

```
In article <227tn0h1dpgi6t90mvlo57m3glj8bsj801@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <nospam-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 26 Oct 2004 10:56:07 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>zeros.

Mr Wagner, I have seen times - more than a few - when a receiving party 
specifies, clearly and unambiguously, that alphabetic fields will be fixed 
length and padded with trailing spaces and numeric fields will be fixed 
length and padded with leading zeroes.  If your embarassment would cause 
you to fail to comply to spec it would be in the client's best interest to 
retain someone of a less sensitive nature.

>The receiver might ask 'Who wrote this thing, a Cobol
>programmer?'.

Were it otherwise the receiver might ask 'Who wrote this thing, a 
know-it-all who thinks he is too good to comply with specs?'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-27T02:10:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410270110.7d1e7f8d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<envmn0h0ga3ppmnami2927quk24p2lp460@4ax.com> <rETed.1763538$yk.290585@news.easynews.com> <imton0tj1dc1iqjt1vs99a773q69oopvp2@4ax.com> <10np0gtjv1p8s35@corp.supernews.com> <7jvpn01ahr4cmrum6p9q252hp1cjh3r5hv@4ax.com> <10nqc24l3tu30e2@corp.supernews.com> <m5iqn0h2sdd9q8t43m4cnaqpf14k9498ed@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410251444.1572d55e@posting.google.com> <sqhrn09gs0elcqken87grt3hbkth4jm3ps@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410260956.2182075d@posting.google.com> <227tn0h1dpgi6t90mvlo57m3glj8bsj801@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <nospam-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> I'd be embarrassed to send a file with trailing spaces and leading
> zeros. The receiver might ask 'Who wrote this thing, a Cobol
> programmer?'. 

If the data is correct and readable then I doubt that the user would
even notice. In fact if they did then the fixed sizes would make it
much more obvious how the data was arranged in columns.

Typically, CSV, and especially TSV, data when viewed as simple text
in, say, Notepad or using TYPE, is difficult to relate into columns,
they simply do not line up.

If viewed using a mechanism that recognised the field separators then
it would make no difference at all.

Thus the fixed field sizes make it much more flexible in how it is
viewed, make it more readily understandable by simple inspection, and
allow it to be imported in more different ways.

What _I_ would be embarressed about is if I made as many factual
errors, factoids, and misinformation as you seem quite happy to
publish.

For example about 90% of what you said about 64bit, use of RELATIVE
files, and dozens of others.


> But in answer to your question, the format is usable.

Thank you.
```

```
>  ...             Or, maybe
> this was addressed, in COBOL 2002, by the FORMAT
> clause, which I never really understood.

No, that's not what the FORMAT clause is about.  FORMAT works with SELECT
WHEN, q.v.

The 2008 draft is expected to have variable-length elementary items that
includes the ability to specify that the length of an elementary item is
determined at execution time by a numeric prefix or by a trailing delimiter,
which is what I think is being asked for here.  See J4/04-0151 for the most
recent draft of this proposal.

    -Chuck Stevens


> FDIS, page 285-287, 13.16.22 FORMAT clause,
> "The FORMAT clause specifies that records written to
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T13:33:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410251233.20ffe11c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com> <9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410232059.1a3c4fb@posting.google.com> <envmn0h0ga3ppmnami2927quk24p2lp460@4ax.com> <rETed.1763538$yk.290585@news.easynews.com> <imton0tj1dc1iqjt1vs99a773q69oopvp2@4ax.com> <10np0gtjv1p8s35@corp.supernews.com> <7jvpn01ahr4cmrum6p9q252hp1cjh3r5hv@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >Mr Wagner, the 'promise', as you put it, was portable
> >source code; not interoperability.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.itl.nist.gov/fipspubs/fip161-2.htm

And where exactly does this specify that 'files' require cr/lf as line
terminators in any combination ?

And where exactly does it specify that these exchanges must be written
using 'ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL' (OLS) as you have been asserting
?

In your mind you seem to have a fixation that ASCII _requires_ line
terminators of cr/lf and that because EDI (of various types) and web
pages etc is usually ASCII then they also _require_ cr/lf and that
LINE SEQUENTIAL is the only way to get this.

Those of us with a wider experience understand what is actually
required somewhat better, and seem to know how to do this in more
varied ways.

And we certainly know not to try to access text files with "RANDOM".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-24T15:05:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410241405.7ae48b10@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <cl5r85$ate$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ndln2mem78r97@corp.supernews.com> <9JHJrpiuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <10nf9km25av146a@corp.supernews.com> <1ngfn0ddj297ctd0it16f3abip2dojtqfe@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410212141.576d6cc3@posting.google.com> <im6in05frv6o0k6jiedsatjall65e0b86p@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410231039.4c5dc94c@posting.google.com> <9nnln0pieo7moso3nruk7ellqjuu1hg8ki@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >You are confused.  'Line sequential' is also just as
> >'implementor-defined' as every other file format. 
> 
> No, it is not implementor-defined; it is defined by ISO-646 and
> industry practice. 

LINE SEQUENTIAL is implementor defined in ANS85 (ie current
compilers). There is nothing in the standard that defines LINE
SEQUENTIAL as anything, let alone as ISO-646 and whatever 'industry
practice' you think applies.


> When the compiler produces a non-standard file by default, 

.. as defined by the implementor.

> we find a
> way to make it deliver a standard one. In the case of MF, we turn off
> null-insertion; in the case of Fujitsu, we supply the record length.
 
Or _we_ use other mechanisms to get the files that we require, such as
DISPLAY to stdout, or SEQUENTIAL files with CR/LF control codes.
```

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote

> .. and I changed the file organization to
> sequential, so that tabs will not have a leading nul character.

There is a run time switch 'N' which cotrols this. You could add '-N'
to the run command or to the COBSW= environment line, or change the
setting using the supplied CALL x"91" 13/14 routines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T16:30:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9JHM$xQ9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <9J4yzBgeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cl5r85$ate$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
.    On  20.10.04
  wrote  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  cl5r85$ate$1@peabody.colorado.edu
  about  Report enhancements


>>   COBOLs solution for this has been the REPORT WRITER, and for many
>> decades already. Unfortunately not implemented in every compiler.

HB> I don't think this is what we want.

   Maybe, but it is what I would offer, albeit with some changes...


HB> Even in the old days, REPORT WRITER made things about twice as easy
HB> to write reports as without it.   Twice as easy wasn't sufficient.

   I guess there was no way to do it even easier...


HB> But what we want (in Mainframe land) nowadays is to have a mainframe
HB> CoBOL program to create a report which the users can view on their PC
HB> and then decide to print on their printers set up the way they want,
HB> and in the format they want.    PC CoBOL has this same requirement -
HB> unless each computer has its separately compiled copy of the main
HB> program REPORT WRITER doesn't do enough.

   The solution is an HTML or XML document, or a CSV file.

   So why not specifying the type of output in the RD, i.e. REPORT  
DESCRIPTION? Or do that even dynamically?

   The idea came when I looked at the code examples from CobolScript  
(http://www.cobolscript.com), where HTML tags are embedded literally  
in the string being DISPLAYed, like this:

000097  PERFORM VARYING counter FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL counter > 8
000098    DISPLAY `<TR BGCOLOR="lightblue">`
000099    DISPLAY `<TD>TCPIP-HOSTENT-ADDRESS(`  &  counter  &  `): </TD>`
000100    DISPLAY `<TD>`  &  TCPIP-HOSTENT-ADDRESS(counter)  &  ` </TD>`
000101    DISPLAY `</TR>`
000102  END-PERFORM.

   Why not telling the compiler, or the run-time system that I want to  
have a HTML document, which would then result in prefixing every  
DETAIL group with a '<LI>', and generating an HTML table with  
'<TABLE>', '<TR></TR>', and <TD></TD>' tags, when the COBOL definition  
is subject to an OCCURS clause? Why should the source program care  
about such details as those tags? Leave that to the compiler and run- 
time system, and avoid errors.

   And why not leaving it to the compiler and run-time sytem to insert  
unit-separators between items and line-separators (LF, CR, or LF/CR)  
when I want to create a CSV file?

   This is the direction which I would like the next standad to  
enhance the REPORT WRITER. I have not yet had a look into the  
standardizing work on XML, though.

HB> Often I create files that are read in by a PC spreadsheet or
HB> database.    The users have the expertise to do whatever they want
HB> from that stage.

   Sure, so let's make it easier to create such an output using COBOL.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Seine Bï¿½cher waren alle sehr nett, sie hatten auch sonst wenig zu tun. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report enhancements

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T14:54:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9JLTANBPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <9J4yzBgeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cl5r85$ate$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
.    On  20.10.04
  wrote  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  cl5r85$ate$1@peabody.colorado.edu
  about  Report enhancements


HB> But what we want (in Mainframe land) nowadays is to have a mainframe
HB> CoBOL program to create a report which the users can view on their PC
HB> and then decide to print on their printers set up the way they want,
HB> and in the format they want.    PC CoBOL has this same requirement -
HB> unless each computer has its separately compiled copy of the main
HB> program REPORT WRITER doesn't do enough.
HB>
HB> Often I create files that are read in by a PC spreadsheet or
HB> database.    The users have the expertise to do whatever they want
HB> from that stage.
HB>
HB> When one office wants to change its report, the program doesn't have
HB> to be changed and tested by all offices.

   I would distinguish two types of printouts:

1) Those which actually represent a transaction and are bound to a  
specific form, like invoices, bank forms, payment slips, tax forms,  
social security forms, and things like that. I would not want some  
department to tamper with the form of those documents.


2) Informational reports about, e.g. merchandise stock levels, sales  
figurs per period and region, price lists, and so on. These were at  
least in part necessary in earlier times when the whole data  
processing was done by batch runs. Nowadays I would recommend not to  
produce printed reports, but to look up those data online in the  
central database, and to print it out on one's own account and format  
as you please, and only if deemed necessary.

   Even in the old days, those reports were not always really needed.  
There are enough stories where the DP department did not produce one  
of those reports, and nobody noticed it...


   And coming back to the means that the programming language COBOL  
provices, I think that even a report presented as an HTML page could  
be produced by the REPORT WRITER feature, to which I would recommend  
an enhancement which inserts all the necessary HTML tags to structure  
the document, which can then be visualized using some CSS style sheet,  
just as you explained. And then printed, or not...


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Da liegen nun die Kartoffeln und schlafen ihrer Auferstehung entgegen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

<a id="t-06c8f4640f"></a>
## Thread: MF: Application Server files/directories with rights — 2 msgs

### MF: Application Server files/directories with rights

- **From:** randi_clausen@ins.state.il.us (R. Clausen)
- **Date:** 2004-10-08T12:15:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<44515df0.0410081115.7413a2c0@posting.google.com>`

```
I have an industry user that executes a MF Cobol program and is
getting the error 'ASLM: Semaphore failure'.  I know that Application
Server files need to be sent with the program and this has been done. 
I believe the problem is that she is only given rights on her
machine's C drive on selected directories as she needs it.  The
network admin installed the program and AppS AND is successful in
executing my program.  Therefore, can someone tell me all the
directories that the Application Server uses along with required
rights.  The network admin can then make the appropriate modifications
thereby allowing the user to execute the program.  Thank you for any
help!
```

#### ↳ Re: Application Server files/directories with rights

- **From:** "Neil Hayes" <Neil.Hayes@za.syspro.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-11T06:55:50+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ckd3re$vb$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`
- **References:** `<44515df0.0410081115.7413a2c0@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

The ASLM: Semaphore failure could be due to the user not having a valid
license for the Applicatiojn Server runtime. It's all very well distributing
the Application Server but making sure that each user has a valid license is
now upon has all to ensure that we configure the environment correctly.

If the environemnet is a mapped drive environment then user should have the
variable 'ASLMFNET ' pointing to the license database, if the user is
Client/Server each with their own installation of the runtime then run the
'APPTrack.exe' utility.... see ASLM in the help, follow to Application
Server Licensing Messages and look at related topics.

Look at the utilities ASLMPCLOCATE.EXE, ASLMPCSILENT.EXE and APPTRACK.EXE.

Um....one other note...if the user is a web based user ensure that the Micro
Focus Registry entry has permissions, I've had spurious events happening
here. On some sites after n days for working 100% (Who knows could be 30
days) the web user suddenly requires access read permissions to the registry
in order to validate the license....just a thought!

Regards

Neil


"R. Clausen" <randi_clausen@ins.state.il.us> wrote in message
news:44515df0.0410081115.7413a2c0@posting.google.com...
> I have an industry user that executes a MF Cobol program and is
> getting the error 'ASLM: Semaphore failure'.  I know that Application
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> help!
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

```
I know this totally off-topic, but when I saw that product name - Fugawi - I
could not help but fondly remembering Johnny Carson's time as host of the
Tonight show.

Mr. Carson had been using the name "Fugawi"  as that of a ficticious
American Indian tribe which had vanished, prompting the question, "Where the
Fugawi?"

At least he used it until the NBC censors (and IIRC, the name of the chief
censor according to Mr. Carson was Priscilla Goodbody) made him stop.

Now - thirty years later - the name resurfaces as a (trademarked!) name for
navigation and map software.... amazing how what goes 'round comes around.
```

```
"dwbauer" <dwbauer@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:%P%9d.204571$Np2.107551@bignews4.bellsouth.net...
> Hey Rookie, Thanks for the Help!
> I just knew there was someone there who knew how to read.
> What do you do for a living?  masturbate? or are you a queer from the left
> coast?

Robert was making a joke. (I found it pretty amusing...<G>).

You are frazzled with this problem so you didn't think it was funny.

Hopefully, the abuse above made you feel better, but on the whole, it is
best avoided...

Now, let's look at your problem:

I believe (from your other post) you are talking about NetCOBOL version 4?

Have you tried setting breakpoints in the program editor?
(pull down the Debug menu option and set breakpoint.)

Once it "breaks" you should be able to animate it.

Are you using the Project Manager? (It will do a lot of lowlevel stuff
behind the scenes when you build the project that might solve the problem
for you. I noticed you said you had linked with DEBUG, this makes me think
you are doing it all manually; the Project Manager will link with the
required options automatically when you build the project.

In case I have read this wrong and you are talking about PowerCOBOL, I had a
similar problem recently where something would not debug. It turned out that
the procedure I wanted to debug was not being executed... because I forgot
to trap the event that would've activated it...<G>

If you really can't get this to work, mail me the project privately.

Pete.
```

## Thread: how to compare two time — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### how to compare two time

- **From:** "William Cai" <William.Cai@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-10T16:28:42+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ckarrv$2q2f$2@mail.cn99.com>`

```
Hi, List

Another question. I am trying to calculate the difference between two time
and find the way to implement it is to complex. Kindly please read below
program and give me some suggestion. Thanks a ton! :-)

        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID. CSIMPCL.
        AUTHOR. TUXEDO QA TEAM.
        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        CONFIGURATION SECTION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01 SYSTEM-TIME-START.
           05 SYSTEM-TIME-START-HH  PIC X(2) VALUE 'HH'.
           05 SYSTEM-TIME-START-MM  PIC X(2) VALUE 'MM'.
           05 SYSTEM-TIME-START-SS  PIC X(2) VALUE 'SS'.
           05 SYSTEM-TIME-START-00  PIC X(2) VALUE '00'.
        01 SYSTEM-TIME-END.
           05 SYSTEM-TIME-END-HH  PIC X(2) VALUE 'HH'.
           05 SYSTEM-TIME-END-MM  PIC X(2) VALUE 'MM'.
           05 SYSTEM-TIME-END-SS  PIC X(2) VALUE 'SS'.
           05 SYSTEM-TIME-END-00  PIC X(2) VALUE '00'.
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        START-CSIMPCL.
          ACCEPT SYSTEM-TIME-START FROM TIME.
          ... ...
          ACCEPT SYSTEM-TIME-END FROM TIME.

      * how to calculate it?



BTW, comparing C/C++/Java, COBOL language is too ... :-(

Thank you again.

-William
```

#### ↳ Re: how to compare two time

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-10-10T10:06:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9IcyWJmPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<ckarrv$2q2f$2@mail.cn99.com>`

```
.    Am  10.10.04
schrieb  William.Cai@gmail.com (William Cai)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  ckarrv$2q2f$2@mail.cn99.com
  ueber  how to compare two time

WC> Another question. I am trying to calculate the difference between two
WC> time and find the way to implement it is to complex. Kindly please
WC> read below program and give me some suggestion. Thanks a ton! :-)


WC>         WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
WC>         01 SYSTEM-TIME-START.
WC>            05 SYSTEM-TIME-START-HH  PIC X(2) VALUE 'HH'.
WC>            05 SYSTEM-TIME-START-MM  PIC X(2) VALUE 'MM'.
WC>            05 SYSTEM-TIME-START-SS  PIC X(2) VALUE 'SS'.
WC>            05 SYSTEM-TIME-START-00  PIC X(2) VALUE '00'.
WC>         01 SYSTEM-TIME-END.
WC>            05 SYSTEM-TIME-END-HH  PIC X(2) VALUE 'HH'.
WC>            05 SYSTEM-TIME-END-MM  PIC X(2) VALUE 'MM'.
WC>            05 SYSTEM-TIME-END-SS  PIC X(2) VALUE 'SS'.
WC>            05 SYSTEM-TIME-END-00  PIC X(2) VALUE '00'.


     I would write the data declaration differently:

           01 System-Time.
              02 Start-Time.
                 03  hour            pic 99.
                 03  minute          pic 99.
                 03  second          pic 99V99
    *> note that this includes the hundredths of seconds
    *> as decimal fraction
              02 end-time.
                 03  hour            pic 99.
                 03  minute          pic 99.
                 03  second          pic 99V99

           77 Elapsed-Seconds        pic 9(6)V99.


     BTW, you can't calculate with alphanumeric items.


WC>         PROCEDURE DIVISION.
WC>         START-CSIMPCL.
              ACCEPT Start-Time FROM TIME
WC>           ... ...
              ACCEPT End-Time FROM TIME
WC>
WC>       * how to calculate it?




   There are various ways to do it, depending in which unit you want  
to have the time: seconds, minutes:seconds, hours:minutes:seconds

   For the first case you could write it in one single statement,  
using what you learned in school: a minute has 60 seconds, an hour 60  
minutes or 3600 seconds:

   COMPUTE Elapsed-Seconds =
       (    hour of end-time * 3600
        + minute of end-time * 60
        + second of end-time )
         -
       (    hour of start-time * 3600
        + minute of start-time * 60
        + second of start-time)


   In case you need the Elapsed-Seconds for display, you should  
replace the "V" in its picture clause by the decimal separator (either  
dot or comma).

   Please note, that you did not take into account that midnight might  
be inbetween the two times; you would need check the date, too. In  
that case, Elapsed-Seconds may need to have more digits.

   Maybe your compiler provides a non-standard function to access the  
system time in, say, milliseconds, then you might use that, when  
portability is not so much of an issue.

WC> BTW, comparing C/C++/Java, COBOL language is too ...

   data oriented? easy?



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Regierung aus dem Volke, durch das Volk und fï¿½r das Volk"
   - Abraham Lincoln, Ansprache in Gettysburg, 19.11.1863
"... was in die revolutionï¿½re Sprache von heute ï¿½bersetzt heiï¿½t:
eine Regierung von Arbeitern, durch Arbeiter und fï¿½r Arbeiter"
                                  - Fidel Castro, November 1994
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how to compare two time

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-10-10T22:11:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Id1aHjuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<ckarrv$2q2f$2@mail.cn99.com> <9IcyWJmPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
.    Am  10.10.04
schrieb  L.Willms@JPBERLIN.de (Lueko Willms)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  9IcyWJmPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de
  ueber  Re: how to compare two time


   How to calculate time elapsed?

   Another way:


LW>            01 System-Time.
LW>               02 Start-Time.
LW>                  03  hour            pic 99.
LW>                  03  minute          pic 99.
LW>                  03  second          pic 99V99
LW>     *> note that this includes the hundredths of seconds
LW>     *> as decimal fraction
LW>               02 End-time.
LW>                  03  hour            pic 99.
LW>                  03  minute          pic 99.
LW>                  03  second          pic 99V99
        *> we need those elements with a sign, so that they
        *> can represent negative intermediate results
                  02 Time-Diff.
                     03  hour            pic S999.
                     03  minute          pic S999.
                     03  second          pic s99V99.
                  02 Time-Diff-Display.
                     03  hour            pic Z9.
                     03  filler          pic X value ":".
                     03  minute          pic 99.
                     03  filler          pic x value ":".
                     03  second          pic 99,99.
           *> the previous line assumes "DECIMAL-POINT IS COMMA".
LW>
LW>            77 Elapsed-Seconds        pic 9(6)V99.
LW>

WC>>         PROCEDURE DIVISION.
WC>>         START-CSIMPCL.
LW>               ACCEPT Start-Time FROM TIME
WC>>           ... ...
LW>               ACCEPT End-Time FROM TIME
WC>>
WC>>       * how to calculate it?


LW>    There are various ways to do it, depending in which unit you want
LW> to have the time: seconds, minutes:seconds, hours:minutes:seconds

   Alternatively to the single COMPUTE yielding a total of seconds  
elapsed, one could try the following:

     MOVE CORR End-Time TO Time-Diff
     SUBTRACT CORR Start-Time FROM Time-Diff
     *> the result is now in hour, minute, and second of Time-Diff

     IF second of Time-Diff NEGATIVE
     THEN
        ADD      60 TO second of Time-Diff
        SUBTRACT  1 FROM minute of Time-Diff
     END-IF
     IF minute of Time-Diff NEGATIVE
     THEN
        ADD      60 TO minute of Time-Diff
        SUBTRACT  1 FROM hour of Time-Diff
     END-IF
     IF hour of Time-Diff NEGATIVE
     THEN
       DISPLAY "We must have passed midnight!"
       DISPLAY "You should have asked for the date, too."
     ELSE
       MOVE CORR Time-Diff TO Time-Diff-Display
       DISPLAY "Elapsed time is " Time-Diff-Display
     END-IF
     .



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Die Interessen der Nation lassen sich nicht anders formulieren als unter
dem Gesichtspunkt der herrschenden Klasse oder der Klasse, die die
Herrschaft anstrebt."            - Leo Trotzki         (27. Januar 1932)
```
