- **Message-ID:** `<10mrau14cmas99f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <yCZ8d.21081$jj2.997385@news20.bellglobal.com> <us89m0proesf23qfn5druc9snmk9nl9n72@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410062301.4f3c7ff8@posting.google.com> <R7Gdner4pp05ovjcRVn-jA@giganews.com> <ck3td7$2778$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10mqriusnd0dvac@corp.supernews.com> <ckjtcr$rhn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10mr4fs562njjd3@corp.supernews.com> <bLgbd.2618771$ic1.269412@news.easynews.com>`

```
Bill, this is no biggie. Mr Stevens mentioned, today, 'that
multiplication is inherently commutative', I recalled an earlier
post concerning 'order of evaluation of conditions', I recalled
the post which mentioned 'rearranging', this led me to 'order
of execution' of arithmetic operations.

Essentially, I was looking at how far down the standard
reaches versus how far up implementor-defined reaches.
You response answered the question.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bLgbd.2618771$ic1.269412@news.easynews.com...
> Rick,
>    There was (as I recall) an interpretation request and answer for the
'85
> Standard (which I believe applies to NATIVE arithmetic in the '02
Standard).
> The conclusion (NOT in these words) was
>
>   "implementor defined means the implementor may do WHATEVER they want
....
> Although customer reaction PROBABLY requires a 'reasonable' evaluation".
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the implementor *must* evaluate A / B first - even in NATIVE arithmetic,
but
> this does NOT mean that they can't take the "precision" of either C or the
> receiving field into consideration when doing the evaluation. FURTHERMORE,
it is
> possible (i.e. conforming - even if not ever done) for the vendor to
ALWAYS
> evaluate "A / B" as "1" and to store the results in a temporary field
which is
> used in the evaluation of  "* C".
>
> The bottom-line (from my perspective) is that *all* arithmetic expressions
in
> the '85 Standard and when ARITHMETIC IS NATIVE is in effect with the '02
> Standard really are TOTALLY implementor defined and that they (the
implementor)
> can do just about ANYTHING they want in this evaluation.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> > > rearranging the components of an arithmetic expression to cause it
to
> >> > > produce the most precise intermediate results '85 or prior
standards,
> > or
> >> > in
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> Correct citation, but there's a later reference that makes clear that
it
> >> applies rigorously only to *standard* arithmetic and not *native*
> >> arithmetic.
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Date:** 2004-10-14T12:58:18+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<m4rsm0t434f1rgt80tugg20ab1btkuvnsa@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <f4H9d.15711$VJ2.12336@fe40.usenetserver.com> <b3638c46.0410091754.731d9cb3@posting.google.com> <9IcxnTKPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1097624606.I6uuWMyrhNtiti36JCKqEg@teranews> <217e491a.0410122328.2592c872@posting.google.com> <1097664884.j5FvRHbcSWnMxPBr3b2oAQ@teranews> <217e491a.0410131039.6ead5035@posting.google.com>`

```
On 13 Oct 2004 11:39:21 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Most of the problems are due to Windows starting as a graphics library
>on DOS and having to provide an environment that grew from there. It
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>design, that allows processes to gain root access.

SYSTEM on Windows is not as omnipotent as root on Unix. That can be
used against malfeasors. Suppose you have a virus that drops the same
file every time installed or executed. Here's how to stop it. Delete
the file. Log on as something other than Administrator. Copy a
zero-length file to the bad guys' file name. Go to Explorer, right
click, Security. Uncheck the Inherit box at the bottom, then uncheck
all permissions. You now have a file that cannot be overwritten or
modified in any way by SYSTEM. Next time the virus runs, you'll see an
error box or event log entries (via eventvwr) pointing to the bad guy.
In any case, the file will not be installed. 

>You should try some to find out why others use them. Tabbed browsing,
>cookie and javascript management by site, no popups, make the internet
>much more accessible.

IE has cookie, javascript and popup management by site .. if people
took the trouble to use it. It is in Security Zones. I recommend
setting the default (Internet) to High and putting your favorites in
Trusted.
```

<a id="t-6a07fd1d11"></a>
## Thread: Useful Utility or Not? — 1 msgs

### Useful Utility or Not?

- **From:** mr.kyle.t@netzero.com (Kyle)
- **Date:** 2004-10-14T09:14:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6f09cae.0410140814.304c3feb@posting.google.com>`

```
I've been out of CoBOL professionally since Y2K relegated me and my
Wang programming job to relational database and SQL coding.  But the
new releases of CoBOL for networking platforms caused me to resurrect
a personal project.  I call it "Tables by Design" and I'm wondering if
it has any commercial potential.  It can provide greater flexibility
during application design resulting in less maintenance programming
after installation.  Or so I believe.

By tables, I'm referring to those CoBOL "Search"able structures of
data which use "occurs" and "indexed by" in their definition, are for
the most part static in content and whose main function is the support
and definition of data within dynamic or primary files.  As a
programmer, I've spent significant hours coding in support of these
data structures. With this utility, that task has been eliminated.

Using the utility, I can create up to 999 table layouts of up to 999
entries in each table.  In the typical "data dictionary" way, up to 24
fields or 150 characters per table entry, can be defined within each
table.  Generic programs are used to enter data, display and report
table entries.  Working-Storage and Procedure Division load paragraphs
are created and a single Select, FD and Open statement are all that is
needed to load as many tables as a program may need to use. 
Documentation about fields is a by-product of the table definition
process and is available, even at the end-user level, when making data
entries.

A unique feature, perhaps, is the ability to use table '921' in table
'922' to establish relationships between and/or validation of the data
being entered to individual fields.  Some of the tables in the
application I wrote using the utility, are used exclusively to
validate data in other tables.

The utility is currently a Window's application written with Fujitsu's
PowerCOBOL 7.0 using the CoBOL85 scripting language option.  The file
structures are traditional flat, ISAM files though I believe the
concept could work with a database structure as well.

I hope some of you will let me know what you think and if there is a
comparable utility already on the market.  If you wish to correspond
privately, my e-mail is mr.kyle.t@netzero.com.  Thanks for reading.

Kyle
```

<a id="t-aff09bacc0"></a>
## Thread: Can Cobol benefit from space.... — 2 msgs

### Re: Can Cobol benefit from space....

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-14T19:10:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MaAbd.101227$a41.38205@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9IpR0wp9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0410130804.14a6551b@posting.google.com> <Lcnbd.735170$gE.42255@pd7tw3no> <9IpR0wp9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:

>.    Am  14.10.04
>schrieb  jjgavan@shaw.ca (James J. Gavan)
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Originally, prior to Michael's suggestion (back in '99), I was thinking 
in terms of getting the index positioning per column - see following 
comments from source, I've added periods as it didn't tab correctly in 
this message) Note the 'Sort Key' is the name and not the mnemonic code 
preceding it :-

*> This program prints an alphabetical listing of description records.
*> The Print Table has MaxLines and MaxColumns. The alphabetically
*> sequenced Collection, (by name) is put in the table "down" and
*> "across" the page. If the Collection contains only 11 records then
*> they would be printed as follows :-
*>
*>               Alphabetical List of - Systems/Services
*>
*> AI...Air (Injection).......................IW.. Injection 
Water............  O...Oil
*> C.. ..Condensate.........................ICO. Inlet Crude Oil  
............PW..Produced Water
*> FG...Flare Gas............................ISO...Inlet Sour 
Oil............... RG.. Residual Gas
*> HMG Heating Medium (Glycol).. IA.....Instrument Air

Problem I had taking each print line and the indexing positions from the 
SortedCollection  was :-

Line 1 - 1, 5, 9
Line 2 - 2, 6, 10
Line 3 - 3, 7, 11
Line 4 - 4, 8,  0

OK you could use just ONE print line and a  formula to work out where to 
'plonk' each description, so that the equivalent to Line 3 above 
contains records for index positions 3, 7 and 11 from the Collection, 
but for simplicity, (and when you come back to program maintenance), it 
appeared easier to use a  two-dimensional print-table approach.

The relevant code :-

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION (Global).

       78 MaxLine                       value 58.
       78 MaxColumn                     value 3.
       01 ws-ActualPageSize             pic x(4) comp-5.

- Create a SortedCollection
- Read ISAM sequentially, (or Table with Cursor) and add elements to 
Collection
- invoke MyCollection "size" returning ls-numberOfElements - so now I 
*know* total size
- If ls-NumberOfElements = 355 then I know I have two full pages plus 
last page with 7 elements, occupying three lines..

       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "P30-print-detail".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 ls-CurrentSize                  pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-LinestoPrint                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-Remainder                    pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-TempSize                     pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division.
        move ws-CollectionSize to ls-TempSize
        perform until ls-TempSize = zeroes

            Evaluate true
                when ls-TempSize = zeroes
                     EXIT METHOD
                when ls-TempSize < ws-ActualPageSize
                     move ls-TempSize to ls-CurrentSize
                when other
                     move ws-ActualPageSize to ls-CurrentSize
            End-Evaluate

            divide MaxColumn into ls-CurrentSize
                  giving ls-LinestoPrint remainder ls-Remainder

            if  ls-Remainder <> zeroes
                add 1 to ls-LinestoPrint
            End-if

            invoke self "P50-print-lines" using ls-LinesToPrint
            subtract ls-CurrentSize from ls-TempSize
        End-perform

        if  ContinueProgram
            invoke os-PrintFile "page-throw" using     PageNumber
                                             returning ws-ErrorCode
        End-if

        if CancelProgram
           invoke self "error-exit"
        End-if

       End Method "P30-print-detail".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "P50-print-lines".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------
       Working-Storage section.
       01 PrintTable.
          05                            pic x(26) value spaces.
          05 PrintLine occurs MaxLine.
             10 PrintColumn occurs MaxColumn.
                15                      pic x(09) value spaces.
                15 Detail-key           pic x(04).
                15                      pic x(05) value space.
                15 Detail-name          pic x(35).

       Local-storage section.
       01 c                               pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 k                               pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 l                               pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 n                               pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-length                       pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-record                       pic x(des-record-length).
       01 ls-string                       object reference.

       Linkage Section.
       01 lnk-LinesToPrint              pic x(4) comp-5.
       Procedure Division using lnk-LinesToPrint.

        initialize PrintTable, n
        move length of ls-Record to ls-Length
        perform varying c from 1 by 1 until c > MaxColumn
             after L from 1 by 1 until L > lnk-LinestoPrint

                if    L > lnk-LinestoPrint
                      Exit Perform

                else  add 1 to n

                      if     n > ws-CollectionSize
                             Exit Perform

                      else   invoke os-Collection "at"
                                using n returning ls-string
                             invoke ls-string "getValuewithSize"
                                  using ls-length returning ls-record
                             move ls-record(ws-KeyStart:4)
                                    to Detail-key (L, C)
                             move ls-record(1:ws-NameLength)
                                    to Detail-Name (L, C)
                      End-if
                End-if
        End-perform

        move zeroes to LineCount PageNumber
        invoke self "P900-print-heading"

        move length of PrintLine(1) to PrintBufferLen
        perform varying k from 1 by 1 until k > MaxLine
            move PrintLine(k) to PrintBuffer
            invoke os-PrintFile "write-and-newline"
                   using PrintBuffer by value PrintBufferLen
                   returning ws-ErrorCode
        End-perform

        if CancelProgram
           invoke self "error-exit"
        End-if

       End Method "P50-print-lines".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------

Perhaps above could be more efficient - but it works !

When I look at your original code and latest change,  I could be wrong, 
but I don't think it allows for the format I'm showing from the source 
code - and from Kellie's original message it appears to be the 
sequencing that she wants. (?).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Can Cobol benefit from space....

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-10-15T06:37:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ItZ9OHuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0410130804.14a6551b@posting.google.com> <9IpR0wp9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <MaAbd.101227$a41.38205@pd7tw2no>`

```
.    Am  14.10.04
schrieb  jjgavan@shaw.ca (James J. Gavan)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  MaAbd.101227$a41.38205@pd7tw2no
  ueber  Re: Can Cobol benefit from space....

JJG>        End Method "P50-print-lines".
JJG>        *>---------------------------------------------------------
JJG>
JJG> Perhaps above could be more efficient - but it works !

   Interesting. I'm not familiar with OO COBOL, and I don't have a  
compiler capable for that (only the free Fujitsu 3.0)

JJG> When I look at your original code and latest change,  I could be
JJG> wrong, but I don't think it allows for the format I'm showing from
JJG> the source code - and from Kellie's original message it appears to
JJG> be the sequencing that she wants. (?).

   I'm not sure what you mean.

   The program (parts) which I had prevented would fill the last page  
columnwise, i.e. possibly just the first column nearly to the bottom  
of the page, and leave the three other columns empty.

   I do know now that this could be changed by adjusting the leaps  
thru the table of items to be placed on one page to the result of the  
division of the number of available items by the number of columns.

   This also allows for dynamic allocation of the number of columns  
and number of lines per page.

   If I had a 2002-conforming compiler, I would do that using the  
REPORT WRITER and a detail item with a "OCCURS DEPENDING ON number-of- 
columns" and a "VARYING subscript-number", with the SOURCE for those  
items being an item in the table of items collected for filling a  
page.

   In the print-out process I would just set those subscripts resp.  
indexes to the indexes into the table of items to be printed.

   As a work-around with a compiler which does not support those  
enhancements to the REPORT WRITER in COBOL-2002, one could use several  
alternative DETAIL items with different number of columns (I mean  
logical columns) and use different GENERATE statemens in a EVALUATE  
selection based on the number of (logical) columns on that page.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Regierung aus dem Volke, durch das Volk und fï¿½r das Volk"

"... was in die revolutionï¿½re Sprache von heute ï¿½bersetzt heiï¿½t:
eine Regierung von Arbeitern, durch Arbeiter und fï¿½r Arbeiter"
                                  - Fidel Castro, November 1994
```

### IE, Microsoft, et al (was: Creative Javascript writing (was: OFF Topic)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-14T20:50:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OEBbd.2674783$ic1.274852@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <f4H9d.15711$VJ2.12336@fe40.usenetserver.com> <b3638c46.0410091754.731d9cb3@posting.google.com> <9IcxnTKPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1097624606.I6uuWMyrhNtiti36JCKqEg@teranews> <ckje2f$9ar$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1097704741.AniPCyaqLBQO+x7MgmVBfQ@teranews> <ckm1d3$96a$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:ckm1d3$96a$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 13-Oct-2004, "Peter E.C Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Reminds me a lot of COBOL and IBM vs ANSI in the late 60's and 70's.  There was 
a de jure Standard and there was a de facto standard (for many - but certainly 
NOT all environments).

The "problems" with Windows, IE  (and Microsoft products in general) reminds me 
a lot of how people "blamed" COBOL for Y2K, i.e the most commonly used product 
(language) was blamed for the problem.

I am *not* trying to say that Microsoft and/or Windows and/or IE is 
"blameless" - only that if they weren't so  VERY common, then
  A) people (hackers) wouldn't try and exploit their deficiencies
  B) people (users and NON-users) wouldn't blame them for widespread problems
```

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0410141102.3219aea8@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E.C Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote
>
<snip>

>  > BTW, Richard, can you confirm what you posted about the Fujitsu Run
> Time
> > licence?  I'm interested to know where you got that and whether it is,
in
> > fact, the case. I had a look through the documentation for the Version 6
> > licence but I can't find anything that says that. However, I know you
are
> > meticulous in these things so I'd just like it confirmed (or not). Was
it
> > hearsay, or do you have a document?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I never had a box. They just mailed me the CD in a jewel case.

It does say "rights to ship" and that is what I have been doing.

> If the run-time was public domain then you, or anyone, can distribute.
>  It isn't, the license (the one they issued to me) is quite clear that
> Fujitsu is only selling the 'right to use' and that all material is
> owned by Fujitsu and protected by Copyright etc.
>

That is certainly a valid interpretation, but I think it is arguable. If I
buy the rights to something (rights to ship) then it doesn't have to be
public domain for me to exercise that right. If one of my clients uses a
component that requires that runtime, then it is a necessary part of what I
have supplied. It comes down to whether "the right to ship" is transferable
and we can agree that from the literature, it doesn't appear to be.

However, I'm not sure whether it would be enforceable (or desirable from
Fujits's point of view, to enforce it). Here's my argument:

The component cannot work without the RTS, so, in order for the component to
be fit for its purpose, it MUST use the RTS.  Fujitsu give us no choice in
this; you cannot compile to Native Code. It can therefore be argued that the
component and its RTS are co-dependent and should be considered as a single
entity. (This is certainly how the user sees it.).  If I assign the rights
to that entity to a third party, then I believe I have done nothing immoral
(even though it might be technically illegal). I can't provide the component
without the RTS, and I cannot legally restrict my customer from
incorporating the component in his own software and distributing it, once he
has bought the rights to it.

I suspect that not too many people are writing COBOL components for use by
others anyway (that was were we came in on this topic) and it is possible
that Fujitsu are aware of the situation but don't take action because it
would be to their detriment to do so. (The few of us who are actually doing
it, would simply move to another language...I don't think any of the COBOL
vendors need to be putting more nails in COBOL's coffin...)


> In fact the license is specific in restricting the rights to a single
> user on a single computer ... for its own internal data processing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I took that to mean the compiler and ancilliary programs. It never occurred
to me that it might also include the Run Time.

> The only 'prior written consent' that I have for version 6 is that on
> the back of the box which grants that right to the purchaser, but the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> If you look at <a
href="http://www.adtools.com/License/index.htm">http://www.adtools.com/Licen
se/index.htm</a>
> you will see where distribution rights are granted, though I am not
> sure if this page constitutes 'prior written agreement' in itself.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hahaha!  Me neither. I guess I'll reconsider the whole deal. The problem
wouldn't arise with DotNET, of course... This might be a spur to move...

Thanks for the information and links.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-10-15T05:30:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cko5cd$smg$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<8acim0li65l1q1arcm9dvcki6148onujj4@4ax.com> <1097795327.LA6FJgCn/9X+3kg1oBZKpg@teranews> <ckn1im$h69$1@panix5.panix.com> <1097798255.I5y+u0FzeWsAorKe/tDIIw@teranews>`

```
In article <1097798255.I5y+u0FzeWsAorKe/tDIIw@teranews>,
Peter E.C Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:ckn1im$h69$1@panix5.panix.com...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Frenchmen stalking your every post...<G>

Have a care, Mr Dashwood... speak of a nuisance and it might appear.

>
>BTW, do you have any explanation for the derivation of "poopie-head"? I've
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>OK...perhaps it is appropraite here after all <G>

Bingo... exactly that, it is my attempt to show name-calling as the High 
Artform it usually exists as... y'see, having spent time in uniform I've 
both called and been called things I wouldn't write on a latrine wall and 
despite the skill I've seen, at times, employed:

Drill Sergeant: 'JONES!' 
New Recruit: 'SIR!' 
DS: 'Look at the mess you've made of this... Jones, you're like a 
fornicating fly, you know that?' 
NR: 'NO, SIR!' 
DS: 'Well, you sure as faeces are... know why you're like a fornicating 
fly, Jones?' 
NR: 'NO, SIR!' 
DS: 'You're like a fly, Jones, because all you do is eat faeces and bother 
people.'

... name-calling can usually be reduced to a simple 'You're a 
poopie-head!' 'No, *you're* a poopie-head... and a Big Smelly, too!'

(As for the derivation of 'poopie' I am not certain... I recall reading, 
someplace, that it came into American use via the Dutch immigrant 
community but my memory is, admittedly, porous.)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Computer Statement Usage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-15T15:02:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ckoopv$kml$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<8acim0li65l1q1arcm9dvcki6148onujj4@4ax.com> <1097795327.LA6FJgCn/9X+3kg1oBZKpg@teranews> <ckn1im$h69$1@panix5.panix.com> <1097798255.I5y+u0FzeWsAorKe/tDIIw@teranews> <cko5cd$smg$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

On 15-Oct-2004, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> ... name-calling can usually be reduced to a simple 'You're a
> poopie-head!' 'No, *you're* a poopie-head... and a Big Smelly, too!'
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> community but my memory is, admittedly, porous.)

It's interesting that the common word used to describe feces was considered
inappropriate - not because of the word so much as the subject.   In modern
society, when the subject isn't circumvented, leftover concepts of the word have
caused it to be replaced by "poop".

Dog poop and poopie diapers are acceptable nowadays.

Oddly enough, it is perfectly acceptable for a CEO of a company to mention that
an idea a subordinate comes up with is B. S.   In fact, there are some classes
for prospective high management women telling them to do so.   It's OK as long
as you don't use the S word to mean what it really means.
```

```
Why not? You're not restrained in judging me.

>> one irresponsible posting from me
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>postings would be that much better than "irresponsible" in the first place.

Thank you for supplying evidence of rudeness.

>> or FOUR from you
>
>Four postings from me or four irresponsible postings from me?

I wrote "or FOUR from you intended to make me uncomfortable." You
separated the qualifier from the sentence, then used 'or FOUR from
you' as a straw man that had me saying your postings were
irresponsible. I didn't say they were irresponsible; I said they were
insulting and unmannerly.

>> intended to make me uncomfortable
>
>No, that was not my intent.  

I think it was your intent. As Winston Churchill said "Even a dog
knows the difference between being tripped over and deliberately
kicked."

>I would further defend, in detail, each of
>these four postings, but my time right now is limited as I am preparing from
>a week-long absence from the office (and from ready access to
>comp.lang.cobol) contributing, I hope, to the future of, and the further
>development of, the COBOL language.

You go, Chuck. Do the good work.

>If these four messages stimulated you to recognize that maybe your behavior
>hasn't been exactly stellar, I would hope and pray that you would regard
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>foster daughter".

Brushing aside "you're making me uncomfortable" means you haven't
learned anything about manners.  Perhaps you think manners are about
formulaic riturals such as Please and Thank You rather than genuine
concern for the other's comfort.

>> Preaching civility while acting unmannerly is self-contradictory.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>once said.

I'm unfamiliar with Twelve Step programs. I know they're vaguely
religious. Do they teach hypocrosy as a way of life, as other
religious precepts do?  Do they tell you to lecture about civility
while simultaniously practicising rudeness?
```

```
there", this just seemed to be a bit more "out there" than usual.  :)

> <<In fact, because it was isolated, one could make the argument that he

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Hmm...  I don't think I really *did* much to anyone else, except maybe 
my parents, who were disappointed for a while.  And, of course, I regret 
making them feel that way.  But, I didn't accrue any debt, I didn't 
father any children then leave their mother high and dry, I didn't get a 
police record...  It would be nice to still have all the money I spent 
being foolish, though - had I invested it, I'd have a nice little nest 
egg going.
```

### Re: Creative Javascript writing

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-14T21:16:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bqGbd.50721$VJ2.9155@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1097704741.AniPCyaqLBQO+x7MgmVBfQ@teranews>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <f4H9d.15711$VJ2.12336@fe40.usenetserver.com> <b3638c46.0410091754.731d9cb3@posting.google.com> <9IcxnTKPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1097624606.I6uuWMyrhNtiti36JCKqEg@teranews> <ckje2f$9ar$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1097704741.AniPCyaqLBQO+x7MgmVBfQ@teranews>`

```
Peter E.C Dashwood wrote:
> 
> I just don't feel passionate about this one way or the other. MicroSoft have
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> products... <G>)

I still use their products - I have no choice at work (and our web-based 
configuration management system is heavily reliant on ActiveX controls, 
so I have to use IE for that), and there are still a few things here at 
home (scanning, FJ5) that I haven't been able to make work under Linux 
yet.  However, I run Firefox on both Windows and Linux because of the 
features.

  - Tabbed browsing - instead of having a ton of windows opened up, you 
can open tabs.  As an example, I usually show people either a Google 
search or a list of columnists (such as the one at 
http://www.townhall.com ), and instead of just clicking the link, you 
can go down the list, right-clicking and picking "Open in New Tab". 
Then, go across the tabs, read, close, etc.  When changing to another 
application, I only have to Alt-Tab past 1 instance of that application.

  - Out-of-the-box intelligent popup blocking - The default settings (at 
least the last time I installed it from scratch) block all popups.  But, 
it's smart about it.  If you click a link that opens a new window, that 
still works.  You can tailor it by site.

  - Cookie management - I have it set to prompt me for each cookie that 
is attempted to be stored.  I have three options for each cookie - Deny, 
Accept for Session, and Accept.  There is also a box that will use that 
response for all cookies from that domain.  So, cookie "ASP_SessionID" 
from the domain I'm visiting will probably get an "Accept for Session" 
with the all cookies box checked.  A cookie from 2o7.net will get 
denied.  Once it's trained, it's great - no popups and no hassles.

I'm not particularly worried about security in IE - I've never had any 
trouble with it (although my wife has run afoul of some spyware in the 
past).  I use Firefox for the features.  :)

>>My interest here is whenever I see someone publicly proclaim that he
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> thundering...

I didn't have a problem with your take on it - especially in light of 
the fact that it's an volunteer effort.  :)  (Some might say I'm biased, 
though...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creative Javascript writing

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-15T16:23:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410151523.5249d6bd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <f4H9d.15711$VJ2.12336@fe40.usenetserver.com> <b3638c46.0410091754.731d9cb3@posting.google.com> <9IcxnTKPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1097624606.I6uuWMyrhNtiti36JCKqEg@teranews> <ckje2f$9ar$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1097704741.AniPCyaqLBQO+x7MgmVBfQ@teranews> <bqGbd.50721$VJ2.9155@fe40.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0410142202.7f378e67@posting.google.com> <38uvm0t7678kug79hce5dkfotjvkqd723p@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >>   - Tabbed browsing - ....

> >I use 'middle-click' or hold the Ctrl key down and left-click.
> 
> IE6 does the same if you hold shift and left-click.

More Wagner factoids - it looks like a fact but isn't.  

IE does not do tabbed browsing so it _cannot_ "do the same".  

Shift left-click opens a new window and annoyingly puts it on top of
what you were looking at while it loads.  Using tabbed browsing avoids
all the annoyances of new windows.  Ctrl-left-click or middle-click
put the linked page in a new tab (in the current window) and leaves it
in background while it loads so that you can select pages to load
while you continue reading and then easily change to those when you
are ready.
```

```
On 15 Oct 2004 16:23:00 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>what you were looking at while it loads.  

"While it loads" is not a concern with a wideband connection. I used
to love that 'pop-under' feature in Opera when using dialup, in the
last century. Now, it's no longer needed.

To return to the previous window, hit alt-Tab.

>Using tabbed browsing avoids
>all the annoyances of new windows.  Ctrl-left-click or middle-click
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>are ready.

There's little functional difference between windows and tabs. Windows
let one return to a page with one click on the toolbar (pre-XP)
whereas tabs often require two clicks. 

Tabs is not a new idea. Opera, Lotus Notes, Eudora and others have
been doing it for years. I find all those non-standard tabbars
disconcerting.
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> "While it loads" is not a concern with a wideband connection. 

It is not always the client end that controls the speed, you can't get
pages faster that the server sends them out.  In any case I still
prefer to have new pages load in background while I continue to read
without distraction.  If I want a new window of to load in current
window I can do that too.

> To return to the previous window, hit alt-Tab.

That is still annoying to me, I dislike having to return from the new
window, tabbed is far better for what I want, and is still not 'the
same thing'.
 
> There's little functional difference between windows and tabs. Windows
> let one return to a page with one click on the toolbar (pre-XP)
> whereas tabs often require two clicks. 

I've never known a tab to require two clicks.
 
> Tabs is not a new idea. Opera, Lotus Notes, Eudora and others have
> been doing it for years. 

As has Mozilla, which just goes to show how far behind IE is.

> I find all those non-standard tabbars disconcerting.

Some can cope with change, some can't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creative Javascript writing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-10-17T06:50:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9J0oC$leflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<f4H9d.15711$VJ2.12336@fe40.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0410151523.5249d6bd@posting.google.com> <1o83n05dmkk4tthp9s791639nf0aaqmis9@4ax.com>`

```
.     Am  16.10.04
 schrieb  robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests (Robert Wagner)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  1o83n05dmkk4tthp9s791639nf0aaqmis9@4ax.com
   ueber  Re: Creative Javascript writing

RW> There's little functional difference between windows and tabs.
RW> Windows let one return to a page with one click on the toolbar
RW> (pre-XP) whereas tabs often require two clicks.

  Tabs have the advantage of not creating new windows, so one does not  
have the toolbar cluttered with windows. The disadvantage is that only  
the title of the active tab is shown in the window-list, all the  
others are hidden.

  I currently have 11 windows open with Mozilla, with a total of 28  
Tabs; having all those tabs open as independent windows would be more  
difficult to manage. Actually two out of these 11 windows are the  
Download manager and the Javascript console, so there are only 9  
actual HTML-Windows open.

  BTW, this is on OS/2, where I do have a real window list on request.

  Sure, it happens that I forget one of those tabs and open the same  
HTML-page again in another window... there are not advantages without  
backdrops.


RW> Tabs is not a new idea.

  Sure, but not available (yet) in MS-IE.

RW> . I find all those non-standard tabbars disconcerting.

  Nobody is forced to use it. I like the tabs.

  And sure, I also have MS-IE available, both because a number of  
pages do not work as intended with Mozilla, and also to test Web pages  
with I prepare myself.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de

Ketten."                         - Karl Marx           (1. Januar 1870)
```

```
>  Tabs have the advantage of not creating new windows, so one does not  
>have the toolbar cluttered with windows. The disadvantage is that only  
>the title of the active tab is shown in the window-list, all the  
>others are hidden.

>RW> Tabs is not a new idea.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

XP solves the clutter problem by showing a single icon per
application. Clicking it gives a picklist of instances. Selection is
standardized for all applications, but at the expense of an extra
click.
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-16T22:41:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3t83n05q9qf9qs0ok0da8ca0dit72q2tgp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<f4H9d.15711$VJ2.12336@fe40.usenetserver.com> <b3638c46.0410091754.731d9cb3@posting.google.com> <9IcxnTKPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1097624606.I6uuWMyrhNtiti36JCKqEg@teranews> <ckje2f$9ar$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1097704741.AniPCyaqLBQO+x7MgmVBfQ@teranews> <bqGbd.50721$VJ2.9155@fe40.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0410142202.7f378e67@posting.google.com> <38uvm0t7678kug79hce5dkfotjvkqd723p@4ax.com> <%O_bd.60276$VJ2.6679@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Fri, 15 Oct 2004 20:28:08 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> On 14 Oct 2004 23:02:04 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>IE6 opens a tab in the same window?  :)

There are only two differences: pop-over vs. pop-under and location of
the selection bar. With windows, your demo can open either page with a
single click. With tabs, there is a 50% chance of needing a second
click. 

XP removes toolbar congestion at the expense of always requiring a
second click. I don't like that.
```

<a id="t-62e2f708be"></a>
## Thread: How to Fail at Software Development — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to Fail at Software Development

- **From:** arthur@belugalake.com
- **Date:** 2004-10-15T05:08:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4h5bm0tsjl77lnm9qalkc3fs4ao8mgik3s@4ax.com>`

```
Hello,

The book "How to Fail at Software Develoopment" is about all the
strange and funny things people do in the name of developing software.
I worked in software development for over 20 years and found silly
things that were being done in the name of software development were
being  repeated again and again. As a matter of fact, most things that
cause software projects to fail are forever  being repeated. And you
can spot the symptoms if you know what to look for.

Most people running software projects don't have much software
experience, so their inventions are usually not new. They rediscover
the same mistakes over and over again. It turned out to be a book of
very funny scenarios--some I lived through, and some were lived
through by others.

Those who don't know the history of software failure  are doomed to
repeat it. You can find the book on Amazon or wherever.

Arthur Griffith
"How to Fail at Software Development"
http://www.anchorpointbooks.com
```

```
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>

Popups are described in Sun's Java 2 Standard Edition here:

http://java.sun.com/j2se/1.4.2/docs/api/javax/swing/Popup.html

Files the applet deletes to cover its tracks were created by it. See:

http://java.sun.com/j2se/1.4.2/docs/api/java/io/File.html

You're right about an applet being unable to kill another process. It
does something that causes it to abend, like sending a packet with a
malformed header.
```

```
On 15 Oct 2004 16:41:53 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Can applets read or write files?
>
>In Java-enabled browsers, untrusted applets cannot read or write files
>at all. By default, downloaded applets are considered untrusted. There
>are two ways for an applet to be considered trusted:

1. It's running on the development machine (sandbox) 
2. It presents a valid authentication certificate. 

My virus contains a certificate referencing Thwaite, CAPE TOWN1. 

An untrusted applet can acccess files two other ways:

3. By calling a trusted applet .. on an older JRE.
4. By invoking a non-Java service. 

My virus uses WinRAR (Microsoft's PKZip) to install files. The system
log contains repeated entries reading (in reverse chronological
order):

The Event log was started.
Microsoft (R) Windows 2000 (R) 5.0 2195 Service Pack 4 Uniprocessor
Free. 
The Event log was stopped.

I did not install SP4 multiple times. Something is spoofing it.
```

```
This is interesting...how is the applet doing this thing.  Where, and 
how, is the non-Java service being loaded?

Are you really sure you are not using the special 'enhanced' JVM from 
Micro$oft?
```

- **Date:** 2004-10-20T22:20:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<09mdn0dln02ith7r4ng4vetecouhhj9of1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410122328.2592c872@posting.google.com> <uakqm099majoevu1sef75qis6obvlro2m2@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-19F7F6.09083615102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <nfsvm0t2mib3jmfg3nmnpbfa11gtqb0oae@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410151541.14349b20@posting.google.com> <drm2n01m1n293f7n165n7vhiaoa7b7csrn@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CC756C.22111116102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ggv5n0t7cqfm2vk8n7kihq9a1859b2b4ph@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6BB978.09165219102004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Tue, 19 Oct 2004 09:16:52 -0400, Joe Zitzelberger
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <ggv5n0t7cqfm2vk8n7kihq9a1859b2b4ph@4ax.com>,
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>That would be most of your problem, and your risk.

Quasi-religious love/hate for brand names is what I call 'consumer
mentality'. In other arenas, people argue endlessly and pointlessly
over the virtue vs. evilness of automobile manufacturers, cellphone
technology (TDMA vs. CDMA) and political parties. I think it's ignoble
to reduce humans to consumers who vote with their wallets or souls.
I'd rather see them involved in the creative process, debating the
real issues rather than being reduced to supporting a brand they
'love' or attacking one they 'hate'.

But for Microsoft, we'd still be looking at text-mode screens
(mainframe world) or one of a dozen incompatible GUIs (Unix, Apple).
Microsoft had the courage to bet the company on a user-friendly format
that's uniform across the board. It also had the courage to make OO
its norm rather than an 'interesting option'.  A company like that
can't be all 'evil'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: MicroSoft sucks and similar sentiments...

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T16:03:45+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1098327833.ZmqNfn2H7sWMELjUG87VPQ@teranews>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410122328.2592c872@posting.google.com> <uakqm099majoevu1sef75qis6obvlro2m2@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-19F7F6.09083615102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <nfsvm0t2mib3jmfg3nmnpbfa11gtqb0oae@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410151541.14349b20@posting.google.com> <drm2n01m1n293f7n165n7vhiaoa7b7csrn@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CC756C.22111116102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ggv5n0t7cqfm2vk8n7kihq9a1859b2b4ph@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6BB978.09165219102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <09mdn0dln02ith7r4ng4vetecouhhj9of1@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:09mdn0dln02ith7r4ng4vetecouhhj9of1@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 19 Oct 2004 09:16:52 -0400, Joe Zitzelberger
> <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Your post made me think, Robert. I really have no bias one way or the other;
I develop in MS because that's where the market is (although I concede that
may be changing). I tend to agree that there are some things we should
appreciate MicroSoft for.

But, I think it is easy to resent the "Number 1" in any field. We saw it
with IBM in the 60s and 70s when the arrogance of some of their salesmen was
really insufferable. There were a lot of shady goings on also, with very
questionable ethics. People had their careers ruined if they didn't toe the
IBM line. I know first hand of one incident where a DP Manager decided to go
with the opposition and a campaign was then mounted to question his
competence (which was beyond reproach)... "If he is competent, how come he
can't see what a superior product we have?" Questions were asked at Board
level (IBM were very fond of lunching Board members) and pressure was
brought to bear on the man in question. He was my Boss and, like most of our
team, I liked and respected him. Eventually, he caved and bought IBM, but
only after writing to the Board his reasons for NOT selecting them in the
first place and dissociating himself from the installation. (He quit as soon
as he could get another job...) This kind of thing can only breed resentment
against "number one" irrespective of how good or bad their product line is.
(I'm pleased to say, that the IBM of today is not the IBM of that
time...lessons have been learned and the Senior Management are in much
better touch with the organisation today than was the case at that time.)

In Australia this "sniping" at "number one", or even anyone who is
successful, is called "tall poppy syndrome". Apparently a Roman General was
asked what to do with the leading citizens of a town that had been captured.
He was in a garden at the time, and without saying anything, took his sword
and swiped the heads off the tallest blooms in a bed of poppies. (The
shorter ones missed the stroke).(GOOGLE is your friend...I haven't looked it
up, but I'm sure you can find it if you want the details.)

I believe the technical criticisms levelled here at MS are probably (for the
most part) fair and accurate and I respect the people posting them.(I'm
working on revamping the TW web site and if it works, I'll try doing it to
some other sites I have a hand in.)

But, like you, I see no need for emotion to come into it.

I don't think about the wealth of Bill Gates when I buy a MicroSoft product
(or when I use an Open Software product)., any more than I think about the
wealth of the owner of my local Supermarket  owner when I do the weekend
shopping. What purpose would it serve? If you resent other people being
wealthy, do what they do and try to do it better...

All of us are entitled to use whatever we want and to have an opinion about
it (or not).

"Slagging matches" do nothing to enhance or diminish brand image and just
make tedious reading in a public forum.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: MicroSoft sucks and similar sentiments...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T09:45:46-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-9E7552.09454621102004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410122328.2592c872@posting.google.com> <uakqm099majoevu1sef75qis6obvlro2m2@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-19F7F6.09083615102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <nfsvm0t2mib3jmfg3nmnpbfa11gtqb0oae@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410151541.14349b20@posting.google.com> <drm2n01m1n293f7n165n7vhiaoa7b7csrn@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CC756C.22111116102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ggv5n0t7cqfm2vk8n7kihq9a1859b2b4ph@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6BB978.09165219102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <09mdn0dln02ith7r4ng4vetecouhhj9of1@4ax.com> <1098327833.ZmqNfn2H7sWMELjUG87VPQ@teranews>`

```
In article <1098327833.ZmqNfn2H7sWMELjUG87VPQ@teranews>,
 "Peter E.C Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
<snip>
> In Australia this "sniping" at "number one", or even anyone who is
> successful, is called "tall poppy syndrome". Apparently a Roman General was
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> up, but I'm sure you can find it if you want the details.)

It is easy enough to dismiss this as sniping at the biggest kid on the 
block, but that ignores the reality that MicroSoft has done many 
seriously unethical things and some illegal things.

The lists are long and widely varied.  I'll cite a few of my favorites 
and suggest a google if you don't remember the details.

MS behaved unethically in agreeing to build a preemptive, multitasking, 
32-bit OS in conjunction with IBM.  It was to be called OS/2.  At the 
last minute, MS pulls the plug on the deal and releases something called 
WindowsNT, which look surprisingly like the joint product.  I'm a big 
fan of competition -- but I don't approve of lying as a tool in the 
course of competition.  MS should not have agreed to a joint venture in 
bad faith.

MS behaved illegally and unethically by stealing source code from an 
evaluation copy of Stacker from StacTech and repackaging it as their 
own.  StacTech offered an evaluation copy of their popular Stacker hard 
drive compression program which then 'accidentally' became DoubleSpace 
in MS-DOS 6.  MS lawyered-up and said sue us.  StacTech was broke very 
soon after and the shareholders of the defunct company received a very 
tiny $12 million settlement.

MS behaved illegally and unethically by stealing source code provided by 
Apple to help development of its Excel and Word products for Mac and 
repackaging large parts of them as Windows 1.0.  MS lawyered-up and 
dragged things out for a decade.  Finally a settlement involving MS 
paying Apple an 'undisclosed' sum, purchase of $150M in apple stock and 
product license agreements for 5 years ended the dispute.  Of course, as 
with all settlements, the party admits no guilt.

MS behaved unethically in agreeing to Suns terms for a Java license 
agreement and then quietly sabatoging the MS JVM.  They added a great 
deal of insecurity by turning handles into pointers.  And they added 
some changes to the AWT that made classes from other compilers 
unusuable.  This was another bad faith deal.

Sometimes, the sniping at a company is because they have consistantly 
behaved badly in their dealings with other companies and customers.  And 
it is just happenstance that they are also the "number one" company.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: MicroSoft sucks and similar sentiments...

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T18:51:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1ihfn0h9rclbjh2j1o22r4p11c9qvbvuge@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<joe_zitzelberger-19F7F6.09083615102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <nfsvm0t2mib3jmfg3nmnpbfa11gtqb0oae@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410151541.14349b20@posting.google.com> <drm2n01m1n293f7n165n7vhiaoa7b7csrn@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CC756C.22111116102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ggv5n0t7cqfm2vk8n7kihq9a1859b2b4ph@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6BB978.09165219102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <09mdn0dln02ith7r4ng4vetecouhhj9of1@4ax.com> <1098327833.ZmqNfn2H7sWMELjUG87VPQ@teranews>`

```
On Thu, 21 Oct 2004 16:03:45 +1300, "Peter E.C Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>But, I think it is easy to resent the "Number 1" in any field. We saw it
>with IBM in the 60s and 70s when the arrogance of some of their salesmen was
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>against "number one" irrespective of how good or bad their product line is.

Dead on. I've been in your manager's position several times. Unlike
him, I didn't cave. In one case, the issue was not IBM vs. another
brand, it was mid-sized IBM vs. big iron. We ran VSE on a smallish
mainframe running <5 MIPS. The IBM 'account manager' had a five-year
plan with target dates for upgrading to MVS and adding memory, bigger
computer, DB2, bigger disk drives, etc. I saw it in writing. Had we
followed it, our annual IT expenses would have gone from $1M to $5M.
After I told management we didn't need upgrades, I could squeeze the
same performance from anemic hardware, IBM began a campaign to derail
me personally. They didn't just take management to lunch, they also
took them to IBM-owned country clubs and gave them personalized tours
of IBM plants and research labs. 

I prevailed for the seven years I worked there, thereby adding $28M to
the bottom line but weakening my resume skillset. When I left (for
unrelated reasons), my replacement was recruited and recommended by
IBM. He quickly upgraded  hardware, went to MVS and DB2, replaced  my
financial systems with an ERP, upgraded hardware a second time, and
tripled the programming staff. Annual IT expenses went from $1M to
$7M. Performance went down. IBM sales quotas were met.. finally.

While it was happening, I resented IBM's bullying tactics so much that
I was moving applications from mainframe to PC, which  really pissed
them off. If I knew then what I know now, I would have moved to Unix.
Ed Yourdon preached Unix and C in a speech I attended ca. 1984. I
didn't give his words much credence because they sounded like
religion.

>(I'm pleased to say, that the IBM of today is not the IBM of that
>time...lessons have been learned and the Senior Management are in much
>better touch with the organisation today than was the case at that time.)

You're right about IBM, not because they gained ethics, rather because
the marketplace changed. Direct selling of big-ticket mainframes
became less profitable than selling services. I disagree with your
assessment of Senior Management. Human nature doesn't change. The
tactics IBM employed in the past are now being used by Big Five
consulting companies. I've seen it while working on projects for three
of them, and in all-day discussions with a fourth yesterday, which I
turned down. They are polished, knowledgeable, educated, smooth
talking .. and inefficient. Management does not see through the BS
because in-house projects were even less efficient.

>I believe the technical criticisms levelled here at MS are probably (for the
>most part) fair and accurate and I respect the people posting them.

I agree, but they forget to mention similar weaknesses on other
platforms. For instance, the Sun JRE had numerous security holes
plugged.

>But, like you, I see no need for emotion to come into it.

Some here, including me,  have an emotional attachment to Cobol. 

>All of us are entitled to use whatever we want and to have an opinion about
>it (or not).

Not on projects where software must be "certified". If it's not on the
Approved List, we're not permitted to install it. Even products
distributed with the operating system and supported by the computer
manufacturer.

>"Slagging matches" do nothing to enhance or diminish brand image and just
>make tedious reading in a public forum.

Repeated often enough, Microsoft bashing has gained currency in the
development community .. just as Cobol bashing did. But end-users, who
seem oblivious to it, keep buying and using MS products. 

The important question is whether the development tail can wag the
dog. When it comes to shrinkwrap software, the answer is no. In the
case of organizational systems, the answer is usually yes. By that
logic, the future of Cobol should be in consumer products (shrinkwrap)
rather than its traditional home in organizational shops.
```

```
In article <1ihfn0h9rclbjh2j1o22r4p11c9qvbvuge@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

> I agree, but they forget to mention similar weaknesses on other
> platforms. For instance, the Sun JRE had numerous security holes
> plugged.

You keep saying that, but you never cite any.  I'll save you the trouble 
-- Sun put together a list of its Java security issues for the last 9 
years.  

   http://java.sun.com/sfaq/chronology.html

There are about 30 and most of the are of the trivial variety "e.g. 
Applet might be allowed to view the clipboard without permission".  Some 
of that number are not even Java problems "e.g. WebSpoofing".

How many weeks does it take for MicroSoft to patch 30 holes?  There is 
no similarity between the two.
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T00:17:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410202317.742e2d97@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410122328.2592c872@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-19F7F6.09083615102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <nfsvm0t2mib3jmfg3nmnpbfa11gtqb0oae@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410151541.14349b20@posting.google.com> <drm2n01m1n293f7n165n7vhiaoa7b7csrn@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CC756C.22111116102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ggv5n0t7cqfm2vk8n7kihq9a1859b2b4ph@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6BB978.09165219102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <09mdn0dln02ith7r4ng4vetecouhhj9of1@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >> I am using the Microsoft JVM. 
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'love' or attacking one they 'hate'.

Actually the discussion was entirely objective. MS is best avoided for
exactly the reason that you outlined - having to spend hours trying to
rid yourself of the problems that MS let through.
 
> But for Microsoft, we'd still be looking at text-mode screens
> (mainframe world) 

There were several GUIs around when MS announced they would make one
too.

> or one of a dozen incompatible GUIs (Unix, Apple).

So MS made another one incompatible with all the others.

> Microsoft had the courage to bet the company on a user-friendly format
> that's uniform across the board. 

Windows is no more 'user friendly' than any of the other GUIs, and
changes with each release, it is hardly 'uniform'.

> It also had the courage to make OO
> its norm rather than an 'interesting option'.  

MS has always been late to catch onto trends, it then buys in one of
the leaders in the market and rushes to the front of the crowd yelling
'follow me'.

Windows was announced when Gates saw DRI demonstrating GEM at the
Comdex show. He then took 2 years to write it.

In Bill Gate's "The Road Ahead" 1st edition there was not one mention
of the Internet.  This was later revised in reprints after he actually
noticed it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creative Javascript writing (was: OFF Topic)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T18:51:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c7ufn0hrslubl7ds12oc11as1vibbd6g69@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<joe_zitzelberger-19F7F6.09083615102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <nfsvm0t2mib3jmfg3nmnpbfa11gtqb0oae@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410151541.14349b20@posting.google.com> <drm2n01m1n293f7n165n7vhiaoa7b7csrn@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CC756C.22111116102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ggv5n0t7cqfm2vk8n7kihq9a1859b2b4ph@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6BB978.09165219102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <09mdn0dln02ith7r4ng4vetecouhhj9of1@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410202317.742e2d97@posting.google.com>`

```
On 21 Oct 2004 00:17:13 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>rid yourself of the problems that MS let through.

If another platform were as dominant as MS, it would have been the
primary target of hackers and probably suffered as many break-ins.

The Web server market is dominated by Apache/Linux, not MS. Look how
often Apache has been hacked. 

>> But for Microsoft, we'd still be looking at text-mode screens
>> (mainframe world) 
>
>There were several GUIs around when MS announced they would make one
>too.

I didn't say MS invented graphics; I said they made it successful as a
user interface.

>> or one of a dozen incompatible GUIs (Unix, Apple).
>
>So MS made another one incompatible with all the others.

Windows is widely used, thereby creating a target for marketers. The
fragmentation of Unix GUIs has created a fog that attracts no one.

>> It also had the courage to make OO
>> its norm rather than an 'interesting option'.  
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Comdex show. He then took 2 years to write it.

Your second paragraph contradicts the first. MS could have bought GEM,
GEOS, Acorn, MOTIF or X. Instead, they developed a GUI in-house. 

>In Bill Gate's "The Road Ahead" 1st edition there was not one mention
>of the Internet.  This was later revised in reprints after he actually
>noticed it.

That's what happens when a 'visionary' runs out of ideas. Bill's
oversight would be understandable if v1.0 had been published before
1990, when the internet was opened to the public. But it wasn't, it
was published in 1995, when success of the internet was obvious, when
AOL had 5M members and MSN had already been launched.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creative Javascript writing (was: OFF Topic)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T23:12:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410212212.7c9d5ce@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<joe_zitzelberger-19F7F6.09083615102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0410151541.14349b20@posting.google.com> <drm2n01m1n293f7n165n7vhiaoa7b7csrn@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-CC756C.22111116102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ggv5n0t7cqfm2vk8n7kihq9a1859b2b4ph@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-6BB978.09165219102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <09mdn0dln02ith7r4ng4vetecouhhj9of1@4ax.com> <217e491a.0410202317.742e2d97@posting.google.com> <c7ufn0hrslubl7ds12oc11as1vibbd6g69@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >Actually the discussion was entirely objective. MS is best avoided for
> >exactly the reason that you outlined - having to spend hours trying to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> primary target of hackers and probably suffered as many break-ins.

That is a flawed argument.  In most cases the viruses are distributed
using _facilities_ designed into the software by MS.  For example
Outlook Express makes it very convenient for virus writers because
attachments can be _executed_, _and_ Windows will hide the full file
name (because users are too stupid and will be confused by showing
them the .exe ?) so that the actual nature of the attachment is easily
disguised.

No other EMail client does that.  I can recieve the most dangerous
payload that can be sent and my EMail does not execute it, does not
hide what it really is.

Also because Windows has inherent security flaws it is much more
readily able to save files into, say, the startup directory so that it
is loaded on a reboot. On non-Windows systems a file can only be saved
to the user's space and then it is not made executable.

> The Web server market is dominated by Apache/Linux, not MS. Look how
> often Apache has been hacked. 

Far fewer times than IIS actually, and with far less serious
consequences.

Apache may have its service disrupted, IIS gets defaced.
 
> I didn't say MS invented graphics; I said they made it successful as a
> user interface.

GEM had sold a million copies before Windows 1 had sold any at all.

What made Windows 3.0 successful was _not_ the GUI at all, but that on
a 386 in enhanced mode it could run 2 or 3 DOS boxes and so users
could run Lotus 1-2-3 _and_ WordPerfect 5.1 at the same time.  OS/2
only had one DOS box at the time.
 
Using Windows as an actual GUI came later and reluctantly.

> >MS has always been late to catch onto trends, it then buys in one of
> >the leaders in the market and rushes to the front of the crowd yelling
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> GEOS, Acorn, MOTIF or X. Instead, they developed a GUI in-house. 

Actually they didn't have to 'buy in' in the case of Windows, they
already had lots of Macintosh code laying around from their project to
put Excel on Macs. They eventually had to pay for this code when Apple
sued them and they settled.


> That's what happens when a 'visionary' runs out of ideas. Bill's
> oversight would be understandable if v1.0 had been published before
> 1990, when the internet was opened to the public. But it wasn't, it
> was published in 1995, when success of the internet was obvious, when
> AOL had 5M members and MSN had already been launched.

Even then MSN was not part of the Internet, it was a private network
for Windows users that was supposed to replace AOL and the Internet.
But Windows users were getting AOL or IBM InternetConnect and ignoring
MSN so MS eventually had to reinvent it and rush to the front of the
Internet movement yelling 'follow me'.
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >> An untrusted applet can acccess files two other ways:
> >> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://sunsolve.sun.com/search/document.do?assetkey=1-26-55100-1

"""The trusted applet must contain code that exploits this
vulnerability."""

So it can't just use any trusted applet, it must be a trusted applet
that specifically has code to defeat the security model. This makes it
no worse than trusting the applet in the first place.
 
> They're relatively safe when scripting and Java are turned off.

There are many security issues with IE that are not related to
scripting or Java, such as the URL 'feature' that makes phishing so
easy.
```

- **References:** `<41659C51.30908@optonline.net> <f4H9d.15711$VJ2.12336@fe40.usenetserver.com> <b3638c46.0410091754.731d9cb3@posting.google.com> <9IcxnTKPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1097624606.I6uuWMyrhNtiti36JCKqEg@teranews> <217e491a.0410122328.2592c872@posting.google.com> <uakqm099majoevu1sef75qis6obvlro2m2@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-19F7F6.09083615102004@knology.usenetserver.com> <nfsvm0t2mib3jmfg3nmnpbfa11gtqb0oae@4ax.com>`

```
In article <nfsvm0t2mib3jmfg3nmnpbfa11gtqb0oae@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

> On Fri, 15 Oct 2004 09:08:36 -0400, Joe Zitzelberger
> <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

For details on the default SecurityManager, see:

http://java.sun.com/j2se/1.4.2/docs/api/java/lang/SecurityManager.html

In an applet context -- that is, running via a browser -- the Popup 
window will be clearly labeled.

File access will be strictly limited and user prompting will occur -- 
e.g. a dialog that says "applet so and so wants to delete 
"C:\WIN32\KERNEL.EXE".  Allow?  Ok  Cancel"

Network access can be limited to the host when the applet was loaded 
from, but more likely it is wide open.  So a malformed packet would kill 
a process.  But a process that would die from receipt of such a packet 
might just be too stupid to live...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer (was: Can Cobol benefit from space....)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-18T13:01:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410181201.c705f4f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0410130804.14a6551b@posting.google.com> <gEfcd.117792$a41.112626@pd7tw2no> <9J0me04eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <9J4xiUHPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

>    What has X/Open to do with COBOL, 

X/Open, now the Open Group, defined a set of standards for COBOL, C,
ISAM file structure (based on C-ISAM), and various Unix bits. The
Cobol extensions include SCREEN SECTION, and CRT ACCEPT/DISPLAY as has
been used in almost all PC compilers since the 80s.  They also defined
all the LOCK-MODE extensions for file sharing with record locking.

In other words it is the X/Open standards that made PC and Unix based
multi-user COBOL systems viable.

> and why would anybody advise not  
> to use one of the very best features of COBOL?

I disagree that it is 'one of the very best', but then I don't have a
mainframe view of systems anymore.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T03:27:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0410181201.c705f4f@posting.google.com>`

- **References:** `<41758a6b.0410130804.14a6551b@posting.google.com> <9J4xiUHPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0410181201.c705f4f@posting.google.com>`

```
.    On  18.10.04
  wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  217e491a.0410181201.c705f4f@posting.google.com
  about  Re: Report Writer (was: Can Cobol benefit from space....)


r> X/Open, now the Open Group, defined a set of standards for COBOL, C,
r> ISAM file structure (based on C-ISAM), and various Unix bits. The
r> Cobol extensions include SCREEN SECTION, and CRT ACCEPT/DISPLAY as has
r> been used in almost all PC compilers since the 80s.

   Thank you for that clarification.

   I have to admit that I didn't use COBOL for production programs  
since 1994, and have used a database program to generate and write  
applications instead (VDP = Visual Data Publisher, a Windows version  
of TDB = Turbo DataBase, see www.dataweb.de). I just started soem  
probes in how far I could use COBOL for additions to the systems I  
build using VDP.

   So I'm not really familiar with this SCREEN SECTION, except that I  
have seen it in manuals which I studied recently.

   What I am missing though, and maybe I have only overlooked this  
feature hidden to my blind eyes, are event handlers for screen events,  
like entering or leaving a field, clicking or doubleclicking a field  
or a button, and such stuff, which I would expect in the DECLARATIVES  
part with an "USE ON (this-or-that-event) ".


r> They also defined
r> all the LOCK-MODE extensions for file sharing with record locking.

  If my background is of any interest -- I can't remember to have used  
Indexed or Relative files in COBOL programs, always databases, maybe  
except for the very first programs written on OS/3; sequential files  
in the context where masses of data were keyed in on data acquisition  
terminals, sorted, evaluated to invoices, and forgotten.

r> In other words it is the X/Open standards that made PC and Unix based
r> multi-user COBOL systems viable.

  Is there a place on the web where this effort can be studied?

>> and why would anybody advise not
>> to use one of the very best features of COBOL?

r> I disagree that it is 'one of the very best', but then I don't have a
r> mainframe view of systems anymore.

  Well, my COBOL experiences are mainly mainframe-based, but I do not  
think that REPORT WRITER is something mainframe-specific. It is, OTOH,  
one of the most underused feature of COBOL, and at the same time one  
of the most powerful. I guess it has been pushed aside for the same  
reasons that shop standards precluded the use of the CORRESPONDING  
phrase in MOVE, ADD, and SUBTRACT, or the COMPUTE statement. Also,  
because it was an optional module in the standards before 2002, REPORT  
WRITER was not implemented in a number of compilers, which again  
caused quite some developers to discard it in exchange for  
portability. The combined effect of that seems to have slowed down  
development of features in the REPORT WRITER module. I'm happy to see  
that it is no longer optional in the 2002 standard.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Seine Bï¿½cher waren alle sehr nett, sie hatten auch sonst wenig zu tun. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
A little historical info on this subject.
An early member of the Cobol committee from a user
organization joined with the objective of seeing
Cobol contain the report writing features of their
IBM systems. If memory serves, he was the only person
in the group who understood his need from exposure to
the features. One of the problems of introducing
features is that 'I never heard of that' or we use
so and so.

It seems that the major problem is including features
that help a lot of people using a variety of methods.
This seems to slow down development for the lack of
a wider community of experience or needs. In my
opinion, this group, contains the largest I've
seen who have wide experience in several fields of
computer use. That's why I think together all of you
could contribute a great deal working at the needs
of a Business Programming Language extension.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T16:20:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410221520.525f32de@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0410130804.14a6551b@posting.google.com> <9J4xiUHPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0410181201.c705f4f@posting.google.com> <9JLTBIFeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 
 
>    So I'm not really familiar with this SCREEN SECTION, except that I  
> have seen it in manuals which I studied recently.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> part with an "USE ON (this-or-that-event) ".

Screen section does not use 'event handlers' the program 'falls off'
the ACCEPT after setting various statuses in CURSOR IS .. and CRT
STATUS IS .. fields.

You do an ACCEPT and then check the results.  This may indicate which
key terminated the accept and where the cursor was positioned.
 
>   Is there a place on the web where this effort can be studied?

Dunno, have you Googled for it ?
 
>   Well, my COBOL experiences are mainly mainframe-based, but I do not  
> think that REPORT WRITER is something mainframe-specific. It is, OTOH,  
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> phrase in MOVE, ADD, and SUBTRACT, or the COMPUTE statement. 

In my view it is 'underused' mainly because it is a programmer-centric
tool rather than being a user-centric tool.  Ever report or change has
to be coded, compiled, tested by programmers before the user gets to
see it.  It is a _programmer's_ solution to a user problem.

Why are you spending your time rewriting reporting programs ?

Why are the users having to wait weeks for a requested report to go
through the process of a system change (program writing) when they
want the report _now_ ?
```

```
take weeks. We don't use punched cards any more for programmings, do  
we?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-23T11:27:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410231027.35973fb4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0410130804.14a6551b@posting.google.com> <9JLTBIFeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0410221520.525f32de@posting.google.com> <9JPY2-PuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote

> r> You do an ACCEPT and then check the results.  This may indicate which
> r> key terminated the accept and where the cursor was positioned.
> 
>    That lacks quite some features then for using it on e.g. a Windows  
> workstation.

There is no requirement, even in a Windows type system, for there to
be separate event handlers for different events.  It is entirely
possible to have a single return point for all events as long as the
type and position of the event can be determined.

For example Flexus SP/2 'falls off the CALL' and has sufficient
information to determine where and how the event occurred.  The action
blocks are put into EVALUATE statements rather than separate event
handlers.

In any case the X-Open screen section was specific to terminals of the
day and predated Macintosh, GEM and Windows.
 
>   Using the REPORT WRITER, there is not much need to rewrite a  
> printing program, because its all in the DATA DIVISION. REPORT WRITER  
> is rather a program _generator,_ which needs some configuration.

  ... by the PROGRAMMER.

While the programmer may not be changing the procedure division he is
still changing source code and recompiling.  In many environments that
requires scheduling, retesting and release processes.
 
>   BTW, it is not limited to "reporting programs", and as I said, and  
> for "reporting programs", I would suggest an alternative:

>    I suggest to look up the data online and interactively in the  
> database, and not print so many reports.

Exactly. They don't want to wait for the programmer.
 
>    BTW, have you ever used REPORT WRITER?

No.  I evaluated using it, I understand entirely what it does, but I
had written my own dictionary based reporting and data extract
programs that were driven by text report specifications and could be
set up and used by users to get at their data.  If they didn't want to
do it them selves then I could dial into their machine and set up the
report in a few minutes.

The thing about RW is that it is a solution.  If it fits the problem
exactly then it is the best solution, if it doesn't quite fit then it
may be the worst. The problem that it best fits is: printing a report
with page breaks and control breaks from a primary file.

If the system has been designed with the use of RW as a target then RW
is the perfect fit for all manner of things.

However, I design systems around other needs and with other
requirements in mind   (such as the mechanisms that I have developed)
and RW just doesn't fit.

I can quite understand your enthusiasm for it and the frustration at
not convincing others that it is a really good thing, but it is of no
use to _me_ at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 8)_

```
The X/Open Screen Section support (and those implementations similar to it) 
pre-dates any "GUI" interfaces.  If you are interested in a GUI implementation 
of Accept/Display syntax in COBOL - look at what AcuCOBOL did for it. See:

http://www.acucorp.com/support/downloads/index.php

"GUI Features of ACUCOBOL-GT"
```
