###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-23T21:06:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2Jzed.7642$nl.4080@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0410221520.525f32de@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0410130804.14a6551b@posting.google.com> <9J4xiUHPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0410181201.c705f4f@posting.google.com> <9JLTBIFeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0410221520.525f32de@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

>l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 
> 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Lueko,

As Richard pointed out 'Windowing' events have no relationship to Screen 
Section ACCEPT/DISPLAY. If you want to get a handle then the first place 
should probably be Microsoft - check out articles and Knowledge Base.

However the following might help, (and I would think you can probably 
pick up on the same thing in Fujitsu, from their latest .Net version 
on-line manuals). Go to the previous on-line reference to Net Express V 
4.0 Manuals, (see below) - OO Programming and take a look at Chapter 19 
- GUIs and Events. It should give you a broad idea. - the code is of 
course Micro Focus specific, but then look at the Event diagrams later 
in the chapter.

In very broad terms Windows is circling, like a satellite, (regardless 
of how many applications you have loaded, COBOL, MS Word, Excel, Access, 
comp.lang.cobol,  etc.). Windows events are queued into a loop and 
Windows is checking to see which was the earliest event triggered, ( a 
mix of the applications above), from a keyboard, button or mouse click 
event.

Windows events are standard - but how your particular compiler reacts is 
dependent upon the tools used for coding GUIs. For example, Fujitsu - 
POWERFORM (?), Micro Focus - (a) Dialog System or (b) Dialog Editor and 
GUI classes - and for both, they have extensions for .Net. 
Liant(RM/COBOL) has their WOW and AcuCOBOL something different again. 
Then there are the tools you will see mentioned in this group, Norcomm's 
Screen IO and Flexus SP2.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/nx40indx.htm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T14:51:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clj3tv$h50$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0410130804.14a6551b@posting.google.com> <9J4xiUHPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0410181201.c705f4f@posting.google.com> <9JLTBIFeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
A competent CoBOL programmer can write a good CoBOL report program quickly and
easily with or without Report Writer.   Sure Report Writer is easier, but it's
not as if writing without it was hard or onerous.    So I see what people at a
site are familiar with, and use it or not depending upon their de facto
standards.
```

<a id="t-99a75ef21c"></a>
## Thread: Cobol/400 — 2 msgs

### Re: Cobol/400

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-15T20:24:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ML_bd.60249$VJ2.31791@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ckonth.p8.1@seniorenheim-reutin.de>`
- **References:** `<ckonth.p8.1@seniorenheim-reutin.de>`

```
mcintyre wrote:
> I'm new to Cobol/400 any I'm trying to get familiar... Coming from VMS,
> I don't understand the behavior of the as/400 with this example:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 04 10 15

I'm guessing you're moving something to W-DATE-2?  That's what's called 
a "group item."  It is implicitly alphanumeric, and by moving something 
to it, you'll replace what's in it, which will make the individual 
variables no longer have their values.

You have two options.  The first, which I prefer, is to move the three 
pieces individually.  Instead of

MOVE MY-DATE TO W-DATE-2

, do something like

MOVE MY-YEAR  TO W-YEAR
MOVE MY-MONTH TO W-MONTH
MOVE MY-DATE  TO W-DAY

, which will leave your slashes intact.  The second option, which is a 
bit more complex (and, some would argue, overly complex just to save two 
lines of code), is to make the sending and receiving group items have 
elementary items (those with a PICture clause) with the same name.  So, 
given

01  MY-DATE.
     05  W-YEAR       PIC 99.
     05  W-MONTH      PIC 99.
     05  W-DAY        PIC 99.

you could type

MOVE CORRESPONDING MY-DATE TO W-DATE-2

(CORRESPONDING can be abbreviated CORR).  The disadvantage to this is 
that now, any time you reference the elementary items, you have to 
qualify which one you're talking about using an OF clause, like...

IF MY-YEAR OF W-DATE-2 > 50

...or something like that.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol/400

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2004-10-20T20:46:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0410201946.84a9857@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ckonth.p8.1@seniorenheim-reutin.de> <ML_bd.60249$VJ2.31791@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
You can try this. Define your receiving field as:

W-DATE-2    PIC 99/99/99.
then
MOVE MY-DATE TO W-DATE-2

It works with MVS level Compilers; should work w/AS400/Cobol.
```

## Thread: COBSQL Oracle 9i and MF Cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### COBSQL Oracle 9i and MF Cobol

- **From:** "Mike Kinasz" <ng_spam_1@kinasz.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-15T22:59:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10n1hclotbipk1e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Anything I should watch out for using MF Net Express 3.1 (no can't use 4.0) 
with COBSQL to access Oracle 9i PL/SQL stored procedures?
Yesterday I found a tidbit saying you can't use 88 level switches that I 
hadn't seen before.....   (bummer).    Anybody else been down the COBSQL 
road that might be willing to throw some tips my way?

Thanks,
Mike K
```

#### ↳ Re: COBSQL Oracle 9i and MF Cobol

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-19T09:20:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cl2hok$m4$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<10n1hclotbipk1e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Mike.

You can use level 88 switches in your application, but not within a host
variable definition. That restriction is a Pro*COBOL limitation, rather than
Cobsql, per the Pro*COBOL Programmer's Guide :

--- start of text ---

Level Numbers

When declaring host variables, you can use level numbers 01 through 49, and
77.
Pro*COBOL does not allow variables containing the VARYING clause or
pseudo-type variables (these datatypes are prefixed with "SQL- ") to be
declared
level 49 or 77.

--- end of text ---

SimonT.
```

---

## Thread: OT: Virus infection WAS Re: Computer Statement Usage — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Virus infection WAS Re: Computer Statement Usage

- **From:** "Peter E.C Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-10-17T13:01:02+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1097971272.FH4/fN1TfrrIhxWCcQNMOQ@teranews>`
- **References:** `<IUY8d.8620$Pd2.7721@news02.roc.ny> <43rjm0d75jtgqmriq0tc4ffbrltsbunauv@4ax.com> <ckep5q$dt8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dl3mm011cq6ctjpl1micqvtfccmklqf8ah@4ax.com> <ckf6ce$ec3$1@panix5.panix.com> <1q4nm0lq7unpc0quhqp489tkefpt5tebmm@4ax.com>`

```
Robert,

I'm really sorry you have been infected and I know you will be doing
everything you can to remove this.

Please don't take offense at the following (comparatively simple)
suggestions; I am not trying to "teach my grandmother to suck eggs" and my
advice in no way reflects on your competency or ability.

(It's just that sometimes when we are under stress we miss what might
otherwise be obvious...)

1. Download and run hijackthis. I have found it extremely useful when there
are registry hooks involved. Inspection of the log from it may give you some
further clues.
http://www.tomcoyote.org/hjt/
2. Download AdAware, update it to the latest version, then run it. It is
free for personal use.
http://www.lavasoftusa.com/software/adaware/
3. I'm sure you have a very good virus protector, but has it been updated?
4. There is Trojan detector software available from: http://xoftspyware.com/
(I found this product outstanding. It found some registry entries that were
missed by AdAware, but AdAware found some stuff missed by Xoft...)

If all of the above reports "clear" and yet you know there is something
running somewhere, then it is possible it is a direct attack on you
personally, with a "tailored" virus or worm. All you can really do is keep
monitoring the ports and running processes. You might think about checking
for executable files that were created/modified around the time the symptoms
started appearing.

Hope you crack it soon.

Pete.

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:1q4nm0lq7unpc0quhqp489tkefpt5tebmm@4ax.com...

response
> >>>such as the one below would be stimulated to anything but contempt and
> >>>disgust toward its author.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Peter,

I've done all you suggested and more. It's not a custom worm; it's a
variant of RASWRAP. Others have asked about it. The only response they
received was run hijackthis, which they reported didn't work. 

Consumer mentality. Where's the challenge in that?

FWIW, A-squared (free) and SpywareDoctor ($) are better than most. I
second your praise of Xoftspy.

Thanks for being helpful,
Robert
```

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2004-10-17T04:32:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1097987504@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Microfocus COBOL on Unix — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus COBOL on Unix

- **From:** "Rapier" <Rapier503@mail.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-19T16:12:23+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1098198763.19163.0@nnrp-t71-02.news.clara.net>`

```
Hi,
     What are the differences between building programs with microfocus 
cobol on the PC and microfocus cobol on unix?

     Is the link and build options just the same is that all you have to do 
to build a runtime version on unix?
   Is the editor and animator the same?

   If anyone has any notes on microfocus Cobol unix please email them to me.

  If anyone knows any good website on microfocus cobol unix development 
please let me know.

thanks in advance
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL on Unix

- **From:** David Speight <dsp8@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-19T19:59:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FKadneJYGY_FOujcRVn-iQ@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1098198763.19163.0@nnrp-t71-02.news.clara.net>`
- **References:** `<1098198763.19163.0@nnrp-t71-02.news.clara.net>`

```
Rapier wrote:
> Hi,
>      What are the differences between building programs with microfocus 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

My only experience with the PC version was the old DOS, Windows 3.1, 
OS/2 version (under OS/2). The version was something like 3.5? The UNIX 
version is 4.0.05.

We used WB (Work Bench) and were using the mainframe emulation.
Under UNIX, we compile using shell script (although tbox could be used).
The animators are very similar. The PC had some flowcharting features 
that the UNIX version doesn't and is slightly more robust in general.
There is very little difference creating an *.int or *.gnt (using 
runtime environment).
The link build features (WB & tbox) are similar as well.
The standard at both shops was not to link and use the *.int.
The editors looks the same, but in the OS/2 shop (a few years back) used 
a PC version of ISPF as the editor.
The AIX shop uses an in house editor.

So IMHO, there is not much difference. At least with the versions I have 
used.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL on Unix

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-20T20:55:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0nhdn0p23run1hhnhq2q3n86vnoe4mdeu7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1098198763.19163.0@nnrp-t71-02.news.clara.net>`

```
On Tue, 19 Oct 2004 16:12:23 +0100, "Rapier" <Rapier503@mail.com>
wrote:

>     What are the differences between building programs with microfocus 
>cobol on the PC and microfocus cobol on unix?

Almost no difference.

You don't have to use Workbench. You can use your own editor, as I do,
and compile from a script or make file. Your editor can run on a PC ..
if you have a network connection and SAMBA or UltraEdit. Lacking
those, you can FTP source file back and forth, hopefully using a GUI
FTP. 

Source programs will compile and run on both platforms with zero
changes, unless you're doing something specific to the platform, such
as GUI. 

>     Is the link and build options just the same is that all you have to do 
>to build a runtime version on unix?

Runtimes are already built, on both platforms. Compiler options are
almost the same. You don't have to worry about linker options unless
you're doing something unusual, such as calling a third-party library.

Gnt and earlier .int are retained for backward compatibility. The
preferred way is compile the  main program to an executable, make all
called programs dynamic and compile them to .so on Unix or .dll on
Windows. 

>   Is the editor and animator the same?

I don't use the editor. Animator is the same.

>   If anyone has any notes on microfocus Cobol unix please email them to me.

The Unix manuals are at:

http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/sx40sp1/sx40indx.htm


>  If anyone knows any good website on microfocus cobol unix development 
>please let me know.

All you need is knowledge of Cobol in general and the above manuals.
Microfocus Cobol is not a different language, despite what employers
think; it is ANSI standard Cobol 85  plus some extensions that you
probably don't need, such as pointers, object oriented and
multi-threading. Demos of those have been posted in this newsgroup.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** rjones0@hotmail.com (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T10:38:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6dd8322.0410210938.1f9fbf6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <9JHJpfaPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Bottom posting

l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote in message news:<9JHJpfaPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>...
> .    On  21.10.04
>   wrote  stringer@no-spam.es.co.nz (stringer)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Belehrung findet man ï¿½fter in der Welt als Trost.  -G.C.Lichtenberg

We english have often changed old spellings and usages while our
american cousins retained the original and vice-versa.  The original
colonisations took place before spelling in England or elsewhere was
standardised, I don't think the terms rationalised or regularised
would be appropriate.  The language used to be much more phonetic, so
it didn't matter so much how words were spelt.  I suspect consistency
only became so normal when printed dictionaries became cheaply
available during the 19th century.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T08:51:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clj7en$jln$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <9JHJpfaPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6dd8322.0410210938.1f9fbf6@posting.google.com>`

```

"Robert Jones" <rjones0@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:6dd8322.0410210938.1f9fbf6@posting.google.com...

> The language used to be much more phonetic, so
> it didn't matter so much how words were spelt.  I suspect consistency
> only became so normal when printed dictionaries became cheaply
> available during the 19th century.

True, but I would contend the "Great English Vowel Shift" that occurred
between the fifteenth and eighteenth century was responsible for the vast
majority of the spelling anomalies of modern English.  Most of the spellings
were phonetic before that (although I grant that often there were two
different underlying orthographies -- Germanic and French).  The phoneticism
of the conventional spellings had pretty much disappeared long before
19th-century dictionaries.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T05:32:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>confused.

Is there any documentation to support this assertion?  According to 
http://dictionary.reference.com/search?q=programme&db=* this word is 
labelled 'Chiefly British variant of program' by the American Heritage 
Dictionary and the Merriam-Webster Medical Dictionary.  According to my 
copy of the Oxford English Dictionary (Vol II, p. 2319, p. 1438) both 
spellings are listed together ('Program, Programme') and the derivation 
notes state '... around the beginning of the 19th c, reintroduced from the 
F. programme, and now usually so spelt (though not pronounced as F.), the 
earlier program was retained by Scott, Carlyle, Hamilton and others and is 
preferable, as conforming to the usual English repr. of the Gr. 
-(gamma-rho-alpha-mu-mu-alpha), in anagram, cryptogram, diagram, telegram, 
etc.'

So... we have two modern American dictionaries calling the variant 
'Chiefly British' and the OED saying the variant is 'now usually so 
spelt'; a ready conclusion is that the person sending you the email got 
this directly backwards.

>
>Illiteracy is not a debate."

Poor scholarly habits are to be pitied, more than anything else... but, 
with diligence and education, it has been shown possible to educate a 
person out of them.

>
>Now I know its off topic, but I make no apologies, because I know how erudite 
>some of you are, and, not meaning to give offense, some of you have been here 
>since the year dot.

This is not a matter of erudition, this is a question of research and 
documentation.

>
>She is of course referring to usage in the Computer world, and not generally.

'(O)f course'?  If that's the case then the OED citing is irrelevant - not 
too many computers around back then - and neither of the American 
dictionaries cited list a computer reference, both points towards 
biology... but mention 'coded instructions'.

>
>Comments welcome. I'll check the list when I'm back from my holidays on 
>Tuesday morning, so thanks in advance.

A safe trip and a prosperous return, old boy.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

```
Having checked my cd version of the concise OED I am amazed to discover 
that it does not list program.

FWIW: common English (British) usage appears to be that Program refers 
to an entity consisting of written computer instructions whereas 
Programme refers to either pamphlets purchased at football (translation: 
soccer) matches or to two or more projects combined loosely. However, I 
have seen Programme used (badly) by people to refer to programs.

While we are at it; can we have disc back?

In message <cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>, stringer 
<stringer@no-spam.es.co.nz> writes
>I quote from an email just sent to me:
>"actually, american's use programme. Correct english is program.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T12:05:10+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2tqtlcF233b6hU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4GOdd.11465$Rf1.8115@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <fcpfn0ldh8id52krrorfbrqdgenk17k3q3@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:fcpfn0ldh8id52krrorfbrqdgenk17k3q3@4ax.com...
<snip>>
> If a person cannot muster minimal skill in English, is it reasonable
> to expect competent locution in a programming language? I don't think
> so.
>
Given my lifetime love of the English Language, I would have agreed with you
Robert.

Until a couple of weeks ago.

I came in contact with a man who is dyslexic and has great difficulty in
reading and writing. (So much so, that he has mail etc. dictated to him by
his computer and is able to "visualise" it aurally, much better than he can
visually.)

His spoken English is far from perfect, but adequate for communication.

The same man taught himself to program in Visual Basic and PHP. Over a
period of 15 years he painstakingly but together applications and websites
that are simply stunning. His competence as a programmer is beyond question,
yet he is technically "almost illiterate". I found from conversation with
him that he has a keen mind and can speak knowledgably on a wide variety of
subjects.

We need to be very careful before trying to establish a correlation between
linguistic/literary ability and programming ability.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T11:29:20-05:00

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> Having checked my cd version of the concise OED I am amazed to discover
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> have seen Programme used (badly) by people to refer to programs.

Speaking as a Canadian born in England, before I thought about it I
would have said that the correct >English< name for a collection of
computer instructions should be "programme", conforming to the usual
spelling standards.  However, as Alistair points out, the two words
refer to different things.  Therefore, the correct word is "program".
> 
> While we are at it; can we have disc back?

desk, desc.  risk, risc.  task, tasc.  mask, masc.   Disc is a circular
thing.  Damnation.  Disk = disc.  Go for it!

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T17:21:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4177E3E0.9B30E688@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

On 21-Oct-2004, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Speaking as a Canadian born in England, before I thought about it I
> would have said that the correct >English< name for a collection of
> computer instructions should be "programme", conforming to the usual
> spelling standards.

I don't recall ever coming across "programme" before this thread, although I
prefer the spelling "theatre" enough to add it to my spell checker.

> However, as Alistair points out, the two words
> refer to different things.  Therefore, the correct word is "program".

There are a couple of other words that have developed variants depending on
meaning.   Sometimes they are spelling, and other times they are almost word
changes.   An example of the latter is "antennas" which is acceptable for TV
aerials, but not for insect appendages.   I suspect "hanged" may be an earlier
case of this same thing.    (The relationship between "further" and "farther" is
coincidental).

I still get startled to see the word "blonde" referred to a male.   I will
probably lose that battle - but I would expect to lose the "e" rather thangain
it.

But I am gratified to see the word "fewer" making a comeback in some supermarket
queues.

I have the book _Eats, Shoots & Leaves_ and am gratified by its sales.

As a programmer, I dislike the English standard of putting the period of a
quoted sentence outside of the quote.   Maybe have it both places, but the
quoted sentence had a period.   Programmers know where the logic needs to be.

Phil Farmer has a novel where people return to earth in a randy future.  
Antlers are called horns then.   It seems odd, but a century ago, nobody would
refer to a bitch as a dog, anymore than we would refer to a cow as a bull.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T16:05:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4177E3E0.9B30E688@mb.sympatico.ca> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

In article <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> writes:
> 
> As a programmer, I dislike the English standard of putting the period of a
> quoted sentence outside of the quote.   Maybe have it both places, but the
> quoted sentence had a period.   Programmers know where the logic needs to be.

Conversely, the nonsensical practice of always putting periods and
commas inside adjacent closing quotation marks, regardless of where
they logically belong, is mandated by most US usage guides (that take
any position on the subject).

Thus we have, for example:

   Howard dislikes placing the period "outside of the quote."

required by most copy editors and the like.  Ridiculous.

I have read (but not verified) that this is an archaic holdover from
the days of movable lead type: small glyphs like periods and commas
were particularly susceptible to breakage when positioned next to
whitespace, so they were moved inside the quotation when possible.

Personally, I disagree with Howard, in the case where the end of
the quoted sentence is also the end of a sentence that incorporates
it, as in:

   As Howard wrote, "Programmers know where the logic needs to be".

There the period must terminate two scopes: that of the quoted sentence
and that of the enclosing sentence.  Unless you're going to employ two
periods - which, frankly, I think looks awful - the logical place to
do that is in the outer scope, with the inner scope implicitly closed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
In article <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com>,
Donald Tees  <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
>Michael Wojcik wrote:
>> In article <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> writes:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>A quotation of a sentence looks like "This is a sentence."?

About such things I was taught that... nothing will please everyone and 
all rules - including this one - have exceptions; the General Practise is 
that quotations of sentence-fragments show no punctuation inside the 
quotes while quotations of sentences do.  For example:

Jones taught that examples should be given 'clearly and unambiguously'.

Jones taught 'Examples should be given clearly and unambiguously.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T17:24:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com> <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

In article <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com writes:
> In article <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com>,
> Donald Tees  <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >A quotation of a sentence looks like "This is a sentence."?

The style guides, copyediting style sheets, and the like that I've
seen would have that as:

   A quotation of a sentence looks like "This is a sentence"?

That is, they reject punctuation on both sides of the closing
quotation mark, even when it would make logical sense, and instead
put it inside if it's a period or comma, and outside if it's
anything else.

I tend to find punctuation on both sides of the closing quotation
mark aesthetically unappealing; it's the movement of periods and
commas inside them regardless of what scope they belong to that
bugs me.  At times, though, there's no good alternative to putting
punctuation on both sides of the closing quote:

   Did he actually write, "I loathe style guides!"?

other than rephrasing the sentence - and rephrasing just to tidy
the punctuation seems a bit much.

> About such things I was taught that... nothing will please everyone and 
> all rules - including this one - have exceptions; the General Practise is 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jones taught 'Examples should be given clearly and unambiguously.'

Unfortunately, nearly all publication style guides used in the US
today, as far as I can tell, disagree on the former, and always would
put the period inside the closing quotation mark.  That's true even
for degenerate cases like:

   Outside the US the period is often known as a "full stop."

where it makes no sense at all (aside from that archaic holdover from
movable-lead typography).

And personally I disagree with the guideline illustrated by your
second example (though of course this is entirely a matter of
convention and opinion, so neither of us can really be said to be
"right").  As I noted in my previous post, it's my preference to put
the period (or other terminal punctuation) in the outermost scope it
applies to.  So if a period ends both a quoted sentence and the
sentence that quotes it, it goes outside the closing quotation mark.
In my preferred style, that is.
```

```
Did he say "This is a sentence?"?

Or did he say "This is a sentence."?

Jack said "Run!".

I replied "Run?".
```

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)

```
In article <cljgbk$34o$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> writes:
> 
> On 25-Oct-2004, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Occasionally this will be insufficient communication.

Well, it will occasionally be ambiguous, but ambiguity is ubiquitous
in natural language.  Whether it's insufficient depends on the
language situation (as they say in Pragmatics): does the ambiguity
lead to failure of communication?

Also, note that I'm not defending the style guides (a genre I am not
fond of), merely reporting them.  Though I do note later in my
previous message that I find punctuation - quotation mark - punctuation
aesthetically unappealing.

> Consider asking:
> 
> Did he say "This is a sentence?"?
> 
> Or did he say "This is a sentence."?

Yes, these are similar to my second example in my previous post.
```

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-10-26T17:06:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clm06q02k01@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com> <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com> <cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com> <cljhg8$jb3$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

In article <cljhg8$jb3$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com writes:
> In article <cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com>,
> Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> everyone.

Agreed, though I'd like to see them prove it :-).

I've often thought that if I were editing a journal, I'd provide my
copy editor(s) with a style guide, but instruct them that if an
author expressed preference for a different style to permit it.
Imposing some consistency is fine when the author has no strong
opinion, but style - even mechanics - has rhetorical force and
should ultimately be the province of the author.

(A related example: my wife just published an essay in _College
English_.  The original made substantial use of visual rhetoric -
typeface changes and the like.  CE insisted on removing a lot of
that, and complained no end about the rest.  She was sympathetic,
as she edits an academic journal herself, but really, would it
kill a *composition* journal to permit authors to write the way
they want?)

That's one of the reasons why I dislike style guides such as Strunk
and White; they tend to recommend all manner of rules that
unnecessarily constrain expression, without providing inexperienced
authors with the understanding of prose style they'd need to know
when to disregard them.  Richard Ohmann wrote a fine piece in
_College English_ back in 1979 arguing against S&W's "Use definite,
specific, concrete language", and it's just as relevant today.
Further, many style guides promulgate truly idiotic prohibitions,
like the ones against "split infinitives", or sentence fragments,
or beginning sentences with conjunctions or ending them with
prepositions.  These are bugbears created by people who thought
English should behave like Latin, or were simply small-minded.  S&W
isn't so bad in this regard, but I still believe it puts writers on
the wrong path.  And I say this as someone who taught composition
at the college level, if only for a couple of years, and had to
plow through the mangled prose of undergraduates on a regular basis.

On the other hand, books on style that don't try to be proscriptive
can be excellent material for authors.  Martin Amis' _The King's
English_, though idiosyncratic, is a good example; he's clear that
he's expressing his opinions about prose style, not delivering
verbatim the wisdom of the muses.[1]  Fowler, in _Modern English
Usage_, generally does a good job of justifying his opinions and
suggesting when they might not apply.


1. Actually, come to think of it, there isn't a muse of prose.  I
guess that falls in Clio's jurisdiction.  Aside from historiography,
I suppose there wasn't a lot of prose being written when the muses
were invented, unlike during, say, the Periclean period.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410251615.2990e9a5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com> <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com> <cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com>`

```
mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote 

>    Outside the US the period is often known as a "full stop."

Which should be correctly phrased as:

    In the USA a full stop is mistakenly called a "period".

Actually that's not correct either.  The quatotion marks (") are for
quoting what somebody said.  It should be in apostrophes as 'period'.



* A period in history is the time from one event until some other (for
example between the deaths of successive kings).  A period of music is
from the first note to the last note of a set of bars, including the
pause before the next period.  The period of a sentence is from its
first sylabyl until the ending pause indicated by the full stop
(indicating a _full_ pause (or stop), rather the short pause (or stop)
of a comma).

I am interested in why and when Americans started getting it wrong.
```

```
In article <217e491a.0410251615.2990e9a5@posting.google.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote 
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am interested in why and when Americans started getting it wrong.

Probably about the same time as about everyone else, give or take a hair.  
From my two-volume version of the Oxford English Dictionary, Vol II, p 
2134, p 699, col. 3:

11.b.  The point or character that marks the end of a complete sentence; a 
full stop (.).

1609 J. DAVIES Holy Roode (1878) 20/2 No Commaes but thy Stripes; no 
Periods but thy Nailes.  1612 BRINSLEY Lud. Lit. 95  In reading, that he 
[the scholar] doe it distinctly, reading to a Period or full point, and 
there to stay.

... and so forth.  It seems, Mr Plinston, that the OED cites references 
which might lead one to conclude that folks from Auld Blighty brought the 
use with them to the Newe Worlde... and I'll bet it does your heart just 
fine to have an American teach this to you.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-10-26T17:22:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clm13p02bbi@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com> <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com> <cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0410251615.2990e9a5@posting.google.com>`

```

In article <217e491a.0410251615.2990e9a5@posting.google.com>, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) writes:
> mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote 
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> quoting what somebody said.  It should be in apostrophes as 'period'.

Those aren't apostrophes; they're "inverted commas".  They just use
the same glyph in many typefaces.

Inverted commas aren't used in conventional US typography.  So-called
"single quotes", which are graphically the same, are, but not for the
purpose you suggest (distinguishing terminological quotation from
direct quotation).  In conventional US typography, "double quotes" are
used for all quotation purposes - direct quotation, referring to a
word or phrase as such, indicating figurative or questionable usage
("scare quotes") - except one: quotation within quotation.  There, US
usage bizarrely employs single quote marks for the embedded quotation:

   "Richard wrote 'that's not correct'", I wrote.

Presumably this convention developed in parallel to the UK one of
employing double quotes for embedded quotations (with the outer
quotation set off with inverted commas).

With more than two levels of quotation, US copy editors seem to lean
toward alternating double and single quotes.  It quickly becomes a
mess, but then quotations with embedded quotations with embedded
quotations is already a mess.

> [snip other definitions of "period"]
> 
> I am interested in why and when Americans started getting it wrong.

There is no "wrong", just as there is no "correct", because there is
no universal standard.  English is as it is used.

The OED shows the usage of "period" as "a full pause such as is
properly made at the end of a sentence" dates back to at least 1587,
and the important English rhetorician Puttenham has "period" as a
synonym for "full pause" in 1589.  It shows "period" as specifically
a name for that mark of punctuation as far back as 1609 (possibly
ambiguous, but a 1612 usage is not).

If US usage "got it wrong", we got it wrong from the UK.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-10-27T15:58:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clogj302bil@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com> <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com> <cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0410251615.2990e9a5@posting.google.com> <clm13p02bbi@news2.newsguy.com> <clm4go$naa$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

In article <clm4go$naa$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> writes:
> 
> On 26-Oct-2004, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The UK is barely 2 centuries old.

Make that "from what is now the UK", if you prefer.

> The Kingdom of Great Britain is about 4 centuries old.  (I'm an ignorant
> USAmerican who doesn't know exactly how old it is).

I think it officially dates to 1707 - see below.

> So we may have got it wrong from the Kingdom of England.   (Is that the
> correct title of 17th century England?)

In the 17th century, England and Scotland were both ruled by James I
(of England, VI of Scotland), from 1603 onward, though the two
countries weren't legally united until the Act of Union in 1707.
Nonetheless, James I/VI liked to call the whole shebang - England,
Scotland, and Wales - "Great Britain".  I don't know how popular that
usage was, though.

Wales, of course, had been conquered by the Kingdom of England long
ago, so even before James the Multivalent England and Wales were a
single political entity, more or less.

But at any event, you're certainly right that the usage in question
no doubt came from England, regardless of what state it was part of.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-27T15:46:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410271446.38705aa4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com> <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com> <cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0410251615.2990e9a5@posting.google.com> <clm13p02bbi@news2.newsguy.com> <clm4go$naa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clogj302bil@news4.newsguy.com>`

```
mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote

> Nonetheless, James I/VI liked to call the whole shebang - England,
> Scotland, and Wales - "Great Britain".  I don't know how popular that
> usage was, though.

'Great Britain' is the name of the largest of the islands that make up
the British Isles.  A reference to 'Great Britain' is to that main
island and does not include other islands such as Wight, Silly, and so
forth which may otherwise be considered part of England.

It seems that many think of 'Great Britain' as a concept akin to 'The
Greatness of the British Empire'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-27T16:19:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clpad6$1m75$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com> <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com> <cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0410251615.2990e9a5@posting.google.com> <clm13p02bbi@news2.newsguy.com> <clm4go$naa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clogj302bil@news4.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0410271446.38705aa4@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0410271446.38705aa4@posting.google.com...

> 'Great Britain' is the name of the largest of the islands that make up
> the British Isles.  A reference to 'Great Britain' is to that main
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Greatness of the British Empire'.

More, including some official bodies of the country, consider "Great
Britain" as a convenient abbreviation for the "United Kingdom of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland", the latter being considered somewhat
unwieldy.  This seems to be common enough that the international license
plate code for the "United Kingodom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland"
isn't "UKGBNI" or even "UK", it's "GB", into which decision the government
of the UK certainly had some input.

Note also that the "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland"
competes in the Olympic Games not as the full title or as "United Kingdom"
but as "Great Britain".

As much as you might like "Great Britain" not to have any official
synonymity with "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland", it
appears the latter organization disagrees with you ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
accord with Michael - 1601 - James I(England) aka James VI(Scotland) - 
King of England, Scotland and Wales. I think there was a reference to 
1707 - that I think ties in with the Hanoverian Georges - and I *think* 
that's the date for the 'Union Flag' (Union Jack) - that from initiation 
into Boy Scouts. (Anybody catch the two-part mini-series on Mary Queen 
of Scots and James her son - I already knew he was, but they 
concentrated on the fact that he was AC/DC and those wretched catholics 
and their Gunpowder Plot - pity they failed :-) ). 'Remember, remember 
the fifth of November' - Guy Fawkes Night is just around the corner.

 During Victoria's reign not sure - She was Empress of India and  the 
United Kingdom which of course included southern Ireland until it became 
the Eire republic in 192?.

Guess you're right. The first reference I hit when googling was the CIA 
! They gotta be right ! Perhaps I'll now check their site for 
WMDs.............. or perhaps Rummy's implementation of the Powell 
Doctrine, (having served two bitter tours in Viet Nam) - "A government 
using armed intervention must have a political plan, and a military 
objective which includes an EXIT PLAN".

Jimmy
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> inclined to think you are California dreamin'.

heh - really?

> Somebody has shoved a 
> stick into an hornets' nest (Islam), and regardless of who makes it to 
> the White House on Tuesday - I'm clear in my mind the 'Islamic' problem 
> will still be around if and when you personally make it to the age of 
> eighty !

The stick was not unprovoked - in fact, there were several provocations, 
dating back 7 years from 9/11/01.  True, Afghanistan and Iraq were (and 
are) not the only sponsors of terrorism - but, only force will show 
terrorists that we mean business.  They cannot be bargained with, 
because they cannot be trusted, and they have no regard for human life.

The war on terror isn't a war on Islam, but Islamic extremists.  I'm a 
Christian, and believe abortion is murder - but I also believe that Eric 
Rudolph went too far.  (He's the one arrested for fire-bombing an 
abortion clinic in Birmingham, AL.)

> I in no way dispute their right to exist as a separate nation - 
> but Israel is the hub of US Middle East foreign policy - just 
> complicates that 'Islamic' thing even more - to the point of being 
> unsolvable ?

Israel shouldn't complicate things at all.  (Yes, I know it does, and I 
understand the history - it doesn't mean that they shouldn't just "Get 
Over It" and move on...)  Organizations such as Hezbollah use terror 
because, for the most part, they can do so unchallenged.  If our country 
had responded to the Ku Klux Klan the same way most European countries 
want Israel to respond to these terrorists, there would be nothing but a 
pure white south.  Hatred is certainly a feeling that one is free to 
have - but when that hatred begins manifesting itself violently, it must 
be met by folks with an even greater resolve to maintain some sort of order.

> No you don't want colonies, but as Jerry Mouse pointed out, you sure 
> want to keep your sticky fingers on their oil rather than the French, 
> Germans or Russians ! (Another fancy name for a colony is Protectorate - 
> just like the Brits did with Egypt until they moved their military to 
> Cyprus in '55).

heh - I don't think we want their oil at all.  Anyone with a relatively 
open mind who's heard Bush's plan for exploring in ANWR (limited 
environmentally friendly drilling, that the Alaskan people want) knows 
that our goal is not to keep our hands in their oil, it's to make it so 
we don't need to.

> Care to stick your neck out and give reasonable dates for both your 
> points # 2 above ?

Sure (as a person, not a military representative)...

In Afghanistan, I'd expect to see maybe another 6 months at current 
strength, then a gradual draw-down.  (Like I said in the original mail - 
  this doesn't include targeted special ops missions to root out UBL...) 
  In Iraq, I'd expect to see another year or two of troops rotated in, 
to augment and train the Iraqi army, and put down the final stronghold 
cities like Fallujah, then a draw-down there as well.

Of course, things can always change - but, barring something unforeseen, 
that's my guess.  Is that reasonable?
```

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-28T00:48:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410272348.1a1a31ea@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com> <VRaed.58443$JG5.981212@news20.bellglobal.com> <clbfe6$5pa$1@panix5.panix.com> <cljcst0ge9@news3.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0410251615.2990e9a5@posting.google.com> <clm13p02bbi@news2.newsguy.com> <clm4go$naa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clogj302bil@news4.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0410271446.38705aa4@posting.google.com> <clpad6$1m75$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

> > 'Great Britain' is the name of the largest of the islands ...

> More, including some official bodies of the country, consider "Great
> Britain" as a convenient abbreviation for the "United Kingdom of Great
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the UK certainly had some input.

> Note also that the "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland"
> competes in the Olympic Games not as the full title or as "United Kingdom"
> but as "Great Britain".

"""It should be noted that UK Athletics nominates athletes for
selection by the British Olympic Association. The BOA has the
exclusive power for the representation of the Great Britain and
Northern Ireland Olympic Team at the Olympic Games."""

Even more interesting is:

"""Tuesday, January 27 2004

The final draft of the British Olympic Association's Team Members
Agreement for this summer's Athens Games contains several references
to the 'Great Britain and Northern  Ireland Olympics Team'.

The references to the 'Northern Ireland Olympic Team' were not in the
equivalent document for the Sydney 2000 games.

The Olympic Council of Ireland maintain that they are the IOC
recognised body for the whole island of Ireland. The OCI has sought
explanation from the British Olympic Association by telephone but have
not had a response.

OCI Secretary Dermot Sherlock has now written to his BOA counterpart
Simon Clegg seeking clarification."""

The British Olympic Association seem to think that name of the team
includes Northern Ireland.

 
> As much as you might like "Great Britain" not to have any official
> synonymity with "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland", it
> appears the latter organization disagrees with you ...

""" ---------------
Pliny the Elder writing around AD 70 uses a Latin version of the same
terminology in section 4.102 of his Naturalis Historia. He writes of
Great Britain: Albion ipsi nomen fuit, cum Britanniae vocarentur omnes
de quibus mox paulo dicemus. (Albion was its own name, when all [the
islands] were called the Britannias; I will speak of them in a
moment). In the following section, 4.103, Pliny enumerates the islands
he considers to make up the Britannias, listing Great Britain,
Ireland, and many smaller islands.
-------------- """"

So the term 'Great Britaim' has been the name of the island since at
least 70 AD.  Of course they used the name of the island when they
united all the Kingdoms of that island into one in 1705.

Often when one country occupies a whole island (eg Australia) the name
of the island and that of the country are the same.  In the case of
the UK the name of the island is different and so either name can be
used to refer to the place, though technically UK includes Northern
Ireland, Wight and various orthers and GB does not.
```

```
mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote 

> Yes, *I* know that (and the last time I checked, "England, Scotland,
> and Wales" pretty much composed the big island).  Tell James; he's
> the one who liked to refer to his kingdom as "Great Britain"

He wasn't wrong, just like NZ is the islands and the country is
actually "The Dominion of New Zealand" and the country on the island
continent Australia is "Commonwealth of Australia".

But most just use the name of the island(s).

> (according to various sources). Perhaps he didn't care for Wight et
> alia.  (Hmm... 

'Australia' ignores Tasmania too.

> I wonder what the political standings of the various
> lesser isles were during James I's reign?  I believe that even today
> the Isle of Man has its own legislature, though it's part of the UK,

"""The Island is a unique self-governing kingdom - a Crown dependency
which belongs to neither the UK nor the European Union. It has its own
parliament (called Tynwald), laws, traditions, culture, cuisine and
postage stamps."""

> and I have some vague and suspect recollection that some of the other
> smaller islands have complicated political histories.)

"""The Channel Islands are not part of the United Kingdom, except for
defense and foreign affairs. They are dependencies of the Crown and
ruled by the Queen of Great Britain and Northern Ireland directly
(note however that Sark is held by J. M. Beaumont, 22nd lord of Sark).
They do not send representatives to Parliament in London, but have
their own Parliaments in Jersey, Guernsey and Sark. The islands are
the only remain of the duchy of Normandy still in the possession of
the English crown."""
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-29T16:30:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2uful0F2a1e9lU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4177E3E0.9B30E688@mb.sympatico.ca> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clbb4e0glv@news1.newsguy.com>`

```
I agree with this message!

I "learned" to do it inside the quote, but I also refuse to do it that way
because having it outside the quote makes much more sense.  This is
especially true for other punctuation marks.  For example:

Did hey say "I hate you"?
vs,
He asked "What's your name?".


>>> Michael Wojcik<mwojcik@newsguy.com> 10/22/2004 10:05:34 AM >>>

In article <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, "Howard Brazee"
<howard@brazee.net> writes:
> 
> As a programmer, I dislike the English standard of putting the period of
a
> quoted sentence outside of the quote.   Maybe have it both places, but
the
> quoted sentence had a period.   Programmers know where the logic needs to
be.

Conversely, the nonsensical practice of always putting periods and
commas inside adjacent closing quotation marks, regardless of where
they logically belong, is mandated by most US usage guides (that take
any position on the subject).

Thus we have, for example:

   Howard dislikes placing the period "outside of the quote."

required by most copy editors and the like.  Ridiculous.

I have read (but not verified) that this is an archaic holdover from
the days of movable lead type: small glyphs like periods and commas
were particularly susceptible to breakage when positioned next to
whitespace, so they were moved inside the quotation when possible.

Personally, I disagree with Howard, in the case where the end of
the quoted sentence is also the end of a sentence that incorporates
it, as in:

   As Howard wrote, "Programmers know where the logic needs to be".

There the period must terminate two scopes: that of the quoted sentence
and that of the enclosing sentence.  Unless you're going to employ two
periods - which, frankly, I think looks awful - the logical place to
do that is in the outer scope, with the inner scope implicitly closed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T09:13:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clj8mf$kh5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4177E3E0.9B30E688@mb.sympatico.ca> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> There are a couple of other words that have developed variants depending
on
> meaning. ... I suspect "hanged" may be an earlier
> case of this same thing.

How recently the semantic distinction between the two variants of the verb
occurred, I don't know, but I'd suggest that using the *irregular*
alternative for certain grammatical constructions involving a male object
(or subject in passive or reflexive constructions) might elicit some
snickers (suppressed or otherwise).   In polite company the *regular* "-ed"
form of the simple past and the participial might be regarded as preferable,
and legitimate, in this context.  "Hanged" is a valid alternative to "hung"
in the 1981 Merriam-Webster Eighth Collegiate.

To me "He got himself hung" means something entirely different from "He got
himself hanged", and I think most speakers of American English would
understand the difference.   I'd have to say "hanged" would be preferred in
this context to describe a form of execution; the first meaning of the
alternative in this context would, I believe be understood by most to refer
to plastic surgery.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

> 
> To me "He got himself hung" means something entirely different from "He got
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

If you're right, then there is developing/has developed yet another
divergence, that being between "American" and "Canadian" English
(regional variants notwithstanding).  Because I can think of many uses
of the word "hung" which mean "suspended", even if colloquially so:
"hung out to dry", "all hung up", "pheasants are hung before they are
cooked",  etc. etc.  I won't say that "hung plus male" in the same
sentence is NOT taken to refer to anatomy, but I will say that many
other possibilities come to mind.  Personally, your example sentence
would mean "he got his neck stretched".  FWIW


PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T12:29:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cljk6a$s33$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4177E3E0.9B30E688@mb.sympatico.ca> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clj8mf$kh5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <417D2E98.99CC88CC@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:417D2E98.99CC88CC@mb.sympatico.ca...

? I won't say that "hung plus male" in the same
> sentence is NOT taken to refer to anatomy, but I will say that many
> other possibilities come to mind.  Personally, your example sentence
> would mean "he got his neck stretched".  FWIW

Well, the point I'm trying to make is that there is a subset of the possible
sentence constructions that involve simple past and past participles that
form an ambiguous reference, one of which is "execution by hanging" and the
other is represented by surgery or other action for which your "he got his
neck stretched" could be termed a euphemism ... "Hanged" is safer in those
contexts, and acceptable in American dictionaries.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T20:01:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cljm2f$6as$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4177E3E0.9B30E688@mb.sympatico.ca> <cl8r7j$bc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <clj8mf$kh5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <417D2E98.99CC88CC@mb.sympatico.ca> <cljk6a$s33$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
A couple of decades ago, I read an article written by Poul Anderson, who wrote
mostly Science Fiction, but some fantasy.   This article was about changing
language and in particular what the coming out of gay culture did to his tool,
the English language.

He would spell faerie in this archaic to avoid confusion.   He missed the words
"gay" and "queer", that he can't use for the same effects.    He mentioned how a
British author hoping for an American audience had better not talk about putting
a faggot to his lips, without knowing his audience.

Of course we have the same changes with words such as "database", and "object".


When Isaac Asimov re-released his Foundation Trilogy, he was tempted to improve
on the prose, but decided not to.   One thing he did change was the word
"atomic" to "nuclear", to reflect the change in our language since the time he
wrote those books.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:

> Having checked my cd version of the concise OED I am amazed to 
> discover that it does not list program.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> While we are at it; can we have disc back?

I checked my OED Fifth Edition - 1963. Didn't surprise me not to find 
'program'. There was a reference to the Greek 'programma' - presumably 
that passes to French giving us our 'English' programme. Back in '63 
ADP/EDP was the new game in town so we adapted to the new American 
'program' when referring to computers - the first line in COBOL is 
PROGRAM-ID. Even now I still refer to a theatre 'programme'.

Your 'disc' - possibly relating back to discus. But again, IBM 
introduced the first computer 'disks'. ("Thanks very much" said ICL when 
the first prototypes failed in UK and people bought ICT/ICL gear instead 
- that's how I got involved with RCA's Magnetic Card File!).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T19:47:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YKcfd.22403$%k.20146@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<clj8t9$ki6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4K_dd.176758$a41.43866@pd7tw2no> <clj8t9$ki6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
>news:4K_dd.176758$a41.43866@pd7tw2no...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
CRAM was NCR. Saw one at Joe Lyons, the UK bakery and 'quick meal' Lyons 
Corner Houses (they initiated the LEO computer - there's a web site 
which gives the history of Leo). From memory the CRAM storage was 
hanging in the air, cards selected and dropped down - then I get real vague.

RCA's MCF - details very vague but approximate. (I've tried searching on 
RCA for details previously - but no luck). Originally for USN for 
artillery range finding. Stood on a workbench about 8 x 4 feet. Covered 
in a plastic smoke grey cover. At the end a read/write head, (about size 
of a large commercial can of coffee).

Magnetic Card - roughly 12 x 5 inches - 166,400 bytes (I think)

Record blocks specified as 'buckets' of 128, 256, 512 or 1024 depending 
opon your record sizes.  For 'ISAMs' the index was stored on either same 
card or an alternate card. Given Primekeys 1,2,3,,5,6, 7 etc... the 
index records were stored ascending. If you added PrimeKey # 4 either 
'insert' shifting the remainder to the right or a 'Tag' was placed in 
position # 4 indicating the index record for # 4 was held in another bucket.

256 cards to a Magazine, with a total of 8 magazines.

Access a card by program, card was extracted and using suction was  
sucked to the right into a tramway guiding it to the Read/Write head 
where it did a figure-of-eight before being returned down the  left 
tramway back into its magazine.  Real fast - if you were trying to 
follow it with your eyes !

Software wise worked great - but overall failed miserably mechanically. 
Just imagine bits of plastic being sucked at high-speed through a 
tramway. At some stage you finished up with scrunched cards. 
Necessitated a lot of backups and rebuilding.

We were first users in UK. Subsequently a Midlands Gas or Electricity 
Board, (wouldn't mind betting it was the same outfit Pete Dashwood last 
worked for in UK), took up the challenge - they bought eight units but 
part of the deal was they had to do their own technical support. They 
had it going for some years on a large customer database.

Once the initial kinks were ironed out of the IBM disks the NCR and RCA 
devices became dinosaurs.

Jimmy
```

```
Ah.  Right you are -- it was the NCR device that I was thinking of.

My first job out of college (1967) was a computer operator at a site that
made extensive use of the IBM 2321, which could be regarded as having
somewhat similar technology (and reliability).  We had one on our 360/40 and
two on our 360/65; around the time we got an additional 360/50 we replaced
the 2321's with two 2314 banks each (shortly before I left in 1969).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-25T19:57:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410251857.3a58b80b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz> <8zh+G0Cj56dBFwTW@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <4K_dd.176758$a41.43866@pd7tw2no> <clj8t9$ki6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <YKcfd.22403$%k.20146@pd7tw2no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote

> CRAM was NCR. Saw one at Joe Lyons, the UK bakery and 'quick meal' Lyons 
> Corner Houses (they initiated the LEO computer - there's a web site 
> which gives the history of Leo).

Just last week I purchased 'A Computer Called LEO' by Georgina Ferry.

> Record blocks specified as 'buckets' of 128, 256, 512 or 1024 depending 
> opon your record sizes.  For 'ISAMs' the index was stored on either same 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> position # 4 indicating the index record for # 4 was held in another bucket.

That seems to be the same as Disk DA Housekeeping and probably has the
same perils of 2nd level overflow!  If there was no room for a record
in the required  bucket then it would put a tag in the bucket and the
record into 1st level overflow, if there was no room for a tag in the
bucket then it would also move a record from the bucket to 1st level
and would have two tags in the bucket.

The program would make up the record in the record buffer and issue a
WRITE.  Record into 1st level, tag to bucket - no room.  Move a record
from bucket to record buffer in program write first tag, go to 1st
level to store the displaced record - no room, try 2nd level overflow
- none or full - return to program with error code indicating that the
write failed.

But the record was written, it is in the file, what failed was a
completely different record which may have been in the files for ages
but is now no longer there and is in the program's record buffer.

Wew learnt that the program had to deal with that by storing the
record away somewhere and merging it back into the file after
reorganizing (or during).
```

#### ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T20:49:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cl97cb0jrr@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`

```

In article <cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>, stringer@no-spam.es.co.nz (stringer) writes:
> I quote from an email just sent to me:
> "actually, american's use programme. Correct english is program.

If this quotation is verbatim, I suggest you can safely ignore it.
English usage advice from someone who fails to capitalize proper nouns
and uses an apostrophe to form a non-possessive plural isn't worth
what you paid for it.

> No, I'm not debating it, it's fact.

Ditto anyone who claims that any usage is "correct english", and then
claims that is fact (rather than opinion).  There is no such thing as
objectively correct English, because there is no standard by which it
could be measured.  We real English-usage pedants know that.

Various people have already pointed out that "program" is the common
US usage, and "programme" is chiefly British, so the ostensible
propositional content of the message was wrong anyway.

If you've quoted that email correctly, I'm afraid its author is
operating under some powerful misapprehensions.  Perhaps delusions.
```

```
The word "program" is derived from the Greek "PROGRAMMA"
(has double M), so the american term is more correct, I think.

news:cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz...

you're

>

>
> Now I know its off topic, but I make no apologies, because I know how
erudite
> some of you are, and, not meaning to give offense, some of you have been
here
> since the year dot.
>
> She is of course referring to usage in the Computer world, and not
generally.

> Comments welcome. I'll check the list when I'm back from my holidays on
> Tuesday morning, so thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT English Language, Program or Programme

- **From:** "Vince Coen" <VBCoenDespawn@btconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T13:00:52
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1098446452@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn>`
- **References:** `<cl7e2t$5am$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`

```
Hello stringer!

21 Oct 04 04:31, stringer wrote to All:

 s> I quote from an email just sent to me:
 s> "actually, american's use programme. Correct english is program.

In the 60's and onward America uses program for a collection of computer 
instructions forming a functional block of code et al.

At the same period some people ( but mostly non IT/DP in the UK) used 
programme.

For most if not all IT staff use 'program'.

When seen as 'programme' it usually indicates that the writer is not a IT 
person.



Vince
In IT etc since 1963.
```

```
Hello All,

Cobol under Unix onto Laser printer? These JPG's are basically 2D bar

```

#### ↳ Re: JPG images printing in MF COBOL

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-21T23:19:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410212219.1bd77f94@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5d69f5038909f7d3ae979c50a79c20fc@localhost.talkaboutprogramming.com>`

```
"Sweatwater1sm6" <sweatwater@gmail.com> wrote

> Does anybody has the experience of printing JPG images thru Microfocus
> Cobol under Unix onto Laser printer? These JPG's are basically 2D bar
> codes.

No. But I do print barcodes on basic text pages (using templates) by
sending PCL codes for printing the required bars of the bar code. 
Basically it is just a series of black or white rectangles being
drawn.  The code I use can do barcodes up to 4 inches high so that can
be read at 20 feet by hand held RF guns.

I also print barcodes to matrix printers using bit mapped graphics or
use the bar code facilitity where it exists.
```

## Thread: Cobol set for AIX - secuential files - DDMEA — 3 msgs

### Cobol set for AIX - secuential files - DDMEA

- **From:** Daniel Vidal <vidal_dan@gva.es>
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T11:22:23+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4178D14F.A72AC01@gva.es>`

```
Hi all

    I have a problem with Cobol and secuential files when migrating from
MVS to AIX.

    I have many batch processes that do 3 steps:

        1 -  extract data from database and write secuential file
        2 -  sort the secuential file (external tool like syncsort)
        3 -  generate report

    Secuential files in Cobol set for Aix have two real files one like
filename.txt and other hidden file named .filename.ddmea .

    If the sort not have 'INCLUDE' or 'OMIT' options i can copy the
original  .ddmea file with the name of sorted file and all work fine
but.... I have some sort processes with 'INCLUDE' option set...

    Someone know any utilitie for generate .ddmea file from secuential
file?
    Someone know the .ddmea file specification?

NOTES: I can't use 'line secuential' files... i have packed data.
              The migrated application is very very old and big!!!

T.I.A.

Salud y Suerte!!!!!!!!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol set for AIX - secuential files - DDMEA

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T16:05:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pi8in0l7ecqbuuuk7ud07hgpuuf5v2ta0b@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4178D14F.A72AC01@gva.es>`

```
On Fri, 22 Oct 2004 11:22:23 +0200, Daniel Vidal <vidal_dan@gva.es>
wrote:

>    I have a problem with Cobol and secuential files when migrating from
>MVS to AIX.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>              The migrated application is very very old and big!!!

Pipes are the preferred way to pass data between programs under Unix.
Disk files are for transport through time, not space. Change program 1
to write to STDOUT and program 3 to read from STDIN. Yes, you CAN send
binary data through a pipe. 

Execute the triad this way:

prog1 | syncsort | prog3

As a last resort, you could unpack the packed fields and use a
standardized file format: ASCII.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol set for AIX - secuential files - DDMEA

- **From:** pottmi@gmail.com (michael potter)
- **Date:** 2004-10-27T21:30:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2379dacc.0410272030.28d79b3f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4178D14F.A72AC01@gva.es>`

```
Daniel Vidal <vidal_dan@gva.es> wrote in message news:<4178D14F.A72AC01@gva.es>...
> Hi all
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Salud y Suerte!!!!!!!!!!


Dan,

I had a similar problem.  I suggest that you convert to the
other indexed file system supported by aix cobol 2.0.  the
name slips my mind, but you should be able to find it in
the programmers reference: 
http://www-306.ibm.com/software/awdtools/cobol/aix/library/

this alternate indexed file system does not use the annoying
.ddmea files.  THere are two ways to tell aix cobol to
use the alternate file system: set an environment variable,
or prefix the name of the file in the program.

I dont think this information specifically addresses your
problem, but at least you will not have to deal with .ddmea.
```

## Thread: [OT] 'Obvious' is in the Mind of the Beholder — 21 this month / 22 total

### [OT] 'Obvious' is in the Mind of the Beholder

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T10:49:03-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clb6kv$ei9$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

As some might have noticed... when words like 'obvious' or 'clarity' get 
bandied about my Standard Response is 'Obvious is in the mind of the 
beholder'; a few minutes back the Muse descended upon me and provided me 
with the following example:

F + 1 = 10

Clearly obvious... and with obvious clarity, neh?

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] 'Obvious' is in the Mind of the Beholder

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T11:48:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10nib1laj3pvje0@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<clb6kv$ei9$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:clb6kv$ei9$1@panix5.panix.com...
>
> As some might have noticed... when words like 'obvious' or 'clarity' get
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Clearly obvious... and with obvious clarity, neh?

Absolutely, F = Lexington; because the bus stops are:
..., Main, Lexington, 10th, 14th, Marshall, ...; therefore,
Main + 1 = Lexington, Lexington + 1 = 10, 10 + 1 = 14,
and 14 + 1 = Marshall.

[I read something similar in a book; but do not remember
the title nor the author.]
```

```
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>the title nor the author.]

That brings to my mind the test of 'extend the number sequence' where the 
numbers used are the stops of one of the Manhattan subway lines, e.g, 14, 
18, 23, 28, 34, 42, 50, 59...

(My memory is, admittedly, porous but I recall that being the route of the 
#1 line up the West side; the next number in the sequence is, I believe, 
66).

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Obvious' is in the Mind of the Beholder

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-22T13:24:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10nigne9qkpdi23@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<clb6kv$ei9$1@panix5.panix.com> <10nib1laj3pvje0@corp.supernews.com> <clbbp2$pk3$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:clbbp2$pk3$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <10nib1laj3pvje0@corp.supernews.com>,
> Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
> >
> ><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message
news:clb6kv$ei9$1@panix5.panix.com...
> >>
> >> As some might have noticed... when words like 'obvious' or 'clarity'
get
> >> bandied about my Standard Response is 'Obvious is in the mind of the
> >> beholder'; a few minutes back the Muse descended upon me and provided
me
> >> with the following example:
> >>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 66).

What a wonderful thing memory and curiosity are.

Under the heading "World View Constraint," Rubinstein writes:
"Churchman [7] relates the story of the mathematics professor
who was asked by his students to give the next number in the
sequence 32, 38, 44, 48, 56, 60. He was told that the properties
of the sequence were well known to him and that the solution
is simple. The professor came up with a complicated polynomial
after much effort, and gave up when he could not generate a
simpler solution. The answer was 'Meadowlark,' the elevated
stop after 60th street in the city subway. The professor rode the
subway daily--and got off at Meadowlark."

Rubinstein, Moshe F., "Patterns of Problem Solving," 1975.

[7] Churchman, C. West, "The Role of Weltanshauung in
Problem Solving and Inquiry," in "Theoretical Approaches to
Non-Numerical Problem Solving," 1970.

No doubt this story has been adapted often; it is both amusing
and educational.
```
